title: Research on an Automatic Detection Device for Impurities in Transformer Oil
authors: Hainan Su, Bangyan Wang, Yucong Sun, Qingwei Zeng, Xianfeng Liu, Qiang Hao, Fenghang Chen, Tian Xin
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: The insulation and cooling performance of transformer oil directly affects the service life of power transformers. Mechanical impurities and metal particles in the oil will accelerate insulation deterioration. Traditional offline detection has strong lag, and manual adsorption devices are inefficient and cannot respond in real time. To address this issue, this paper designs an integrated automatic detection and adsorption device: with automatic detection as the core, an adsorption module is constructed in combination with activated carbon nano-adsorbents. The performance of the device is verified through detection, judgment, adsorption, and feedback. A transformer oil impurity simulation experiment platform is established to test the detection accuracy and adsorption efficiency under different impurity concentrations and flow rates.

title: Optimal Operation of Wind Power Integrated Systems Considering Demand Response
authors: Wenxia Liu, Guanzhen Wang, Suhua Lou, Jierui Yang, Xinrui Xiong, Tao Xiong
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: In the face of energy crises and environmental deterioration, wind power has become an important solution to these problems. However, wind power has characteristics such as volatility, which poses difficulties for the scheduling of systems containing wind power. To address this, demand-side response, as a flexible resource that responds quickly and has high economic benefits, offers an alternative pathway for enhancing wind power utilization while reducing the system's operating costs. Accordingly, this study develops a day-ahead time-of-use electricity pricing PDR model grounded in the elasticity theory of electricity demand, as well as an IDR model considering capacity compensation and electricity compensation. Furthermore, this paper takes into account the costs of thermal power, IDR costs, carbon emission costs, load shedding costs, and related constraints, and establishes an optimization operation model for a system containing wind power considering demand-side response. Finally, through a 10-machine system example, a series of comparative analyses are conducted to illustrate the utility of different types of demand response for the operation of systems with wind power.

title: A Strategy-Proof Mechanism for Fair Allocation of Multiple Resources with Access Constraints in Mobile Edge Computing
authors: Jie Li, Xingyu Liu, Wenbo Wang, Xiaodong Fu
abstract: Mobile edge computing (MEC) transforms the network edge into an intelligent computing platform by deploying heterogeneous servers near end users. However, allocating resources to diverse users in MEC faces several challenges: wireless access may be limited by access point range, user demands often mismatch heterogeneous server configurations, and both computing and communication resources are constrained. Fair and efficient multi-resource allocation can promote sharing while utilizing idle capacity for high-workload users. To address this, we propose LMM-TSF, a Lexicographically Max-Min Task Share Faireness allocation mechanism that accounts for server access and external wireless resource constraints. Theoretically, LMM-TSF satisfies several desirable properties including envy-freeness, Pareto optimality, sharing incentives, and strategy-proofness. Large-scale simulations using Alibaba cluster traces show that LMM-TSF outperforms state-of-the-art fair allocation mechanisms in resource utilization and upholds the sharing incentive property.

title: A Programmable Nanosecond High-Voltage Pulse Generator Based on MOSFET Transistors
authors: Zhidong Zhu, Wenfang Liu, Feng Shao, Zhenyu Jin, Xiaomin Liu
abstract: High-voltage nanosecond pulse generation has traditionally relied on avalanche transistors, yet these devices are inherently limited by stochastic breakdown mechanisms and thermal instability. To overcome these physical constraints, this work implements a deterministic active gate-drive topology using SiC MOSFETs. By forcing the devices to switch via active gate control rather than passive avalanche breakdown, we fundamentally alter the energy dissipation profile, achieving a stable 1.4 ns rise time with <100 ps jitter while eliminating thermal drift. Furthermore, we address the spectral challenges of nanosecond edges by modeling the high-voltage PCB traces as wideband microstrip transmission lines. This spectral-domain approach mitigates signal degradation caused by the skin effect and dielectric losses, which are often overlooked in standard designs. Additionally, we implement a rigorous bandwidth (BW) verification criterion to ensure measurement accuracy. The system demonstrates a continuously tunable output (200 V–3 kV) and drives capacitive loads up to 100 pF without waveform collapse, proving its viability for precision industrial applications.

title: HRVFusion: Video-based Long-Term Heart Rate Variability Measurement with Conditional Diffusion Models
authors: Xing Yao, Rencheng Song, Juan Cheng, Chang Li, Xun Chen
abstract: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) has shown great potential for heart rate variability (HRV) analysis due to its non-contact and convenient nature. However, HRV measurement relies on long-term and high-quality blood volume pulse (BVP) signals, which pose substantial challenges for existing rPPG techniques. To address this, we propose HRVFusion, a conditional diffusion model-based framework for HRV estimation from rPPG signals. In the forward diffusion process, raw chrominance (CHROM) signals extracted from a video are incorporated to simulate the complex noise disturbances typically observed in rPPG signals. During the reverse process, to enhance the model's sensitivity to HRVs, the high-precision BVP reconstruction is jointly guided using both the chrominance signal and its time-frequency ridge extracted via the wavelet synchrosqueezed transform (WSST). Furthermore, a convolution and mamba hybrid network is introduced to handle inputs of arbitrary length, meeting the requirements for long-term HRV analysis. A public dataset for long-term video-based HRV extraction, consisting of 15-minute recordings from 18 participants, is presented for the first time. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance across multiple HRV metrics, with a mean absolute error (MAE) of 5-8 ms for SDNN, RMSSD below 18 ms, and Pearson correlation coefficients above 0.85 for frequency-domain indices (LF, HF, LF/HF), outperforming existing approaches. This study provides an accurate and robust non-contact solution for long-term video-based HRV measurement.

title: Complex-Valued Transformer with Improved Positional Embedding for MIMO-OFDM Channel Denoising
authors: Huu Binh Minh Tran, Kengo Ando, Chandan Pradhan, Hiroki Iimori, Szabolcs Malomsoky
abstract: Channel denoising plays a critical role in enabling accurate channel estimation for modern multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO)–orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) systems. As antenna counts and frequency bands proliferate, channel impulse responses become increasingly complex, challenging conventional denoising methods. Motivated by the success of data-driven techniques, we introduce a novel, fully complex-valued transformer architecture tailored for beam–delay domain channel denoising. Key innovations include an inverse exponential positional embedding that avoids corrupting dominant delay taps and an encoder-only design that streamlines one-to-one mapping from noisy to clean channel matrices. The network is trained in a supervised fashion to minimize mean square error (MSE) loss function. Simulation results using 3GPP Urban Micro channel model at 3.5 GHz carrier frequency demonstrate that the proposed framework reduces mean estimation error compared to the legacy threshold-filter method and recent state-of-the-art machine learning (ML)-based denoisers, by a significant margin at 44% and 33%, respectively.

title: Simulation and Regulation Method of Water Resource Trading Behavior in Ecosystems Using Evolutionary Game Algorithm
authors: Yuhan Zhang
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 International Conference on Information Economy, Data Modeling and Cloud Computing
abstract: None

title: Research on Lithium Battery Soh Estimation Based on Icoa-Lstm*
authors: Fangting Zheng, Mei Zhang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: To enhance the accuracy of State of Health (SOH) estimation during the nonlinear degradation process of lithium batteries, this paper proposes a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model optimized by an Improved Crayfish Optimization Algorithm (ICOA). By incorporating the ICOA algorithm, the model effectively addresses the hyperparameter sensitivity issues inherent in traditional LSTM. Key health features strongly correlated with capacity degradation-such as initial charging voltage and average discharge voltage-are selected as inputs for the ICOA-LSTM model to achieve precise SOH estimation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed ICOA-LSTM model exhibits high predictive accuracy and engineering applicability, significantly outperforming the standard LSTM model.

title: Steady-State Aware Hierarchical DRL Framework for Dynamic Task Offloading in Vehicular Edge Computing
authors: Jinyang Zhang, Sai Zou, Youliang Tian, Wei Ni
abstract: Emerging applications in next-generation vehicular systems place stringent demands on mobile networks, demanding uninterrupted, ultra-low latency and ultra-high task throughput performance. To address these challenges in vehicular edge computing, this paper proposes a three-layer divide-and-conquer deep reinforcement learning algorithm (H3DRA) to optimize task offloading by steady-state awareness in highly dynamic network topology. Specifically, we introduce an adaptive task allocation algorithm based on interruption prediction to determine the optimal offloading destination for each task. We further develop a clustering-assisted hierarchical deep reinforcement learning algorithm, enabling adaptive routing in multi-hop, multi-route and dynamic topologies. Simulation results show H3DRA surpasses conventional methods, significantly reducing service interruptions and improving task throughput.

title: Symmetric Private Information Retrieval (SPIR) on Graph-Based Replicated Systems
authors: Shreya Meel, Sennur Ulukus
abstract: We introduce the problem of symmetric private information retrieval (SPIR) on replicated databases modeled by a simple graph. In this model, each vertex corresponds to a server, and a message is replicated on two servers if and only if there is an edge between them. We consider the setting where the server-side common randomness necessary to accomplish SPIR is also replicated at the servers according to the graph, and we call this as message-specific common randomness. In this setting, we establish a lower bound on the SPIR capacity, i.e., the maximum download rate, for general graphs, by proposing an achievable SPIR scheme. Next, we prove that, for any SPIR scheme to be feasible, the minimum size of message-specific randomness should be equal to the size of a message. Finally, by providing matching upper bounds, we derive the exact SPIR capacity for the class of path and regular graphs.

title: A Rate Analysis on Channels With Unreliable Entanglement Assistance
authors: Jonas Hawellek, Marcel A. Mross, Christian Deppe, Eduard A. Jorswieck
abstract: Entanglement resources shared between a transmitter and a receiver can enhance communication rates. In practice, however, these resources are imperfect, and different models are available to address these issues. One approach is to consider the entanglement assistance as not being reliably available. This model provides rate pairs describing the worst-case and best-case communication scenarios. We propose an extended model that introduces a stochastic assumption regarding the availability of entanglement assistance. Thus, this model enables the analysis of average communication rates. For the qubit depolarizing channel, we demonstrate how this model allows for the optimization of a superposition coding strategy by adjusting the amount of entanglement used to maximize communication rates. Furthermore, we observe that the advantage provided by entanglement assistance on a depolarizing channel does not monotonically increase with the intensity of the depolarizing noise, but it is particularly less helpful at a depolarizing probability of 0.193.

title: Glaucoma Detection Using Gray-Level Co-Occurrence Matrix and Extreme Learning Machine
authors: Bharath K P, Govindaraj P, Anilkumar V, Divya M N, Rajesh Kumar Muthu, Rani Chinnappa Naidu
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Glaucoma, a major cause of blindness, that doesn't manifest symptoms until there has been a noticeable loss of vision necessitates early and accurate detection. Early intervention is crucial in preventing further vision deterioration, and the application of these advanced analytical methods can significantly aid in glaucoma treatments and early identification. This study introduces an automated diagnostic framework designed to identify glaucoma using retinal fundus images. The system employs texture-based descriptors derived from the Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) to characterize retinal patterns, and classification is performed using an Extreme Learning Machine (ELM). Additionally, Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) features combined with a feedforward neural network are utilized to distinguish between healthy and glaucomatous retinal images. To evaluate the proposed systems, we have used publicly available HRF dataset healthy and retinal images were used. The proposed system provides better results compare to the other existing state-of-theart techniques.

title: Reactive to Agentic:Next Gen AI Agent Transition Framework
authors: Rinki Singh, Tammana Sachdeva, Sabita, Shikha Arora, Deepak Garg
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: The development of the artificial intelligence (AI) system has advanced past simple reactivity-supported and rule-based agents to high-level learning mechanisms. The current shift that is emerging is associated with agentic AI systems, that are autonomous, goal-directed, and reason based and adaptive decision-making. The paper introduces a formal transition model that is used in developing agentic agents of the next generation. We describe the key characteristics, architectural requirements, technologies which enable this transformation as well aspects of ethics behind this transformation. In order to determine the validity of the framework, we provide a case study in the field of autonomous flying rebooking, showcasing how the use of multi-agent orchestration and goal planning can limit the user intervention and make the system more adaptive and better functioning. The effectiveness of our approach is confirmed by a comparative assessment of its work with well-known agentic systems (AutoGPT, ReAct, and CAMEL) in such important parameters as task success, autonomy, and coordination. The present work is contributing the potential to develop ethical and practical grounds of improving intelligent, scalable, and human-aligned AI systems.

title: Textual Symptom Understanding Using ZSL and FSL for Clinical Predictions
authors: Umesh Gupta, Sudhanshu Gupta, Sehaj K Khurana, Sakshi Gupta
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Nowadays, NLP has enabled the interpretation of unstructured clinical text for disease prediction, presenting opportunities to expand diagnostic support in low-resource healthcare environments. This study addresses the challenge of mapping free-text symptom descriptions to disease categories when labeled data is scarce. To evaluate methods suitable for such settings, five deep learning models-BART, BioBERT, FLAN-T5, BioClinicalBERT, and a CNN baseline-are compared across four publicly available medical datasets. The models are assessed under two learning paradigms: Zero-Shot Learning, where predictions are made without prior exposure to labeled examples, and Few-Shot Learning, where a limited number of examples guide training. Performance is measured using widely accepted metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Results demonstrate that domain-specific transformer models, particularly BioBERT and BioClinicalBERT, consistently outperform general-purpose models, achieving higher scores under both learning scenarios. Contextual biomedical pretraining enables these models to reliably extract and leverage clinical semantics from patient narratives, even with minimal supervision. This work highlights the practical value of specialized NLP solutions for early disease detection and supports their deployment as lightweight, scalable tools in real-world clinical and resource-limited settings.

title: Deadline-Aware Data Collection for Satellite IoT Networks
authors: Chi-Han Wu, Kate Ching-Ju Lin
abstract: As Internet of Things (IoT) devices are widely deployed for remote data sensing, Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks are used in areas where traditional terrestrial ground stations are unavailable. To address the challenges of dynamic topologies and limited spectrum resources between satellites and IoT devices, recent research has focused on improving spectrum utilization through transmission scheduling. However, these efforts have often overlooked the timing requirements of IoT data collection. To address this limitation, our work introduces a deadline-aware data collection approach for satellite networks. We formulate an optimization model that explicitly accounts for the penalties associated with data expiration and aims to maximize on-time data collection. A greedy algorithm is proposed to solve this optimization problem by prioritizing data transmissions based on dynamic channel conditions and data urgency. Simulations conducted using NS3 show that deadline-aware scheduling effectively meets heterogeneous deadlines and, compared to deadline-oblivious scheduling, improves resource utilization across various scenarios.

title: Optimizing Training of Policies on Hierarchical Multi-fidelity Environments
authors: Hanzhi Yu, Hasan Farooq, Julien Forgeat, Shruti Bothe, Kristijonas Cyras, Md Moin Uddin Chowdhury, Mingzhe Chen
abstract: In this paper, we investigate a novel digital network twin (DNT) assisted deep learning (DL) model training framework that enables a base station (BS) to select the data collection source from both physical network and DNT for training DL models used to optimize network performance. In particular, we consider a DNT enabled cellular network that consists of a physical network where a BS uses several antennas to serve multiple mobile users, and a DNT that is a virtual representation of the physical network. The BS must adjust the antenna tilt angles to optimize the data rates of all users. Due to the user mobility, the BS may not be able to accurately track the network dynamics. Hence, a reinforcement learning (RL) approach is used to dynamically adjust the antenna tilt angles. To train the RL, we can use data collected from the physical network and the DNT. The data collected from the physical network is more accurate but incurs more communication overhead compared to the data collected from the DNT. Therefore, it is necessary to determine the ratio of data collected from the physical network and the DNT to improve the training of the RL model, so as to optimize the tilt angle of each antenna in response to user mobility. We formulate an optimization problem to jointly optimize the tilt angle adjustment policy and the data collection strategy, aiming to maximize the data rates of all users while constraining the time delay introduced by collecting data from the physical network. To solve this optimization problem, we propose a hierarchical RL framework consisting of a two-level proximal policy optimization (PPO). The first level PPO dynamically adjusts the antenna tilt angles, and the second level PPO determines the ratio of data collected from the physical network to improve the first level PPO training performance. Compared to traditional single RL algorithms such as deep Q network (DQN), the designed method optimizes the data collection ratio and the antenna tilt angles at diverse time intervals, allowing the second level PPO to adjust the data collection ratio with a large timescale using the training information provided by the first level PPO, and allowing the first level PPO focuses on adjusting the antenna tilt angles with a small timescale. Simulation results show that our proposed method reduces the physical network data collection delay by up to 71.85% compared to a hierarchical RL that uses a deep deterministic policy gradient algorithm as the second level RL.

title: Evaluation of Reactive Power Equipment Voltage Support Capability Based on Gray Clustering
authors: Ying Huang, Huixin Liu, Xiaojie Pan, Dejun Shao, Mengxuan Shi, Yajun Wu, Mujie Zhang, Jun Yang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: Due to the weak reactive power regulation capability of reactive power equipment, which can easily cause voltage instability when connected to the receiving-end power grid, we propose voltage support capability assessment methods based on fixed weight clustering using the grey whitening weight function. First, the portfolio weight optimization model for cooperative game is established to calculate the index weights based on the principle of minimizing deviation. Second, to address the limitation of the traditional triangular whitening weight function whose center point jumps lead to ambiguous evaluation, two center point hybrid sinusoidal whitening weight function design methods are proposed. Finally, the reactive power equipment is evaluated hierarchically, and the simulation results verify that the hybrid sinusoidal whitening weight function is evaluated with higher accuracy.

title: On the Analytical Error Performance of LoRa-Based LEO Satellite IoT
authors: Quantao Yu, Deepak Mishra, Hua Wang, Dongxuan He, Jinhong Yuan, Michail Matthaiou
abstract: Recently, Long-Range (LoRa)-based low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite Internet-of-Things (IoT) has been recognized as a key enabler to provide ubiquitous connectivity for the Internet of remote things. A rigorous error performance analysis of such a new paradigm is quite essential for its practical implementation and evaluation. In this paper, we propose a novel analytical framework for quantitatively characterizing the error performance of LoRa-based LEO satellite IoT systems by leveraging an empirically-verified satellite-to-ground channel model, where both the channel characteristics of near-Earth satellite communications and the waveform properties of LoRa modulation are considered. Moreover, to enable a practical analysis, inter-end device interference is taken into account in the presence of both time and frequency offsets. Based on this, closed-form symbol and bit error rate expressions are derived by approximating the impact of the overall interference distributed within the decision metrics to that of the peak interference. Numerical simulations not only validate our theoretical analysis but also provide further insights into the error performance improvements of LoRa-based LEO satellite IoT systems.

title: HLAST: Hierarchical Label-Aware Summarisation Transformer for Enhanced Cybersecurity Log Analysis
authors: Shiven Vasan, Suryansh Vikram Singh, Dolly Sharma, Saurabh Yadav
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: The exponential growth of cybersecurity log data results in severe impediments for security analysts in extracting insightful information from overwhelmingly vast technical data. This paper proposes HLAST (Hierarchical Label-Aware Summarisation Transformer), a novel two-stage architecture combining DistilBERT-based classification with T5-based summarisation for improved cybersecurity log analysis. The method works around the critical gap between traditional anomaly detection approaches and human-interpretable summaries required in Security Operations Centers. Raw logs are first semantically classified into threat types by a fine-tuned DistilBERT model at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{9 2. 0 3 \%}$</tex> accuracy, and summaries are then generated contextually by a label-aware <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{T 5}$</tex> transformer with a ROUGE-L score of 77.1 %. HLAST was experimentally evaluated on a hybrid dataset composed of CICIDS-2017 network traffic logs and LogHub Zookeeper system logs and found that it generates interpretable, actionable intelligence for cybersecurity practitioners. The modular design permits HLAST to be deployed in real-time environments, thus alleviating the work burden on analysts and enhancing threat response times.

title: Quantum Network Simulators Integration with Open-Source 5G Mobile Stack
authors: Majid K, Shubh Agarwal, Siddharth Das, Riccardo Bassoli, Frank H. P. Fitzek, Chandrashekar Jatoth, Koteswararao Kondepu
abstract: The rise of quantum computing poses a major threat to traditional ciphers, specifically making the Radio Access Network (RAN) vulnerable to future quantum-based attacks. To address this challenge, this paper proposes quantum-inspired RAN (Q-RAN), a next-generation framework designed to future-proof telecom networks against quantum threats. As a foundation, to evaluate, we integrate quantum network simulators with the Open-Source OpenAirInterface (OAI) stack to deploy a 5G standalone network capable of supporting quantum operations. We analyzed these, and the best fit is taken for quantum proofing. This paper considered four different quantum simulators — QuNetSim, SimQN, SeQuence, and NetSquid — for integrating with the 5G Open source stack. The framework generated by these simulators is called Quantum Gateways (namely Qtunnels) enables seamless message exchange between quantum and classical RAN components. This integrated architecture showcases how quantum simulators can be harmonized on existing 5G infrastructure, establishing a resilient and adaptable telco ecosystem catered to the quantum era or post-quantum era. In addition, this paper quantifies the Qtunnel impact — F1 Interface — between the Central Unit (CU) and Distribution Unit (DU) of RAN. The results show that the Post Quantum Cryptography (PQC)-enabled F1 interfaces provide a two-fold overhead delay when compared to the benchmark delay, however, the PQC-enabled RAN interfaces are resistant to quantum threats.

title: Physical Layer Security of THz Orbital Angular Momentum MIMO Systems with a Successive Interference Cancellation Eavesdropper
authors: Duschia Bodet, Innem V. A. K. Reddy, Josep M. Jornet
abstract: Spatial multiplexing is not always feasible in terahertz and sub-terahertz (THz) MIMO channels due to the primarily line-of-sight propagation, but recent works have explored the possibility of using orbital angular momentum (OAM) to enable multiplexing in line-of-sight scenarios. This paper explores a certain physical-layer security risk and a corresponding mitigation strategy for THz MIMO systems using orbital angular momentum (OAM). A novel successive interference cancellation eavesdropping attack is introduced for OAM-MIMO THz systems, and simulations demonstrate that the security of the link decreases in the far field. A security countermeasure is introduced and proven to be effective in physically protecting the communications link.

title: Intelligent Multi-link EDCA Optimization for Delay-Bounded QoS in Wi-Fi 7
authors: Peini Yi, Wenchi Cheng, Jingqing Wang, Jinzhe Pan, Yuehui Ouyang, Wei Zhang
abstract: IEEE 802.11be (Wi-Fi 7) introduces Multi-Link Operation (MLO) as a While MLO offers significant parallelism and capacity, realizing its full potential in guaranteeing strict delay bounds and optimizing Quality of Service (QoS) for diverse, heterogeneous traffic streams in complex multi-link scenarios remain a significant challenge. This is largely due to the limitations of static Enhanced Distributed Channel Access (EDCA) parameters and the complexity inherent in cross-link traffic management. To address this, this paper investigates the correlation between overall MLO QoS indicators and the configuration of EDCA parameters and Acess Catagory (AC) traffic allocation among links. Based on this analysis, we formulate a constrained optimization problem aiming to minimize the sum of overall packet loss rates for all access categories while satisfying their respective overall delay violation probability constraints. A Genetic Algorithm (GA)-based MLO EDCA QoS optimization algorithm is designed to efficiently search the complex configuration space of AC assignments and EDCA parameters. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach’s efficacy in generating adaptive MLO configuration strategies that align with diverse service requirements. The proposed solution significantly improves delay distribution characteristics, and enhance QoS robustness and resource utilization efficiency in high-load MLO environments.

title: A Comparative Analysis Using ANN and SVM to Understand Consumer Behavior in Digital Marketing
authors: Shubhashree Sahoo, D. K. Mohanty, R. Shakthivel, Rupa Rani Sharma, Priyanka Sharma, K. Sengottaiyan
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Marketers can now access massive data sets that include all of this information. You can look for patterns in how users use your site using these datasets. Traditional ways of analyzing data aren't enough anymore because customer behavior is both nonlinear and dynamic. There is a rising need for intelligent systems that can learn from data and accurately guess what people will do in the future. The hardest part is figuring out how to act like this. We want to see how well artificial neural networks (ANN) and support vector machines (SVM) can guess what clients will buy in this study. We cleaned up the information we acquired from e-commerce and social media sites. This meant finding traits and making the data more consistent. Then, models of supervised learning were employed to look at the data. We used F1-score, recall, accuracy, and precision as performance measures for the test. The ANN model was better than the others, especially when it comes to figuring out nonlinear and sophisticated consumer behaviors. This was especially true when the datasets were bigger. The Support Vector Machine (SVM) was shown to work well on smaller datasets that were highly different from one other. The study's results reveal that ANN is better for marketing plans that can change, whereas SVM is better for reaching specific groups of people.

title: Adaptive Federated Learning with Lyapunov Optimization for Robust Radio Link Failure Detection in 5G Networks
authors: Umar Farooq, Aroosa Hameed, Aris Leivadeas, Ioannis Lambadaris
abstract: Radio Link Failure (RLF) detection is essential for maintaining reliable connectivity in 5G networks. However, traditional centralized detection mechanisms often encounter scalability and latency constraints when managing large-scale, geographically distributed infrastructures. To address this challenge, we introduce a Lyapunov-driven federated learning framework that adaptively selects gNodeBs based on both data utility and historical participation. This approach leverages an LSTM-based local model to capture temporal patterns in link performance, thereby enhancing RLF detection. Extensive evaluations on a real-world 5G dataset demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared to baseline approaches when detecting rare failure events. By simultaneously prioritizing performance and fairness, this framework offers a scalable solution suited to diverse and dynamic 5G environments.

title: Collaborative Inference in the Extreme Edge: A Proactive Task Allocation Scheme
authors: Rawan F. El-Khatib, Nizar Zorba, Hossam S. Hassanein
abstract: The growing demand for foundation model (FM)-powered services has sparked debates about the scalability of their deployment, due to the intensive resource requirements, latency sensitivity, and privacy-related concerns. To overcome these limitations, recent efforts advocate for the democratization of AI through eXtreme Edge Computing (XEC). XEC leverages the idle computational capacities of user-owned devices, referred to as eXtreme Edge Devices (XEDs), to enable distributed inference. While recent advances have brought this vision closer to technical feasibility, achieving low-latency inference at the eXtreme Edge (XE) remains challenging due to the dynamic and heterogeneous nature of XEDs and the full-task dependency among subtasks required for successful inference. In this work, we introduce the Proactive Minimum Response Delay (P-MRD) scheme. P-MRD incorporates predictions of dynamic resource availability to proactively form collaborative compute clusters that ensure full inference task completion while minimizing response delays and maintaining Quality of Service (QoS) constraints. We formulate the task allocation problem as a Binary Integer Linear Program (BILP) and derive an analytical solution using Lagrangian analysis and the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions. Additionally, we propose a greedy heuristic algorithm that efficiently approximates the optimal solution. Performance evaluations using data from a realistic testbed demonstrate that P-MRD achieves a 31% reduction in the response delay and a 62% increase in the inference success rate compared to prominent baselines.

title: One Model to Catch Them All: Autoencoder-Based Anomaly Detection in Next-Generation Networks
authors: Islam Obaidat, Furqan Rustam, Anca Delia Jurcut
abstract: Anomaly detection is crucial for ensuring the security and reliability of next-generation heterogeneous networks, which integrate a variety of devices (e.g., IoT and traditional devices). Autoencoder (AE)-based approaches have shown promise in identifying network anomalies by modeling benign traffic and detecting deviations. However, existing AE methods, mostly evaluated in homogeneous environments, struggle to generalize in next-generation multi-environment (M-En) networks (comprising both IoT and traditional devices) due to diverse traffic patterns. This generalization issue is evidenced through an initial ablation study, where AE models trained solely on a single traffic type (e.g., IoT-only) fail to detect anomalies effectively in M-En networks. To address this limitation, a Residual Autoencoder (RD-AE) specifically designed for anomaly detection in next-generation M-En networks is presented. RD-AE is trained on a combined actual benign dataset containing both IoT and traditional traffic, allowing it to accurately identify abnormal (malicious) deviations in M-En networks. An attention-based BiLSTM classifier subsequently categorizes these detected anomalies, differentiating between malicious IoT and traditional traffic to support targeted defensive measures. A human-in-the-loop continuous learning mechanism is integrated into the classifier, ensuring adaptability to emerging threats. A realistic testbed is used to generate M-En traffic and evaluate the proposed framework in real time. Experimental results show that RD-AE achieves up to 97.4% accuracy in detecting previously unseen attack scenarios.

title: Research on Short-Term Load Forecasting in Power Systems Employing Neural Networks Improved by Normalization Modules
authors: Yunkai Du
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: Short-term load forecasting plays an important role in power system security and economic dispatch. Based on the serious performance difference usually present in the real power system between the training set and testing set that generally occurs under the conventional neural network model of forecasting, the present paper presents the enhanced neural network algorithm. This paper explains in detail the basic construction of neural network and the method of neural network learning. It is further revealed that the normalization modules play the dominant role on the robustness of model training, the computational speed, and the model genericity for the task, and some methods of their improvement were given. Optimized normalization methods result in the decrease in Mean Squared Error of prediction. Prediction curves of the model tend to close up with curves of actual loads variation trend. This proves the method of optimization of normalization modules is the effective way to increase both prediction accuracy and the generalization capability of NNs for short-term load forecasting, and it can enhance the accuracy of short-term load forecasting in this way.

title: On the Robust Position Control of Variable Stiffness Series Elastic Actuators
authors: Emre Sariyildiz, Moe Horikoshi, Takahiro Nozaki
publish: 2026 IEEE 19th International Conference on Advanced Motion Control (AMC)
abstract: This paper presents robust position control strategies for the novel Variable Stiffness Series Elastic Actuator (VSSEA). By employing a constructed state-space model, two control schemes are developed in a unified framework: a state-feedback controller and a sliding mode controller, both integrated with a second-order disturbance observer (DOb). The proposed framework achieves high-performance motion control by precisely estimating and compensating for internal and external disturbances, while preserving the nominal dynamic response. Simulation results demonstrate that pole-placement-based controllers are highly sensitive to disturbances, whereas Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR)-based controllers offer improved robustness at the expense of slower dynamics. By incorporating the second-order DOb, robustness is significantly enhanced without degrading time response, and the LQR controller can be tuned solely for performance optimisation. Experimental results confirm that the proposed robust position controllers can be implemented in real world applications. These results highlight the effectiveness of the proposed approach and lay the foundation for future investigations on robust stability and performance under different stiffness settings.

title: Quantum Computing: Anticipated Developments and Applied Prospects
authors: Himani, Yash Sharma, Vaibhav Anand, Sunita Joshi
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Quantum computers offer potential revolutionary computing capabilities, but there is a myriad of issues to resolve before these systems can be made useful, such as error rates, scaling, and how they can be integrated with classical computers. This paper discusses the future developments that are expected and how they can be applied by reviewing the current theoretical and scientific developments. An extensive literature review and consultations with the experts in the field of quantum algorithms, hardware structures, and error- correction technology were used to evaluate the trends linked to quantum computing. The results indicate that hybrid quantum- classical systems are in a position to solve short-term constraints, and will find application in cryptography, optimization and material simulation. Next, qubit coherence and gate fidelity continue to improve and thus will increase the level of computational reliability and expand its industrial applicability. The paper highlights the need for interdisciplinary cooperation in the development of a synergy between the informative theory and its application practice. In summary, though quantum computing is only in its early years, its outlook points to the far-reaching impact it could have regarding intractable problem-solving, provided that current technical limitations are addressed. They will be essential to sustaining research and development in such a way that will eventually achieve its transformative potential in different areas.

title: Transmit Power Minimization in Stacked Intelligent Metasurface-Aided Multi-User Systems
authors: Haoxian Niu, Jiancheng An, Shining Lin, Lu Gan, Michail Matthaiou, Symeon Chatzinotas
abstract: Stacked intelligent metasurfaces (SIMs), emerging as a revolutionary programmable electromagnetic architecture, have demonstrated unprecedented capabilities in manipulating wireless propagation environments. However, the existing research on SIM-aided downlink communication does not consider the fairness among users. Therefore, this paper studies a SIM-aided hybrid analog-digital system, which aims to fairly guarantee the communication quality of each user while minimizing the transmission power. The hybrid system avails of a SIM for enhancing the communication channel with digital precoding to effectively suppress the interference between users. To this end, we formulate a transmit power minimization problem under quality-of-service constraints, solved by an efficient alternating optimization (AO) algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate that compared to conventional fully digital multiuser multiple-input single-output (MISO) systems, the proposed SIM-aided hybrid system requires 6.93 dBm less transmit power under the same signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) constraints for users. This work reveals the SIM’s powerful wave-based beamforming capabilities, providing effective solutions for energy-efficient networks with low hardware cost.

title: AQUILA: A Flexible Architecture Guideline for Building Custom Distributed Systems Testbeds
authors: Luigi Mastromauro, Edwin Kayang, Mishel Jyothis Paul, Eric Jahns, Muslum Ozgur Ozmen, Michel Kinsy
publish: 2025 IEEE 23rd International Conference on Embedded and Ubiquitous Computing (EUC)
abstract: Testing and developing distributed systems often requires either large-scale, rigid infrastructures or purely softwarebased simulators that fail to capture the complexity of real-world environments. In this work, we introduce AQUILA, a flexible architecture guideline and reference implementation designed to help researchers and developers build their own customizable testbeds using heterogeneous hardware and software resources. AQUILA enables scalable and reconfigurable experimentation even with limited equipment, supporting general-purpose, reconfigurable, and edge computing domains. By combining lightweight task orchestration, dynamic resource management, and hybrid communication protocols, AQUILA provides a practical solution for deploying, testing, and optimizing distributed applications. We describe the architecture, demonstrate its adaptability across multiple computing domains through concrete use cases, and discuss its potential for bridging the gap between abstract simulation and costly industrial testbeds.

title: Encoding of Entanglement-assisted Quantum Codes with Fault-tolerant Syndrome Measurements
authors: Abhi Kumar Sharma, Pavan Kumar, Shayan Srinivasa Garani
abstract: Entanglement-assisted (EA) quantum codes relax the condition of dual-containment property on the classical codes by using pre-shared Bell pairs across the quantum transceiver. We design an encoder for the EA Calderbank-Shor-Steane (CSS) quantum codes by utilizing the operators from the Clifford group. We also present a fault-tolerant measurement strategy for obtaining syndromes by incorporating an extra resource state while working with a faulty measurement device.

title: Leveraging Bi-Directional Channel Reciprocity for Robust Ultra-Low-Rate Implicit CSI Feedback with Deep Learning
authors: Zhenyu Liu, Yi Ma, Rahim Tafazolli, Zhi Ding
abstract: Deep learning-based implicit channel state information (CSI) feedback has been introduced to enhance spectral efficiency in massive MIMO systems. Existing methods often show performance degradation in ultra-low-rate scenarios and inadaptability across diverse environments. In this paper, we propose Dual-ImRUNet, an efficient uplink-assisted deep implicit CSI feedback framework incorporating two novel plug-in preprocessing modules to achieve ultra-low feedback rates while maintaining high environmental robustness. First, a novel bi-directional correlation enhancement module is proposed to strengthen the correlation between uplink and downlink CSI eigenvector matrices. This module projects highly correlated uplink and downlink channel matrices into their respective eigenspaces, effectively reducing redundancy for ultra-low-rate feedback. Second, an innovative input format alignment module is designed to maintain consistent data distributions at both encoder and decoder sides without extra transmission overhead, thereby enhancing robustness against environmental variations. Finally, we develop an efficient transformer-based implicit CSI feedback network to exploit angular-delay domain sparsity and bi-directional correlation for ultra-low-rate CSI compression. Simulation results demonstrate successful reduction of the feedback overhead by 85% compared with the state-of-the-art method and robustness against unseen environments.

title: Implementing an Intelligent Hybrid Wired/Wireless SDN Switch with Machine Learning
authors: Jiayang Chen, Yang Xiang, Yue Su, Kien Nguyen, Hiroo Sekiya
abstract: A hybrid wired/wireless SDN switch enables unified control across mixed-access networks but faces challenges in real-time traffic classification and flow management due to the lack of intelligence. This paper presents an intelligent hybrid SDN switch augmented with embedded machine learning (ML) models for accurate traffic classification and low-latency prediction. We evaluated six supervised ML algorithms, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, Naive Bayes, k-Nearest Neighbors, and Neural Network, under both single-flow and concurrent multiflow scenarios using real traffic traces, including DNS, Ping, Telnet, and interactive gaming protocols. The evaluation results show that the Random Forest model consistently outperforms others, achieving classification accuracies of 99.97% in single-flow and 99.86% in multi-flow scenarios, while accurately predicting the traffic in millisecond-level inference latency. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of embedding ML-driven intelligence into hybrid SDN switches to support real-time, application-aware traffic control in emerging network infrastructures.

title: Sponge Attacks on Sensing AI: Energy-Latency Vulnerabilities and Defense via Model Pruning
authors: Syed Mhamudul Hasan, Hussein Zangoti, Iraklis Anagnostopoulos, Abdur R. Shahid
abstract: Recent studies have shown that sponge attacks can significantly increase the energy consumption and inference latency of deep neural networks (DNNs). However, prior work has focused primarily on computer vision and natural language processing tasks, overlooking the growing use of lightweight AI models in sensing-based applications on resource-constrained devices, such as those in Internet of Things (IoT) environments. These attacks pose serious threats of energy depletion and latency degradation in systems where limited battery capacity and real-time responsiveness are critical for reliable operation. This paper makes two key contributions. First, we present the first systematic exploration of energy-latency sponge attacks targeting sensing-based AI models. Using wearable sensing-based AI as a case study, we demonstrate that sponge attacks can substantially degrade performance by increasing energy consumption, leading to faster battery drain, and by prolonging inference latency. Second, to mitigate such attacks, we investigate model pruning, a widely adopted compression technique for resource-constrained AI, as a potential defense. Our experiments show that pruning-induced sparsity significantly improves model resilience against sponge poisoning. We also quantify the trade-offs between model efficiency and attack resilience, offering insights into the security implications of model compression in sensing-based AI systems deployed in IoT environments.

title: A Truth Discovery Method for Mobile Crowd Sensing in Mines Based on a Hybrid Bi-LSTM/GRU Network
authors: Chang Su, Haifeng Jiang, Chaogang Tang, Shuo Xiao, Huaming Wu, Ruidong Li
abstract: Ensuring safety and operational continuity in underground coal mines requires robust mine monitoring. Traditional methods based on fixed sensors and manual inspections suffer from limited coverage, high cost, and poor real-time performance. Mobile Crowd Sensing (MCS), enabled by miner-carried devices, offers flexible coverage but introduces challenges such as data sparsity, noise, and heterogeneity due to device variability and electromagnetic interference. This article proposes a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory/Gated Recurrent Unit-based Truth Discovery (BLGTD) method for mine MCS. The model integrates spatiotemporal sequence modeling with Monte Carlo Dropout-based uncertainty quantification, enabling adaptive fusion of multi-source data. Experimental results show that BLGTD achieves a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.19 ± 0.01 ppm in CH<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf> concentration estimation when 90% of data comes from reliable miners, yielding a 57.8% improvement over traditional weighted averaging. The method demonstrates strong robustness under conditions of data incompleteness, device heterogeneity, and signal interference.

title: Enhancing Unlabeled Signal Representation: A Diffusion-Feature-Based Semi-Supervised Framework for AMR
authors: Haoyue Tan, Yu Li, Zhenxi Zhang, Xiaoran Shi, Feng Zhou
abstract: Existing deep learning (DL)-based AMR methods achieve impressive performance with abundant labeled signals. However, in non-cooperative scenarios, acquiring labeled signals is challenging, leaving a vast amount of unlabeled data underutilized by current DL based automatic modulation recognition (AMR) approaches. To address this challenge, we propose a semi-supervised AMR framework based on a modulation signal diffusion generative model (MSDGM). This framework follows a two-stage training strategy. In the first stage, unlike existing unsupervised paradigms that rely on proxy tasks or pseudo-labels, MSDGM learns the data distribution from unlabeled signals. In the second stage, MSDGM is frozen as a feature extractor, and a classifier is trained on a small set of labeled signals to achieve effective AMR. This decoupled training paradigm significantly reduces the dependence on label quantity, ensuring rapid generalization and robust recognition even with minimal labeled signals. Extensive experimental results demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed MSDGM-based framework under few labeled signal scenarios. Notably, at SNR>0dB with only 2 labeled signals for each class, the proposed method achieves accuracies exceeding 72% and 76% on the 11-modulation-type recognition tasks of RML2016.10A and RML2022, respectively, significantly outperforming existing supervised and semi-supervised AMR methods.

title: TAMIS: Trust-Driven Federated Learning via Monetary Incentives for Social Event Analysis
authors: Eirini Eleni Tsiropoulou, Md Nafis Washir
abstract: The rapid growth of social media activity has generated vast amounts of fragmented data resulting in substantial challenges in the social event analysis while preserving user privacy. Federated Learning (FL) offers a decentralized solution and enables the local model training without sharing raw data. However, user participation requires appropriate incentives and trust in Online Social Network (OSN) providers. This paper presents TAMIS, a trust-driven FL framework enhanced by monetary incentives for social event analysis. TAMIS introduces a novel trust-based reinforcement learning (RL) mechanism for OSN provider selection that enables the users to identify the most reliable provider based on analysis quality and incentives. Additionally, a game-theoretic bargaining model ensures the fair incentive distribution among the users. Detailed simulations show TAMIS’s superior performance, scalability, and adaptability compared to centralized models, while effectively balancing trust, fairness, and efficiency. The framework’s ability to prioritize high-accuracy users and dynamically adapt to the users’ charactestics makes it a robust solution for decentralized, trust-aware FL systems, with significant potential for real-world applications.

title: Human Activity Recognition Using Infrared Array Sensors: A Multi-Modal Approach
authors: Mondher Bouazizi, Saqib Mehmood, Yue Yin, Tomoaki Ohtsuki
abstract: Human activity recognition (HAR) is important for assistive care, enhancing safety and timely assistance. Traditional HAR systems using wearables and non-wearables have limitations, such as intrusiveness, privacy concerns, dependence on environmental conditions, and reliance on a single modality where noise can drastically affect their performance. In this paper, we propose a novel HAR approach using infrared (IR) thermal sensors, which offer a non-intrusive and privacy-preserving solution. Our method addresses key challenges in HAR using IR sensors, including low resolution, sensor noise, and the limitations of single-modal approaches, by integrating multi-modal learning. The proposed deep learning framework combines depth estimation, pose detection, and thermal feature extraction, utilizing a hybrid CNN-LSTM model with an attention mechanism to improve activity classification accuracy. Through extensive experimentation, we demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms traditional HAR methods which use raw IR sensor data and rely on a single modality, achieving high precision and robustness across diverse environmental conditions. By employing all modalities, our approach reaches an accuracy equal to 98.78%.

title: Collaborative Evaluation and Improvement Method for the Operational Resilience of Distribution Networks by Integrating Data-Driven and Physical Models
authors: Yuehao Yan, Xiaohui Liu, Wenli Wang, Yanan Yang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: This paper proposes a collaborative evaluation and improvement method for the operational resilience of distribution networks, which integrates data-driven and physical models, to address the difficulty in quantifying the impact of voltage sag caused by the opaque production information of multiple sensitive users in the distribution network, as well as the challenge of limited effectiveness of operational resilience improvement strategies. Based on the traditional resilience index framework, a quantified operational resilience index was constructed that takes into account the impact of voltage sag on sensitive users. To characterize the differential tolerance characteristics of voltage sag under different sensitive loads, a voltage sag trajectory feature system was established, and a data-driven operational resilience evaluation model was designed based on this. Further embed the evaluation process into a multi-objective energy storage optimization configuration physical model, forming a hybrid driven solution architecture. The simulation verification based on IEEE 33 node system shows that the proposed model effectively solves the problem of the lack of explicit function relationship between voltage sag characteristics and resilience indicators, and significantly improves the accuracy of system operation resilience evaluation while ensuring economy.

title: Efficient Precoding for LEO Satellites: A Low-Complexity Matrix Inversion Method via Woodbury Matrix Identity and arSVD
authors: Mohammad Momani, Thomas Delamotte, Andreas Knopp
abstract: The increasing deployment of massive active antenna arrays in low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites necessitates computationally efficient and adaptive precoding techniques to mitigate dynamic channel variations and enhance spectral efficiency. Regularized zero-forcing (RZF) precoding is widely used in multi-user MIMO systems; however, its real-time implementation is limited by the computationally intensive inversion of the Gram matrix. In this work, we develop a low-complexity framework that integrates the Woodbury (WB) formula with adaptive randomized singular value decomposition (arSVD) to efficiently update the Gram matrix inverse as the satellite moves along its orbit. By leveraging low-rank perturbations, the WB formula reduces inversion complexity, while arSVD dynamically extracts dominant singular components, further enhancing computational efficiency. Monte Carlo simulations demonstrate that the proposed method achieves computational savings of up to 61% compared to conventional RZF precoding with full matrix inversion, while incurring only a modest degradation in sum-rate performance. These results demonstrate that WB-arSVD offers a scalable and efficient solution for next-generation satellite communications, facilitating real-time deployment in power-constrained environments.

title: AI-Enhanced Web Crawler for Real-Time Discovery of Emerging Trends in Sustainable Finance
authors: Shiela David, Manjula Pattnaik, B Mahalakshmi, Mohammed Ali Sohail, Balagiri Chaitanya, S. Meenakshi
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: The evolving field of sustainable finance, which encompassing ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) investing, green bonds, and climate-related financial disclosures—demands timely, data-driven insights from vast web-based sources such as policy updates, corporate filings, news outlets, and research publications. Manual tracking is no longer feasible due to the volume and velocity of data. Traditional web crawlers are rule-based and lack contextual understanding, leading to poor relevance detection, high false positives, and missed emerging topics. This paper shows how to create an AI-powered web crawler using techniques including Natural Language Processing (NLP), subject modeling, and spotting trends. We use a mix of keyword-driven crawling and BERT/FinBERT transformer-based language models to figure out what is semantically relevant. Emerging keyword recognition takes advantage of the fact that term frequency-inverse document frequency (TF-IDF) changes over time. Temporal trend analysis over time-sliced document sets, on the other hand, uses Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA). We put the suggested crawler through its paces on 50,000 URLs. These included blogs established by regulatory entities, sites that simply report on financial news, and blogs run by schools and other organizations. When compared to baseline crawling, it was 87.5% accurate and 82.3% recall. This means it could find new problems with acquiring money in a way that lasts.

title: Automatic State Machine Inference for Binary Protocol Reverse Engineering
authors: Junhai Yang, Fenghua Li, Yixuan Zhang, Liang Fang, Yunchuan Guo, Zifu Li
abstract: Proprietary protocols are widely used to ensure efficient data transmission, enhance privacy, and meet specific application requirements. However, the lack of public standards often leaves their security inadequately evaluated, posing significant challenges for network security. Protocol Reverse Engineering (PRE) is used to analyze protocols by inferring their structure and behavior. However, existing PRE methods primarily focus on protocol format analysis, neglecting Protocol State Machine (PSM) analysis, which can lead to insufficient detection of abnormal behaviors and potential vulnerabilities. To address this, we propose an automatic PSM inference framework for unknown protocols, incorporating a fuzzy membership-based auto-converging DBSCAN algorithm for protocol format clustering, followed by a session clustering algorithm based on Needleman-Wunsch and K-Medoids algorithm to classify sessions by protocol type. Finally, we refine a probabilistic PSM algorithm to infer protocol states and transitions. Experiments show that our method can infer PSMs while enabling precise protocol classification.

title: Timing advance and Doppler shift estimation in LEO satellite networks: A recursive Bayesian study
authors: Ashutosh Balakrishnan, Pierre Popineau, Philippe Martins
abstract: Low earth orbit (LEO) satellite based non-terrestrial networks are a key theme of the upcoming 6G networks. These space networks are proposed to be used for high-mobility use-cases like airplanes and vehicles. The initial access process between a base station (BS) and a user equipment (UE) involves timing advance (TA) value computation at the BS, requiring precise BS location information at the UE. It becomes more challenging in LEO satellite networks due to the fast moving LEO satellites and large pathloss, in addition to the mobile UE. This paper aims to compute the TA and Doppler shift experienced at the UE by modeling the joint system dynamics in a LEO satellite-mobile UE network through an extended Kalman filter (EKF) based recursive Bayesian framework. The framework accurately models the joint system dynamics by considering the LEO satellite acceleration. It constructs the Jacobian to linearize the inherent non-linearities present in the motion. Probabilistic insights regarding the state-update and propagation are also provided. The analytical framework factors in the limited satellite visibility at the UE and the satellite-UE geometry w.r.t. the earth center. The proposed framework is also useful when the satellite and UE clocks are not in sync, with the corresponding clock drift a function of the measured time difference of arrivals. Our results showcase the efficacy and robustness of the proposed EKF framework to estimate the TA and Doppler shift, even at very high UE speeds. The work is expected to be extremely useful in realizing LEO satellite based non-terrestrial networks.

title: Research on Assessment and Application of Reasonable Capacity for Tobacco Retail Licensing
authors: Shifen Zhang, Lei Liu, Min Zhao, Cansheng Wei
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 International Conference on Information Economy, Data Modeling and Cloud Computing

title: Hierarchical Text Classification with LLMs via BERT-Based Semantic Modeling and Consistency Regularization
authors: Shuaidong Pan, Di Wu
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 International Conference on Information Economy, Data Modeling and Cloud Computing
abstract: None

title: The impact of supply chain uncertainty on dual innovation within manufacturing enterprises: An empirical analysis based on computational modeling and text mining
authors: Rongyan Jia, Jiaqi Meng

title: Regularized Deep Joint Source-Channel Coding for Robust Task-Oriented Semantic Communications
authors: Taewoo Park, Eunhye Hong, Yo-Seb Jeon, Namyoon Lee, Yongjune Kim
abstract: Semantic communications based on deep joint source-channel coding (JSCC) aim to improve communication efficiency by transmitting only task-relevant information. How-ever, ensuring robustness to the stochasticity of communication channels remains a key challenge in learning-based JSCC. In this paper, we propose a novel regularization technique for learning-based JSCC to enhance robustness against channel noise. The proposed method utilizes the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence as a regularizer term in the training loss, measuring the discrepancy between two posterior distributions: one under noisy channel conditions (noisy posterior) and one for a noise-free system (noise-free posterior). We further show that the expectation of the KL divergence given the encoded representation can be analytically approximated using the Fisher information matrix and the covariance matrix of the channel noise. Notably, the proposed regularization is architecture-agnostic, making it broadly applicable to general semantic communication systems over noisy channels.

title: SflLLM: Efficient Split Federated Learning for Large Language Model over Wireless Networks
authors: Kai Zhao, Chen Zhu, Mingzhe Chen, Chongwen Huang, Zhaohui Yang, Zhaoyang Zhang
abstract: Fine-tuning large language models (LLM) in a distributed manner over edge devices with limited communication and computational resources presents substantial challenges in wireless networks. To tackle these issues, this paper proposes a novel Split Federated Learning framework tailored for LLM (SflLLM), which integrates split federated learning with parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques. By employing model partitioning and low-rank adaptation (LoRA), SflLLM significantly reduces the computational load on edge devices. Moreover, the introduction of the federated server not only facilitates parallel training but also enhances privacy preservation. To accommodate the heterogeneous communication conditions and diverse computational capacities of edge devices—while accounting for the influence of LoRA rank selection on model convergence and training overhead—we formulate a joint optimization problem. This problem simultaneously optimizes subchannel allocation, power control, model split point selection, and LoRA rank configuration, with the objective of minimizing the overall training latency. An alternating optimization algorithm is developed to efficiently solve the proposed problem and accelerate the training process. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared to conventional methods, the proposed resource allocation scheme and adaptive LoRA rank selection strategy significantly reduce training latency.

title: Research on Optimization Strategy for Ordered Charging and Discharging of Electric Vehicles Based on Load Aggregator Management
authors: Zhe Wang, Fan Xu, Jiaxin Dong, Ji Li, Wentao Sun
publish: 2025 International Conference on Energy Evolution and Power Engineering: Transition, Intelligence, and Autonomy (EEPE-TIA)
abstract: With the growing adoption of electric vehicles (EVs), uncoordinated charging poses challenges to grid stability, such as increased peak load and peak-valley differences. This paper proposes an optimized ordered charging and discharging strategy managed by a load aggregator (LA). Using Monte Carlo simulation, EV travel and charging behaviors are modeled under both disordered and ordered modes. A multi-objective optimization model is developed, considering wind power consumption deviation, user satisfaction, and LA economic benefits. An improved Adaptive Particle Swarm Optimization (APSO) algorithm is applied to solve the model, with validation via CPLEX. Simulation results show that the proposed strategy flattens the load curve, enhances wind power integration, improves user satisfaction, and increases LA profits, verifying its effectiveness and practicality in supporting grid stability and EV integration.

title: Research on Linear-Switch Hybrid Power Hardware-in-the-Loop Simulation Technology
authors: Huaiguang Gu, Zhiwei Mi, Longze Kou, Feng Ji
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: The new power system topology, control, fault evolution, and other processes characterized by “double high” exhibit broadband, fast, nonlinear, and complex phenomena, posing severe challenges to simulation, testing, and verification technologies. Power Hardware in the Loop (PHIL) combines the convenience of real-time digital simulation with the authenticity of physical dynamic testing, bringing great flexibility to the research and testing of new power electronic equipment. In order to solve the challenges of wide voltage, high power, high bandwidth, high precision, high voltage swing rate, low delay and high requirements in PHIL simulation technology, this paper proposes a linear switch hybrid four quadrant power hardware in the loop simulation technology, and verifies it in electromagnetic transient simulation software PSCAD/EMTDC. The verification results show that the technology has certain feasibility.

title: Simulation Analysis of Thermal Electric Coupling Characteristics of Multiple Extraction Methods for Heating Units
authors: Libin Wen, Jinji Xi, Hong Hu, Zhiyuan Sun
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: Cogeneration units with steam extraction are restricted by the rigid “heat-led electricity” coupling, which limits peak-shaving capability and reduces efficiency. To address this issue, a full-process simulation model of a 300 MW subcritical cogeneration unit was established on the EBSILON Professional platform, and three extraction schemes-intermediate/low-pressure crossover, hot reheat, and cold reheat-were evaluated. Based on the analysis, a hybrid steam extraction collaborative operation strategy is proposed. The strategy prioritizes crossover extraction under rated conditions, as it minimizes power loss and matches industrial steam demand. When the main steam pressure falls below the required 0.8 MPa, the system dynamically switches to cold reheat extraction, which extends the minimum load by sacrificing part of the thermal efficiency. This adaptive switching effectively combines high efficiency with extended flexibility, broadening the feasible heating domain. Simulation results show that the hybrid strategy increases peak-shaving depth by 37 %, providing theoretical and engineering support for flexible lowcarbon power systems.

title: Magnetically Coupled Resonant Wireless Charging Technology Research of Electric Vehicles
authors: Zuyue Song, Muhua Lin, Qingshuai Yu, Tuanlong Zhang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: With the popularization of electric vehicles and the growth of market demand, wireless charging technology, as a safe, convenient and highly intelligent charging method, has greater advantages over wired charging and battery swapping charging methods. At present, magnetic coupling resonant type is widely used for wireless charging of electric vehicles. This article elaborately introduces the main components and working principles of the wireless charging system for electric vehicles, and conducts scheme demonstration and principle analysis for each part, revealing the structural and functional characteristics of the system. Secondly, by using the Simulink software, the constant current characteristics of the primary coil of the LCC-S compensation topology structure, the constant voltage characteristics of the output voltage of the rectifier bridge, and the effectiveness of the PI control strategy were verified. Finally, the system was connected as a whole and powered on for testing. The experimental results showed that the charging voltage was 220 V and the charging current was 5 A, verifying the feasibility and effectiveness of the design scheme.

title: Why Go Heavy? Rethinking Visual Information Reduction in EEG-to-Image Alignment
authors: Sangmin Jo, Adrián Ayuso-Muñoz, Wootaek Jeong, Heung-Il Suk
abstract: EEG-based visual brain decoding has recently gained increasing attention, as it seeks to align brain signals with visual representations to infer aspects of human visual perception. Recent approaches typically leverage pre-trained vision encoders (e.g., CLIP and DINO) for EEG–image alignment, and several studies have shown that applying image augmentations can further improve alignment. However, despite this progress, there is still no systematic analysis clarifying which visual encoders, or augmentation strategies most effectively facilitate EEG–image alignment. In this work, we present a comprehensive benchmark evaluating EEG-to-image alignment across thirteen pre-trained visual encoders and four EEG encoders, along with five augmentation strategies. Our findings s how that (i) CNN-based visual backbones consistently outperform ViT-based models, (ii) smaller visual models yield stronger alignment, revealing a negative correlation between model scale and alignment quality; and (iii) augmentations that reduce visual detail substantially improve alignment across all encoders. These results provide practical guidelines for selecting and designing models for EEG–vision alignment and highlight the importance of controlling visual information complexity in cross-modal representation learning.

title: Optimized Scheduling of Vehicle-to-Grid Interaction Based on Electric Truck
authors: Kan Zhong, Bo Bo, Zhenyu Jiang, Sixiang Zhao, Xiaokun Yang, Honglin Liu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: In response to the significant fluctuations in grid load caused by unregulated charging and discharging of large-scale electric trucks after they are connected to the grid, this paper proposes an optimized scheduling model for vehicle-to-grid (V2G) interaction based on electric trucks. The model employs the Monte Carlo method to simulate the operational scenarios of electric trucks. In the upper-level model, the objective is to minimize the operational costs of electric vehicle aggregators, while the lower-level model aims to minimize the total charging and discharging costs for electric truck users. Through a master-slave game theory approach to pricing, the model derives the optimal charging and discharging strategies. Simulation examples demonstrate that the proposed model effectively controls the charging and discharging behavior of electric trucks while balancing the interests of both aggregators and users.

title: Path Planning and Obstacle Avoidance Optimization for a Spraying Robot Based on an Improved Artificial Potential Field Algorithm
authors: Tengfei Zheng, Long Liu, Yahao Kuang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechanical Engineering, Intelligent Manufacturing, and Automation Technology (MEMAT)
abstract: To mitigate the risk of collisions during the automated spraying of complex components such as port equipment, this study proposes a path planning and obstacle avoidance optimization algorithm that integrates the Artificial Potential Field (APF) method with the Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT) algorithm. The proposed method addresses key limitations of conventional APF approaches in complex environments, including entrapment in local minima, inaccessibility of target positions, and path oscillations. To ensure path feasibility, the algorithm incorporates a failure detection mechanism that triggers localized RRT-based path replanning when APF fails, thereby enhancing target reachability. Additionally, a path smoothing process is applied to improve the overall trajectory quality. Simulation experiments conducted on a small-scale balance beam model with embedded obstacles demonstrate that the proposed hybrid algorithm can effectively avoid collisions and generate smooth, feasible spraying trajectories, thereby overcoming the drawbacks of traditional approaches. This work offers a robust path planning strategy and valuable technical reference for enhancing the intelligence and safety of robotic spraying operations.

title: Research on a Multi-Source Heterogeneous Data Verification Scheme for Substation Monitoring Systems
authors: Yuwen Xia, Zhaoguo Li, Hualiang Zhou, Jingrong Zheng, Zhexue Zou, Zebin Xu, Shengjie Xu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: During the renovation and expansion of substations, many changes occur in secondary system configuration data, making consistency checking and reasonableness verification of database modifications especially critical. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a multi-source heterogeneous data verification scheme tailored for substation monitoring systems. The scheme integrates multimodal data access and standardized adaptation technology, progressive data consistency checking strategies, a configuration change reasonableness verification mechanism based on power graph, and multidimensional semantic result presentation techniques, thereby establishing an efficient and precise data verification framework. The proposed scheme has been validated through practical applications including substation expansion projects, domestic database adaptation, and remote terminal unit (RTU) configuration downloads. Both construction efficiency and system operational reliability have been significantly improved.

title: Enhancing Reliability of Error-Related Potential Decoding for Multi-Agent Systems: An Offline Feasibility Study using Uncertainty Quantification
authors: Seong-Ho Ahn, Sungu Nam, Kyungyoon Jung, Pyeong Soo Kim, Sang Jin Jang, Sulgi Kim, Sangwan Lee, Jaeseung Jeong
abstract: Error-related potential (ErrP) serves as neural signatures of human error perception, offering advantages for implicit feedback to enhance reinforcement learning agents. However, the practical implementation of ErrP-based feedback in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) environments is challenged by the noise of EEG and the misclassified feedback. These limitations pose risks to the complex collaborative mechanisms of MARL. To address these limitations, we propose the application of Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) to ErrP decoding to enhance reliability. We develop UQ-EEGNet, a model capable of quantifying both Aleatoric Uncertainty to mitigate the influence of noisy data in training and Epistemic Uncertainty to identify distributional shifts induced by severe artifacts. Our results demonstrate that UQ-EEGNet outperforms the baseline EEGNet, achieving an improvement of 0.0216 in accuracy and 0.0272 in F1-score, along with a reduction in Expected Calibration Error. Furthermore, UQ-EEGNet exhibits superior calibration, particularly in low-probability ranges, and maintains robustness against simulated noise conditions across various signal-to-noise ratios. These results demonstrate the feasibility of UQ for ErrP decoding with stimuli derived from multi-agent scenarios, indicating that incorporating UQ effectively mitigates the risks of noisy predictions.

title: Improved Pied Kingfisher Optimization for Wireless Power Transfer Parameter Tuning
authors: Mengjiang Wu, Ying Jiang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: The Pied Kingfisher optimization algorithm is a novel single-objective meta-heuristic algorithm, which has excellent global search ability and fast convergence. Single-objective optimization algorithms are difficult to handle the trade-offs among multiple objectives, which limits their application in complex real-world decision-making scenarios.
