title: Dynamic Service-Network Coordination: A Real-Time Optimization Framework for Live Streaming
authors: Qimiao Zeng, Wei Quan, Yirong Zhuang, Qing Pan, Qi Chen, Jinxia Hai, Yuming Zhang, Xue Zhang
abstract: The rapid growth of mobile-based global live streaming services has intensified the challenge of delivering high-quality, real-time video transmission, particularly due to the mismatch between diverse network requirements and rigid resource adaptation strategies. Traditional multipath transmission approaches often lack the dynamic adaptability needed to support service diversity, with isolated path selection leading to resource contention and static or coarse-grained allocation mechanisms failing to respond promptly to network changes. These limitations negatively impact both service quality and overall resource utilization. To address these challenges, this paper presents a flexible real-time service transmission system. Specifically, the system introduces a service diversity-aware mechanism coupled with collaborative optimization of network resource paths, enabling high-quality differentiated transmission services. Furthermore, a dynamic routing optimization algorithm is proposed, leveraging In-band Network Telemetry (INT) technology to monitor network resources in real time and flexibly generate optimal routing strategies. We conducted long-distance (over 2000 kilometers) cross-domain testing in real-world large-scale Information Service Providers (ISPs) networks. Experimental results demonstrate that our solution can operate effectively in existing solutions while outperforming current approaches in terms of Quality of Service (QoS).

title: A Spatio-Temporal Hierarchical Diffusion Framework for Training-Free Perceptual Remote Sensing Image Compression
authors: Yangxuan Cheng, Fanyang Meng, Hao Qi, Han Shen, Zhongqiang Zhang, Ye Wang, Yongsheng Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote sensing data contains vast amounts of geographic structural information and fine-grained textural details, making compression essential and necessitating efficient processing for downstream interpretation tasks. Generative models have shown great potential in learned image compression, offering high compression rates and exceptional generation quality. Among these, diffusion models stand out for their ability to produce high-quality reconstructions. However, they may hallucinate small structures and textures due to significant information loss at limited bitrates. Furthermore, compression based on the diffusion process suffers from long inference times caused by numerous denoising steps, which hinders practical deployment. We propose Spatio-Temporal Hierarchical Codebook Diffusion (STHCD), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates resources to align with this hierarchy. STHCD introduces three synergistic components: (1) Spatial Residual Transformation via parameter-free unshuffle operations to adapt spatial granularity; (2) Channel-wise Codebook Splitting for flexible bit allocation without memory explosion; and (3) a Time-Dependent Hierarchical Policy to orchestrate these operations across timesteps. Extensive experiments on nine datasets show that STHCD significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in perceptual metrics (LPIPS, DISTS, FID). At equivalent bitrates (∼0.12 bpp), STHCD achieves an 8∼12.5× speedup (80∼125 vs. 1000 steps) over standard temporal codebook diffusion while optimizing LPIPS by 29.5%. Furthermore, STHCD can achieve a remarkable 15-step diffusion at the same bitrate, with only a minor LPIPS drop, establishing a new paradigm for hierarchical remote sensing compression. Our project is available at https://sthcd.github.io.

title: Energy-Aware Load Balancing of Massive 10G-EPON OLTs on Multi-Node GPU Servers for Fully Virtualized Central Offices
authors: Takahiro Suzuki, Yasuhiro Matsumoto, Sang-Yuep Kim, Jun-Ichi Kani, Tomoaki Yoshida
abstract: With the increasing demand for diverse services in access networks, ranging from legacy services to next-generation applications, network function virtualization (NFV) is being applied to access networks to enable agile service creation and flexible function deployment. While the virtualization of upper-layer management and control functions of optical line terminals (OLTs) has progressed apace, maximum flexibility by extending virtualization to lower-layer functions, including media access control (MAC) and physical-layer (PHY) functions, has been demonstrated using graphic processing units (GPUs). With improved scalability, fully virtualized OLTs have been shown to consume 31.8 W of power per OLT, which is still high, and no work focus on the realization of efficient virtual OLTs. In this paper, we propose a novel energy-aware GPU load balancing method for fully virtualized OLTs, and evaluate it on a multi-node supercomputer by running significant numbers of OLTs. The proposed method reduces the power consumption of compute nodes by minimizing the number of GPUs used for OLT deployment to match the traffic volume. In an evaluation, we run 320 OLTs on a supercomputer with multiple nodes having multiple GPUs OLTs are allocated to GPUs as determined by the load balancer proposed herein. The results shows that, when traffic volume is small, the proposed method can reduce the power consumption by approximately 98 % while maintaining throughput performance compared to the conventional round-robin method, and can hold consumption to 0.594 W per OLT.

title: Near-End Listening Enhancement via Combined Noise-Dependent Spectro-Temporal Energy Reallocation
authors: Filippo Villani, Wai-Yip Chan, Zheng-Hua Tan, Jan Østergaard, Jesper Jensen
abstract: This paper addresses the near-end listening enhancement (NELE) problem, i.e., how to modify a clean speech signal prior to presentation in noise to improve perceptual aspects, particularly speech intelligibility. We introduce a general optimization problem that generalizes and extends those used in our previous NELE studies. The problem is concave and we derive its closed-form solution. Within this framework, we first revisit our previously proposed OptFractASII method, which redistributes speech energy across frequency to optimize a modified approximation of the Speech Intelligibility Index. We then derive a novel NELE method, TemporalASII, which reallocates energy across time frames to boost weak speech components. We propose to combine these methods into a cascaded system, ftASII, which redistributes speech energy across both time and frequency. Extensive objective speech intelligibility predictions and a subjective listening test show that ftASII outperforms state-of-the-art NELE methods. In particular, ftASII achieves intelligibility improvements from 5.4 dB to 10.0 dB in challenging real-world noise conditions.

title: Prediction and Explainability of School Dropout in Peru Using Machine Learning and Explainable Artificial Intelligence
authors: Andrée Barboza, Jhonatan Huamani, José Santisteban, Nelson Maculan, David Mauricio
abstract: School dropout represents a critical challenge for educational systems in developing countries. This study proposes an integral framework that combines machine learning techniques and explainable artificial intelligence to predict and explain school dropout in the Peruvian context. The proposal incorporates key variables in four dimensions: personal, family, sociodemographic, and academic. The study implements and compares five machine learning algorithms (Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, Gradient Boosting, K-Nearest Neighbors, and XGBoost) along with SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) for explainability, integrated into a web application called EduPredict. The experiments conducted on a dataset of 1,456 records from a public school in Metropolitan Lima, using 20 predictive factors, demonstrate exceptional performance, with Gradient Boosting achieving an accuracy of 97.27% and precision of 99.28%. The explainability analysis using SHAP identified behavior and academic performance as the most determining factors, significantly contributing to the dropout risk. The validation with 10 educational professionals through structured surveys revealed high levels of usability, utility, and satisfaction, confirming the practical applicability of the system in real educational environments. The framework has potential to be adapted to other educational contexts in developing countries, offering a personalized approach to mitigate school dropout.

title: Enhancing Synthesized Speech Detection Using Modified Group Delay and Self-Supervised Compact Convolutional Transformer
authors: Soulaiman Moualla, Oumayma Al Dakkak, Omar Hamdoun, Assef Jafar
abstract: The escalating sophistication of synthesized speech necessitates detection systems that target fundamental physiological constraints rather than surface artifacts. Our approach leverages Modified Group Delay (MGD) to capture the vocal tract characteristics that remain challenging for synthesizers to replicate. We then use this robust representation to train a Compact Convolutional Transformer (CCT) model via self-supervised learning. This training is based on masked prediction, inspired by models like Wav2Vec2, using an Information Noise-Contrastive Estimation (InfoNCE) loss to force the model to learn the local, deep, and contextual structure of real human speech. The trained CCT thus becomes a powerful encoder, producing discriminative embeddings. Finally, a simple supervised binary classifier is trained on top of these embeddings to distinguish between real and synthesized speech, creating a hybrid detection system resilient to novel and evolving synthetic threats. This method achieved state-of-the-art performance when evaluated on the ASVspoof 2019 LA dataset, with an Equal Error Rate (EER) of 0.0093%, thus performing a new benchmark without augmentation. Furthermore, it demonstrated superior generalization, with an EER of 0.61% on the more challenging ASVspoof 2021 LA dataset and excellent performance with an EER of 6.13% on the In-The-Wild dataset.

title: Sudoku: Refining DRAM Address Mapping through Advanced Timing and Configuration Analysis
authors: Minbok Wi, Wanju Doh, Seungmin Baek, Seonyong Park, Mattan Erez, Jung Ho Ahn
abstract: Understanding undocumented DRAM address-mapping functions is crucial for enabling well-known attacks, such as RowHammer, and for gaining deeper insights into memory system behavior. Prior software-based reverse-engineering approaches primarily rely on access latency differences caused by row-buffer conflicts to identify functions that distinguish DRAM banks. Other works aim to decompose DRAM address mappings into component-level details, either operating only on specific CPU platforms or being limited to identifying functions up to the rank level, without revealing finer-grained DRAM components. In this paper, we propose Sudoku, a software-only tool that automatically decomposes full DRAM address mappings into channel, rank, bank group, and bank functions. Sudoku first distinguishes channel-indexing functions by exploiting bandwidth differences across DRAM address-mapping functions. It then leverages two newly identified timing channels to decompose the remaining address-mapping functions: (1) refresh-interval-induced latency variations and (2) timing constraints arising from consecutive memory accesses. Together, these techniques enable Sudoku to refine DRAM address mappings into component-level functions, allowing it to identify not only banks but also finer-grained DRAM components that were previously difficult to distinguish. We evaluate Sudoku on recent Intel and AMD processors and demonstrate that it successfully decomposes DRAM address mappings into component-level functions.

title: Machine Learning Models for the Design and Optimization of Stack-Structured Permanent Magnet Thrust Bearings
authors: D. K. Supreeth, Siddappa I. Bekinal, Naveen Saini, Krishna Pratap Singh
abstract: This paper presents artificial neural network (ANN) models for the design and optimization of multi-ring permanent magnet thrust bearings (MRPMTB) based on results from three-dimensional (3D) mathematical models. To begin, a mathematical model is presented to calculate the axial force and stiffness of MRPMTB. Then, to understand the influence of bearing geometrical dimensions on its features, a single-ring thrust permanent magnet bearing (PMB) was optimized within a control volume to maximize axial force and stiffness, using modified MATLAB codes to solve the force and stiffness equations. Furthermore, based on the optimization results, comprehensive datasets of the optimal geometric dimensions of bearings for maximum axial force and stiffness within a control volume are generated using MATLAB codes. Five ANN models are developed to predict optimal geometric parameters and corresponding maximum force and stiffness values using generated datasets. Out of the applied ANN models, the Bayesian Multi-layer Perceptron gave higher values of R2 and lower mean absolute error (MAE). Finally, the ANN model results are validated using a finite element simulation tool (COMSOL Multiphysics).

title: LBCM: A Scalable and DDoS-resistant Cross-domain Authentication Protocol for IIoT using Chaotic Maps and Merkle Tree
authors: Yu Huang, Maode Ma, Hui Wang, Yuankun Xia, Yinjuan Shi
abstract: To overcome the challenges of trust isolation and scalability bottlenecks in cross-domain collaboration within the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT), this paper proposes LBCM, a lightweight authentication protocol based on consortium blockchain and Chebyshev chaotic mapping. The protocol establishes a hybrid on-chain storage and off-chain verification trust architecture, and leverages the semi-group property of Chebyshev polynomials to achieve efficient and secure mutual authentication and key agreement. To address the storage limitations of blockchain, we design a global Merkle Tree commitment mechanism governed by smart contracts. Acting as consortium nodes, Trusted Authorities collaboratively maintain the global Merkle Root through a Propose-Vote-Execute consensus process, thus fixing the on-chain storage overhead at a constant level (O(1)), regardless of network scale. Furthermore, the protocol incorporates a “Filter-First, Access-Later” proactive defense strategy, enabling core nodes to rapidly filter out malicious traffic at the edge and effectively defending against Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks. Formal verification using ProVerif and comprehensive performance evaluations demonstrate that LBCM significantly outperforms existing mainstream schemes in terms of computational efficiency, communication overhead, and storage cost, while maintaining high security guarantees.

title: Adversarial-Training-Based Robust Reinforcement Learning Control for Autonomous Vehicle Trajectory Tracking
authors: Donghyun Lee, Taeyoung Oh, Jinkyeom Kim, Jinwoo Yoo
abstract: Autonomous vehicles must maintain stable and accurate trajectory and speed tracking even in scenarios involving unpredictable disturbances and changing environmental conditions. Reinforcement learning (RL)–based controllers that directly output control inputs often exhibit performance degradation or instability when encountering out-of-distribution scenarios or disturbances. Model-based controllers are sensitive to model mismatch and parameter variations. Notably, even residual RL approaches offer limited robustness if disturbances are insufficiently represented during training. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an integrated tracking control architecture in which a linear quadratic regulator (LQR) serves as the nominal controller, while an RL policy provides residual compensation for steering and longitudinal (acceleration/deceleration) commands. To improve controller robustness, the training process is formulated as a min–max adversarial learning problem: The adversary learns a disturbance-injection policy while the controller minimizes tracking errors and ride-comfort penalties. Evaluation of the proposed method across a range of driving scenarios in the CARLA simulation environment demonstrates that it reduces trajectory errors and constraint violations compared with pure LQR, model predictive control, and an existing RL-based PID controller, both in the presence and absence of disturbances. These results demonstrate the improved tracking stability and robustness of the proposed controller.

title: The Impact of Green Investment on Carbon Emission Intensity in a Dual-Carbon Context: An Empirical Study Based on Listed Companies
authors: Yao Liang
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 International Conference on Information Economy, Data Modeling and Cloud Computing
abstract: None

title: Impact of Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs) on Traditional Banking Systems
authors: S. Aravinth, K. Neelaveni, Mamillapalli Venu Akshay Kumar, A. Yasir Ahamed, S. Lokeesh, S. Kumaran
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs) have rapidly developed into new financial systems that now worry traditional banking operations. A mathematical model and simulation-based analysis determine how CBDCs affect liquidity management, credit supply, and deposit stability. Based on the proposed model there will be a 32% decrease in transaction fees while payment efficiency through border regions will improve by 28% to provide instant settlement instead of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{T}+\mathbf{2}$</tex> days. Analysis of financial inclusion demonstrates how the rate of banking access for unbanked people has elevated by 45%. The analysis based on deposit shift modeling indicates that traditional bank deposits will decrease by 12% so banks must develop strategies for managing liquidity. The analysis establishes both the economic and operational effects of CBDCs and puts forward risk reduction methods. Financial efficiency improves through CBDC systems although specific regulations must be implemented to stop adverse impacts on traditional banking businesses.

title: Leveraging Artificial Intelligence and the Internet of Things for Sustainable Digital Transformation in Green Business Practices
authors: Shishir Rastogi, Padma, Saurabh Gupta, Ankit Agarwal, Parul Goyal, Sudheer S Marar
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Sustainable business operations need creative solutions that reduce the environmental footprint and maximize resource usage. The proposed model uses IoTenabled sensor networks for live tracking of energy use, emissions, and waste output, with predictive analytics powered by AI and RL for dynamic optimization of sustainability actions. Comparative evaluation against conventional approaches to sustainability using KPIs such as energy efficiency, reduction of waste, minimization of carbon footprint, and optimization of logistics is employed for model validation. The outcomes illustrate substantial improvements of up to 30% improvement in energy efficiency, 24% reduction in carbon footprint, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$25-40 \%$</tex> less waste, and 17 % improvement in logistics efficiency. These outcomes identify the efficiency of AI-IoT integration in autonomous management of sustainability, guaranteeing real-time adjustability and operating efficiency. The framework is extensible across sectors, offering self-learning sustainability solutions that allow enterprises to automate decisions, minimize expenses, and secure environmental compliance. This study makes its represent how AI and IoT can be combined to develop smart, data-based sustainability models for transform green business operations.

title: Neural Signatures of Emotion Processing in Native and Non-native Speech Imagery
authors: Hyuna Roh, Hyeong-Yeong Park, Se-Na Jang, Seong-Wook Kim, Ah-Hyun Kim, Ji-Hoon Jeong
abstract: This study investigates the neural signatures of emotion processing during speech imagery under native (L1) and non-native (L2) language conditions using electroencephalography (EEG). Participants performed a speech imagery (SI) task involving four valence-oriented emotion sentences, and power spectral density (PSD) features were extracted to compare full-spectrum and band-specific frequency representations. Three ma-chine learning models, including support vector machines (SVM), regularized linear discriminant analysis (RLDA), and gaussian naïve bayes(GNB), were applied to classify emotional valence. The results show that L1 processing exhibited higher performance with band-specific features, while L2 processing benefited from full-spectrum information, suggesting a more distributed neural activation pattern induced by increased cognitive load during L2 emotional processing. Alpha-band analysis further revealed language-dependent spatial differences, with L2 showing broader distributed activation across regions of interest. Channel expansion from base to extended electrode configurations did not yield substantial performance improvement in either condition, indicating that the core emotional information is localized or sufficiently captured by the base channels. These findings pro-vide insight into language-dependent affective processing during speech imagery and highlight the importance of spectral–spatial characteristics in bilingual neural decoding.

title: DLM-IDS: Leveraging LLM for Efficient IoT Intrusion Detection with Limited Training Data
authors: Liying Li, Mingyang Zhao, Zixuan Wang, Guyue Li, Bin Xiao
abstract: Artificial intelligence has demonstrated significant potential for traffic analysis in IoT intrusion detection systems. However, existing machine learning (ML) solutions struggle with high false alarm rates due to a lack of malicious data. The advantages of large language models (LLMs), particularly their few-shot learning capabilities, can effectively address this issue. Nonetheless, LLMs face challenges such as high computational overhead and detection latency, which make them impractical for IoT intrusion detection. One promising solution is to leverage knowledge distillation, shrinking the LLM, the teacher model, into a smaller student model that requires limited malicious examples while preserving the low latency characteristic of traditional ML-based models. In this paper, we propose DLM-IDS, an efficient and accurate traffic analysis framework for IoT intrusion detection with small training datasets, empowered by LLM knowledge distillation. Specifically, DLM-IDS introduces a chain-of-thought (CoT) mechanism that enables a pre-trained LLM to interpret network traffic patterns without requiring additional training or fine-tuning. By extracting rationales from the LLM (around 200B parameters), the teacher model, DLM-IDS constructs a small yet highly accurate student traffic analysis model (around 200 million parameters). Experiments show that with only 800 examples in the training dataset, DLM-IDS maintains over 98% AUC while reducing inference time from 3.2s to 0.037s per flow detection, enabling practical low-latency deployment on resource-constrained IoT devices.

title: Joint Optimization of UAV Deployment and Task Scheduling for Multi-UAV-assisted MEC System
authors: Jiaman He, Zheng Chang, Li Wang
abstract: Leveraging their inherent mobility and rapid deployment capabilities, Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) can deliver computing services to ground devices with enhanced speed and flexibility. Consequently, UAV-based Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) networks have garnered significant research interest and attention. However, in contrast to ground edge servers, UAVs functioning as computing nodes face a unique set of challenges. Their decision-making processes must not only address user computational demands but also concurrently consider critical factors such as energy consumption and optimal deployment strategies. To tackle these challenges, we introduce a multi-UAV deployment and cooperative scheduling strategy designed to comprehensively optimize deployment locations, task latency, overall energy consumption, and the load distribution across the UAV network. Specifically, we decompose the overarching optimization problem into two distinct sub-problems. For the deployment problem, we propose a clustering algorithm rooted in the modularity-improving Louvain method, aiming to minimize both mobile device transmission power and UAV energy consumption. Then, we present a reinforcement learning-based algorithm capable of making dynamic scheduling decisions that adapt to system fluctuations. Finally, we validate the practicality and effectiveness of our integrated deployment and scheduling scheme through rigorous experimental evaluation.

title: Failure Probability Calculation for $\pm$ 800 kV DC Line Arresters Considering Multiple Lightning Strikes
authors: Xu Li, Yuanzhi Wu, Yongcheng Guo, Leilei Ding, Yongxia Han, Chao Huang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm$</tex> 800 kV UHVDC transmission has become a pivotal technology for addressing the reverse distribution of energy resources and load centers in China. Lightning-caused tripping and arrester failures on these lines can directly lead to substantial load losses, severely compromising grid security and power supply reliability. Focusing on the risk of lightning-induced arrester failures along the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm$</tex> 800 kV Kunliulong UHVDC transmission line, this study first conducts a statistical analysis of lightning parameters in the traversed provinces of Guizhou and Guangdong. Province-specific calculation parameters are proposed, incorporating the proportion of multiple stroke flashes, probability distribution of lightning current amplitude, and cloud-to-ground lightning density. Subsequently, an electromagnetic transient simulation model of the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm$</tex> 800 kV DC transmission line is established, and a method for calculating the failure probability of line arresters considering multiple lightning strikes is developed. Finally, the influence of different lightning characteristics and terrain conditions on arrester failure probability is analyzed through simulation. The results demonstrate that while no failures occur in plain terrain, mountainous areas significantly increase the failure probability. Moreover, the failure probability in Guangdong Province, which experiences more intense lightning activity, is markedly higher than in Guizhou Province. Therefore, for regions with high lightning activity and mountainous terrain, it is recommended to enhance tower protection measures to reduce the failure probability or include multiple lightning strike conditions in the arrester selection criteria to ensure the safe and stable operation of the transmission line.

title: eMARL: Efficient Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Energy Saving in RAN
authors: Jessica Moysen Cortes, Euhanna Ghadimi, Stavroula Vassaki
abstract: Large-scale coordination between clusters of distributed base stations presents a significant challenge for performance optimization in future Radio Access Networks (RANs). We propose a scalable and efficient learning framework for energy saving in RANs, leveraging Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) and reasoning-based techniques. The proposed framework scales effectively to enable coordination across hundreds of cells and large clusters of base stations, while enduring policy generalization across diverse RAN conditions. Additionally, we integrate reasoning-based approaches within the online RL training process, reducing complexity and accelerating convergence for more efficient learning. We validate our solution’s effectiveness through 5G-compliant system-level simulations, showing significant energy savings with minimal impact on user experience rates.

title:
Modeling and Simulation of a Low-Energy CO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Liquefaction Process for Marine Applications
authors: Bingqi Ma, Jingyu Zhao, Qiuyan Chen
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: In response to rising CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> emissions from maritime transport, increasing focus has been placed on implementing carbon capture and storage (CCS) solutions aboard ships. This study conducts a comparative simulation analysis of three cryogenic CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> liquefaction methods designed to meet final liquefaction conditions of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-49^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex> and 7 bar: high-pressure compression with throttling, compression coupled with expansion, and a hybrid compression-liquefaction-throttling approach. Process simulations were carried out using Aspen HYSYS to evaluate energy performance across the schemes. Results show that the throttling-based liquefaction route consumes excessive energy, limiting its applicability in marine scenarios. For the remaining two schemes, system energy demand is largely governed by the interaction between compressor input and refrigeration system effectiveness, as reflected by the coefficient of performance (COP). When COP <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\geq 0.7$</tex>, the compression-expansion route demonstrates superior energy efficiency at lower pressures. The hybrid strategy achieves its lowest energy consumption of 273 kW at 14.4 bar and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex>. These results support the development of compact, low-energy liquefaction solutions for maritime CCS applications.

title: MLPilot: An Intelligent CI/CD Flight Controller for Auto-Tuning Resource Allocation in MLOps Kubernetes Pipelines
authors: Radhakrishnan Krishna Kripa
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advances in Data-Driven Analytics And Intelligent Systems (ADACIS)
abstract: The growing complexity of machine learning (ML) workflows, combined with the scale of continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipelines, has placed increasing pressure on infrastructure efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and deployment reliability. Traditional Kubernetes-based resource schedulers, while robust for stateless services, fall short when orchestrating dynamic, compute-intensive ML workloads. In this paper, we introduce MLPilot, a novel CI/CD-aware flight controller designed to intelligently auto-tune resource allocation across MLOps pipelines in Kubernetes environments. MLPilot integrates seamlessly with existing CI/CD tools and leverages a telemetry-driven deep reinforcement learning engine to dynamically adjust compute, memory, and GPU configurations across pipeline stages. Our system architecture includes a Kubernetes-native operator, real-time telemetry aggregator, and a learning-based policy optimizer that jointly minimize latency, reduce infrastructure cost, and enhance deployment stability. Through extensive experiments on real-world ML workloads—including NLP, computer vision, and tabular models—we demonstrate that MLPilot achieves up to 32% faster pipeline execution, 27% lower resource cost, and 20% higher GPU utilization compared to state-of-the-art baselines. These results highlight the importance of intelligent, learning-based orchestration in future MLOps deployments. Our current evaluation is on a single 3-node cluster and does not yet enforce formal safety constraints for multi-tenant isolation; we outline these as priorities for future work.

title: A Comparative Study Of Classical And Deep Learning Models For Facial Emotion Recognition On The CK+ Dataset
authors: Imane Aaniba, Hajar Chouhayebi, Nawal Alioua, Othmane Alaoui Fdili, Abdesadik Bendarag
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advances in Data-Driven Analytics And Intelligent Systems (ADACIS)
abstract: In this study, we present a comparative analysis of three learning models: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Support Vector Machines (SVM), and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for facial emotion recognition on the CK+ dataset. To ensure a fair comparison, we implement a harmonized experimental pipeline where each model uses facial regions extracted by the Dlib library and resized to 48×48 pixels. The classical models (KNN and SVM) rely on Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) features, while the CNN learns features directly from grayscale face images. Simulation results show that CNN achieves the best accuracy (98.74%), but requires (539.34s) and 0.14s for training and inference times. SVM reaches similar performance with (98.53%) accuracy and much lower training (12.91s) and inference times (0.0028s). KNN is the fastest to set up, with (96.85%) accuracy, (17.08s) of training time, and (0.019s) for inference. These results highlight a trade-off between performance and efficiency: CNN offers the highest accuracy but is computationally expensive, SVM provides an excellent balance between accuracy and speed, while KNN remains a simple and quick option for computational and energy constrained systems.

title: Heterogeneous Federated Dynamic Graph HyperNetwork for Image Classification_supp1-3672375.pdf
authors: Liu Yang
abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaboration among distributed clients, but practical deployments often face heterogeneous models and non-IID data, leading to degraded communication and personalization. In addition, real-world FL systems frequently encounter newly joined clients that require rapid adaptation and abnormal clients that may upload corrupted updates, further exacerbating instability and hindering global convergence. To address these challenges in image classification, we propose HFedDGHN, a Heterogeneous Federated Dynamic Graph HyperNetwork that jointly models inter-client relations and personalized parameter generation. Specifically, a graph structure learner adaptively captures client correlations to construct a dynamic collaboration graph, while a graph-convolutional hypernetwork generates model parameters for heterogeneous architectures, enabling implicit knowledge transfer without sharing local data or weights. Moreover, the framework naturally supports meta-learning-based generalization, allowing efficient adaptation to newly joined clients. Furthermore, the dynamic graph enhances robustness by isolating abnormal clients, as they tend to be excluded from most neighborhoods during adaptive graph construction. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that HFed-DGHN achieves superior accuracy compared to state-of-the-art personalized and heterogeneous FL methods, while naturally improving robustness and scalability in real-world deployments.

title: Progressive NeRF Training With Dynamic Frequency Allocation for Sparse-View Synthesis
authors: Meng Pan, Xiaohua Xie
abstract: NeRF's performance degrades under sparse view conditions due to overfitting to limited input data. Recent works have alleviated this issue by linearly expanding the visible frequency bands of NeRF's inputs as training progresses, which means they allocate equal time to low frequency and high frequency components. However, we observe that learning low frequency components requires less training time, whereas learning high frequency components demands more. Based on this insight, we propose a Dynamic Position Encoding (DPE) mask that allocates additional optimization time to higher frequency bands as they are gradually activated. To further improve the efficiency of NeRF training, we introduce a Multi-Stage Training (MST) paradigm: we generate four RGB image scales via three <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> downsamplings of training images and NeRF is optimized from the lowest resolution to the original resolution in the final stage for high rendering quality. Extensive experiments on the LLFF and DTU benchmarks demonstrate that our approach not only synthesizes high-fidelity renderings with only three input views but also achieves a remarkable <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3.1\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> reduction in training time.

title: Research on Primary Frequency Response in Wind-Thermal Systems Using Hybrid Energy Storage
authors: Jiaqi Zheng, Xiaoxue Wang, Yan Li, Shuyan Zeng, Chengjie Cao, Jianbin Fan, Han Li
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: In response to the issue caused by large-scale wind power integration, which leads to the replacement of traditional power sources, a reduction in system inertia, and a decrease in frequency regulation capabilities, severely affecting the safe and stable operation of the power system, this paper proposes the construction of a hybrid energy storage system consisting of flywheel energy storage and electrochemical energy storage, applied in primary frequency response in a wind-thermal system. First, a power system frequency regulation model is established, taking into account wind power penetration. Then, a first-order inertia model and virtual droop control are used, combined with the state of charge and Logistic regression function, to build a hybrid energy storage model involving both flywheel energy storage and electrochemical energy storage. Finally, a frequency fluctuation simulation model under wind power penetration is completed, verifying that the proposed method has both inertia response and primary frequency response capabilities, effectively alleviating wind power overshoot phenomena and improving the stability of the power system.

title: Ultra-Fast and Energy-Efficient Channel Estimation for Massive MIMO-OFDM Systems with Memristor Crossbar Based In-Memory Computing
authors: Yi-Hang Ren, Shaoshi Yang, Zi-Hao Xiong, Yu-Xin Zhang, Jia-Hui Bi, Sheng Chen
abstract: Massive multi-input multi-output (MIMO) signal processing algorithms heavily rely on high-dimension matrix operations, which impose excessively high computational complexity. Moreover, in the post-Moore era, the performance of the classical von Neumann computing architecture is facing severe limitations. The in-memory computing (IMC) technique holds the potential to break the memory wall and enhance the circuit’s energy efficiency. In this paper, we present an memristor crossbar based IMC circuit design for performing the classical least square (LS) channel estimation with high computation parallelism. Simulation results demonstrate that even when considering the writing and reading errors, the mean square error (MSE) of the proposed circuit with 7-bit memristor is almost the same as that achieved by the digital computer. Moreover, the proposed circuit achieves the same level of computing performance as the NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada Generation, but with about 1/18 times as low computation time and about 25 times as high energy efficiency, as this benchmark commercial processor.

title: Multi-Objective Route Optimization via Dueling DQN for Teleoperated Driving
authors: Iskander Zellagui, Sidi-Mohammed Senouci, Inès El Korbi, Shajjad Hossain
abstract: This paper presents a new approach to route optimization for teleoperated driving systems, addressing the multi-objective challenge of simultaneously minimizing travel distance, network latency, and maximizing available bandwidth. We model a real-world urban environment as a graph with stochastic network parameters and employ a Dueling Deep Q-Network (Dueling DQN) architecture, chosen for its ability to decouple state value and action advantage estimation, making it effective for routing problems requiring evaluation of both position quality and transition decisions.Our approach achieves the highest combined performance score (1.3956), representing 3.4% and 4.3% improvements over Lowest Latency (1.3815) and Shortest Path (1.3380) algorithms respectively. This demonstrates effective multi-objective trade-off management: 56.9% KPI compliance (30ms latency, 20Mbps bandwidth) with minimal distance overhead (40 vs 30 spatial units). Single-objective approaches fail to balance requirements—Shortest Path achieves only 22.6% KPI compliance, while Highest Bandwidth produces impractically long routes (120 spatial units) despite 99.2% KPI rate.Results demonstrate our Dueling DQN’s effectiveness in dynamically balancing competing objectives, providing intelligent adaptation to changing network conditions—a capability traditional single-objective optimization approaches cannot achieve.

title: Research on an Unmanned Apple Picking Vehicle Based on Multi-Sensor Fusion
authors: Xiafeng Zhang, Xiwei Gao, Chang Liu, Hongchao Gao
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechanical Engineering, Intelligent Manufacturing, and Automation Technology (MEMAT)
abstract: As a major global producer of apples, China has developed an L4-level unmanned apple-picking vehicle utilizing multi-sensor fusion technology to address key challenges in apple harvesting. The power supply system for the environmental perception module was specifically emphasized in the design. The developed bionic flexible gripper reduces the damage rate during apple picking to just 3%.Based on the YOLO v8 model, data collection, annotation, and training were conducted, achieving a recognition accuracy of over 94% under varying lighting conditions, different fruit densities, and occlusions. The system efficiently and accurately identifies and detects apples of different sizes (large, small, and other types), providing a basis for automated sorting. The study also explored multi-sensor fusion methods for the unmanned picking and transport vehicle, mapping principles, mapping techniques, and evaluation criteria, significantly improving mapping quality and efficiency. Furthermore, algorithms for robotic arm grasping, intelligent transport, and sorting were developed, enabling intelligent picking and automated sorting with a success rate exceeding 94.8%. This meets the real-time requirements of complex orchards for simultaneous picking and sorting.

title: Stability Analysis of Single-Phase Current Source Rectifier with Power Decoupling Capability
authors: Huifang Gong, Yao Sun, Jianheng Lin, Haihong Wang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: Constant power loads (CPLs) present negative impedance characteristics that tend to cause instability. In this paper, the stability of the single-phase current source rectifier (SCSR) with power decoupling capability under CPL is investigated. To accurately capture harmonic coupling dynamics, the harmonic state space (HSS) model of the SCSR is first built. Based on eigenvalue analysis, the stability boundary concerning load power is determined. It is shown that the larger the load power is, the easier the system becomes unstable. By fully utilizing the power decoupling cell, an active damping compensation method is designed to improve the system's stability. The results are carried out to verify the effectiveness of the theory analysis.

title: Enhancing Payment Convenience with UWB: Long-Range Contactless Payments
authors: Joohyun Lee, Junyoung Choi, Jinwook Seo
abstract: The integration of ultra-wideband (UWB) technology into smartphones has raised expectations for numerous potential applications. Despite this potential, UWB-based services have not yet achieved widespread adoption. To address this gap, we propose a novel UWB-based mobile payment system. Conventional proximity payment methods, such as near field communication, require users to bring their payment device into close proximity with a terminal. In contrast, UWB enables meter-level ranging, thereby creating new opportunities for contactless transactions over greater distances. Our proposed system allows two independent merchant devices to simultaneously process long-range payments with their respective customer devices without interference. We demonstrate the feasibility of this approach through an implementation on commercial smartphones (Samsung Galaxy S23+ and S23 Ultra). Experiential results show that the UWB payment system reliably detects customer devices, performs ranging, and completes a transactions with an average latency of 792 ms. These findings highlight the potential for UWB-based payments to become a seamlessly integrated aspect of everyday consumer transactions in the future.

title: Improving Automatic Modulation Long-Tail Recognition with Class-balancing Diffusion Model
authors: Yu Li, Xiaoran Shi, Haoyue Tan, Feng Zhou
abstract: Automatic modulation recognition (AMR) is critical in modern wireless communication systems and cognitive radio applications. Deep learning (DL) methods have become main-stream for AMR, achieving remarkable performance on balanced datasets. However, practical scenarios commonly exhibit long-tailed distributions, where abundant samples exist for head modulation types but middle and tail types suffer from severe scarcity. Such imbalance biases standard DL models towards the head classes, significantly compromising their effectiveness on tail classes. Current signal balance strategies are limited by domain-specific knowledge and insufficient sample diversity. To address these challenges, this paper proposes DiffuMLR, a novel class-dependent label information attenuation diffusion model for long-tailed modulation recognition. Specifically, we propose a label decay mechanism within a diffusion probabilistic model, dynamically adjusting label contributions during the reverse denoising process. DiffuMLR ensures semantic invariance of tail class samples while enhancing sample diversity. Extensive experiments conducted on real-world and public datasets with varying imbalance factors demonstrate significant improvements in recognition accuracy under long-tailed conditions. DiffuMLR can seamlessly integrate with existing AMR architectures and enhance AMR model robustness in realistic wireless communication scenarios.

title: A Modular Network Digital Twin for Radio Coverage Prediction: From Theory to Practice
authors: Ayat Zaki-Hindi, Jean-Sébastien Sottet, Sumit Kumar, Ion Turcanu, Sébastien Faye
abstract: Network Digital Twins (NDTs) offer a structured framework for modeling, predicting, and optimizing wireless networks. This paper presents a modular NDT implementation based on the GreyCat platform, integrating graph-based data models and external functional algorithms for indoor radio coverage prediction. For the first time, we implement an NDT system aligned with ITU-T Recommendation Y.3090, covering both basic and functional model instantiation from modular and interoperable abstract structures. We generated a practical dataset using a software-defined radio (SDR)-based OpenAirInterface5G setup, with a gNB and commercial UE deployed in a controlled environment. This real-world dataset was used to benchmark Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models for predicting RSRP-based radio coverage. Our results show that CNN outperforms GPR in under-sampled conditions, and we demonstrate how the modular architecture supports flexible model integration and benchmarking. This work represents a significant step toward practical, data-driven NDT deployments for wireless systems.

title: Efficient Federated Learning of Mixed-Token Transformers for Cellular Feature Prediction
authors: Daniel Becking, Jost Arndt, Ingo Friese, Karsten Müller, Jackie Ma, Thomas Buchholz, Mandy Galkow-Schneider, Wojciech Samek, Detlev Marpe
abstract: In telecommunications, Autonomous Networks (ANs) automatically adjust configurations based on specific requirements (e.g., bandwidth) and available resources. These networks rely on continuous monitoring and intelligent mechanisms for self-optimization, self-repair, and self-protection, nowa-days enhanced by Neural Networks (NNs) to enable predictive modeling and pattern recognition.In this work, we propose a neural architecture called Mixed-Token Transformer (MTT) that can process and predict both numerical and textual tokens representing various mobile network features (e.g., ping, SNR or band frequency). We train the models using a Federated Learning (FL) approach, enabling multiple AN cells — each equipped with an MTT — to collaboratively learn from cellular data while preserving data privacy.Since FL involves frequent transmission of neural network updates, it requires an efficient, standardized compression strategy for reliable communication. To address this, we investigate NNCodec, our implementation of the ISO/IEC Neural Network Coding (NNC) standard. Our experimental results on the Berlin V2X dataset demonstrate that NNCodec achieves transparent compression (i.e., negligible performance loss) while reducing communication overhead to below 1%, showing the effectiveness of combining NNC with FL in collaboratively learned autonomous mobile networks. Our proposed MTT architecture speeds up model inference by ≈ 5×. Code is available at: github.com/Jostarndt/NNCodec-FL-MixedTokenTransformer.

title: Energy-aware Multicast, Multi-channel Supports For Over-The-Air Software Update in Component-based IoT Networks
authors: Minh Hai Dao, Kim Khoa Nguyen
abstract: Over-The-Air Programming (OTA) is an emerging framework for updating IoT devices deployed at scale and in remote areas, mitigating the need for manual intervention post-deployment. Prior research focused mainly on optimizing the update order of software components in memory to achieve energy efficiency. So far, the literature has not yet considered the major amount of energy consumed by data transmission during the update process. In this paper, we propose using multicast, multi-radio, and multi-channel communications for supporting OTA updates in component-based IoT networks, in addition to an optimized memory replacement schedule for each IoT device. We formulate an optimization model named joint multicast multi-radio multi-channel software update (M3U), considering both the data dissemination process and software replacement in memory. This model minimizes the overall energy consumption across all devices during updates. Since M3U is a combinatorial optimization problem, which is NP-hard, we propose a linearization, a relaxation, and a heuristic algorithm to find a near-optimal solution with a much lower computational time. Our experimental results demonstrate that the proposed solution outperforms prior works and approximates the optimal solution (i.e., with only 2% difference).

title: Antennas in Walls: Performance Analysis of Microstrip Patch Antennas Designed for Internet of Paint (IoP)
authors: L. T. Wedage, Mehmet C. Vuran, Bernard Butler, Christos Argyropoulos, Sasitharan Balasubramaniam
abstract: This study presents a simulated transceiver with a microstrip patch antenna (MPA) designed to resonate at 150 GHz and embedded in paint. The in-paint MPA (IP-MPA) is designed for the Internet of Paint (IoP) paradigm, which envisions seamless device communication through a paint layer on walls. This study introduces a comprehensive channel model for transceivers in paint at arbitrary depths and IP-MPA orientations. The best antenna orientations are analyzed for IoP channel performance. Extensive simulations indicate that the lateral waves, which propagate along the air-paint interface, exhibit the lowest loss, making this path the most reliable for communication between transceivers in paint. Furthermore, the maximum received power for each propagation path, except for the direct path, depends on depth. The findings suggest that the proposed network of IP-MPA-enabled transceivers for IoP has the potential to transform conventional walls into an integrated high-speed wireless communication and sensing infrastructure.

title: A Zero-Touch O-RAN Framework for Federated Few-Shot IDS with LLM-Oracle Verification
authors: Abdullatif Albaseer, Moqbel Hamood, Raeed Al-Sabri, Mohamed Abdallah, Ala Al-Fuqaha
abstract: This paper presents FFS-ORAN-IDS, a federated few-shot intrusion-detection framework that secures streaming traffic in Open Radio Access Networks (O-RAN) while respecting their stringent latency and resource constraints. The framework addresses the twin challenges of scarce attack labels and heterogeneous data, where naïve pseudo-label injection without sufficient confidence propagates errors, large-scale labeling of streaming traffic is impractical, and inherent uncertainty often requires costly human intervention. FFS-ORAN-IDS combines three coordinated x-functional blocks: a confidence-adaptive curriculum that releases pseudo-labels only when local TabTransformers are reliable, a diversity filter that retains the most informative uncertain packets, and a token-budgeted large-language-model (LLM) oracle that verifies the remaining hard samples. A mixed-integer optimization jointly governs curriculum pacing, sampling size, and Oracle LLM calls so that each federated round minimizes detection loss, propagation error, and LLM token cost under per-round resource caps. We train FFS-ORAN-IDS in two stages: an initial few-shot phase that fits the TabTransformer on the scarce ground-truth packets, followed by iterative rounds that refine the model with oracle-verified pseudo-labels. Experimental evaluation on the CIC-IDS 2018 benchmark shows that the proposed framework improves detection accuracy by 6%, reduces label-error propagation by 20%, and lowers energy consumption by 40% in the most label-constrained scenarios.

title: Analysis of Negative Damping Characteristics in Scenarios Including Oscillatory Components
authors: Honglu Xu, Dianqing Zhang, Caiyun Gao, Zhaoyang Li
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: Conventional approaches predominantly concentrate on assessing stability within particular frequency ranges or oscillation patterns, lacking a comprehensive explanation of negative damping traits across the full oscillation frequency spectrum. Hence, we propose a methodology for analyzing negative damping characteristics that takes into account scenarios involving oscillatory components. Firstly, a detailed small-signal model of the primary circuit and control links is formulated, accounting for the interplay between oscillatory and fundamental frequency components. Based on this, an impedance model incorporating oscillatory elements is constructed to precisely depict the impedance behavior under the interaction between the renewable energy converter and the power grid. Finally, a system model is developed via an experimental simulation platform to analyze the impact of superimposed oscillatory components across various frequency bands on negative damping performance.

title: Analyzing User Resistance in Adoption of Robo-Advisory Services
authors: Rashmy Moray, Pratap Kakde, Priya, Ravjyot Kaur Chhabra, Sradha Sajeev
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Digitalization has gained momentum particularly within the financial services industry with the advent of roboadvisory services which is an automated, algorithm-based provision of financial planning services. In spite of the significant benefits these services can provide, the use of the robo-advisory services is still confined, especially in emerging markets. This paper aims to explain why customers hesitant to use roboadvisory platforms based on the theoretical concept of Innovation Resistance Theory (IRT). Primary data is collected using a structured questionnaire to reach various groups of the demographic respondents. The findings imply that the usage barriers, perceived risk, and image-related issues are key elements of resistance, whereas tradition and the absence of perceived value are also demonstrated resistance. The results indicate that IRT can be applied to understand consumer reluctance in relation to fintech innovations, i.e., robo-advisory services. The insights can be used by fintech service providers that will help minimize resistance and boost adoption levels.

title: Decentralized Service Negotiation for Fine-Grained Resource Elasticity in Cross-Domain Networks
authors: Qing Pan, Qimiao Zeng, Yirong Zhuang, Qi Chen, Mingjiang Fu, Wei Quan
abstract: Large-scale distributed computing is being widely adopted, and data transmission requirements between cloud and edge computing are becoming increasingly complex and diverse. Achieving efficient cross-domain data transmission demands both high-bandwidth network capabilities and real-time responses to changing service needs. However, existing network solutions are often limited to specific network domains, failing to effectively interact between service demands and network resources. This is particularly evident in cross-domain network transmission scenarios, where traditional network architectures lag in responding to service needs, and there is insufficient information flow sharing between different domains. This complicates the management of the cross-domain network. To effectively address these challenges, this study proposes an innovative decentralized cross-domain network framework based on service negotiation. This framework utilizes real-time communication interface schemes to exchange information between various network domains and negotiate and schedule inter-domain network resources within predetermined data channels. Using optimized dynamic resource allocation strategies, this framework provides elastic user-level adjustments to network resources, allowing rapid adaptation to changes in network resources and improving overall utilization efficiency of network resources.

title: Capacity Optimization Based Complementary Power Generation Method for Fluctuating Photovoltaic CSP
authors: Hongna Zhang, Shuaimin Hou
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: In order to reasonably optimize the capacity allocation of the power generation system and suppress the fluctuation of output, a capacity optimization allocation method of photovoltaic solar thermal composite complementary power generation system considering volatility was designed. According to the operation mechanism of photovoltaic solar thermal composite complementary power generation system, the optimal operation strategy of power generation system considering the forward-looking effect is designed to suppress the short-term output fluctuation value of the power generation system. Based on the minimum penalty loss and heat storage loss of short-term output fluctuation exceeding the upper and lower limits, the maximum renewable energy penetration rate is the objective function, and the heat storage balance of charge and discharge heat compensation and the complementary characteristics of photovoltaic solar thermal composite are used as constraints, and the capacity optimization allocation model of power generation system is established. The particle swarm optimization algorithm is used to solve the model to obtain the optimal capacity optimal configuration. Experiments show that this method can effectively obtain the optimal capacity allocation, suppress the short-term output fluctuation value of the power generation system, and improve the heat storage state. When the forward-looking period is 5 hours, the method has the best effect on suppressing short-term output fluctuations.

title: Dual-Mapping Sparse Vector Transmission for Short Packet URLLC
authors: Yanfeng Zhang, Xu Zhu, Jinkai Zheng, Weiwei Yang, Xianhua Yu, Haiyong Zeng, Yujie Liu, Yong Liang Guan
abstract: Sparse vector coding (SVC) is a promising short-packet transmission method for ultra reliable low latency communication (URLLC) in next generation communication systems. In this paper, a dual-mapping SVC (DM-SVC) based short packet transmission scheme is proposed to further enhance the transmission performance of SVC. The core idea behind the proposed scheme lies in mapping the transmitted information bits onto sparse vectors via block and single-element sparse mappings. The block sparse mapping pattern is able to concentrate the transmit power in a small number of non-zero blocks thus improving the decoding accuracy, while the single-element sparse mapping pattern ensures that the code length does not increase dramatically with the number of transmitted information bits. At the receiver, a two-stage decoding algorithm is proposed to sequentially identify non-zero block indexes and single-element non-zero indexes. Extensive simulation results verify that proposed DM-SVC scheme outperforms the existing SVC schemes in terms of block error rate and spectral efficiency.

title: A Location-Based Resource Selection Scheme for Periodic Message Delivery in NR-V2X Networks
authors: Yirong Pan, Bingying Wang, Jun Zheng
abstract: This paper proposes a novel location-based resource selection (LoRS) scheme for periodic message delivery in an NR-V2X network. The proposed LoRS scheme utilizes vehicles’ geographic location information to generate indexes for vehicles and makes resource selection for the vehicles based on the indexes. In particular, it introduces two novel feedback mechanisms to alleviate resource conflicts caused by the hidden-node problem and half-duplex transmission constraint. A packet reception status feedback mechanism is designed to avoid packet collisions and an index update conflict feedback mechanism is designed to avoid vehicle indexing errors and unnecessary index updates. Simulation results show that compared to the SB-SPS scheme, the LoRS scheme can significantly improve the packet delivery performance of the network in terms of the average packet reception rate (PRR) in the network.

title: Intelligent Cross-Layer Congestion Control for Dynamic mmWave Networks with High Throughput and Fairness
authors: Qianqian Pan, Akihiro Nakao
abstract: Millimeter-wave (mmWave) communications provide ultra-wide transmission bandwidth and ultra-high data rates for 5G and beyond networks. However, the high propagation loss and high transmission frequency make mmWave susceptible to blocking, frequent line-of-sight (LOS)/non-line-of-sight (NLOS) switching, and huge fluctuations in transmission quality. Existing congestion control algorithms face two main challenges in mmWave environments: (1) They are incapable of distinguishing whether a loss is caused by congestion or by channel degradation, and (2) they are vulnerable to the huge throughput fluctuations and frequent LOS/NLOS switches in mmWave links. These challenges cause frequent congestion window initialization and retransmission, thereby degrading the transmission performance in mmWave communication. To solve this issue, we propose an intelligent cross-layer congestion control approach for high-bandwidth and high-loss mmWave links. First, we design a smart cross-layer LOS/NLOS classifier to distinguish LOS and NLOS links based on physical-transport layer features. Second, we propose a deep reinforcement learning-driven online environment evaluation method to decide current transmission conditions. Third, we devise an adaptive congestion control algorithm to determine strategies based on LOS/NLOS detection and transmission condition decision. Finally, experimental results show our proposed method improves transmission performance while guaranteeing fairness.

title: End-to-end Neural-Classical Concatenated Feedback Coding with OFDM Guidance
authors: Adnan Can Buyuksirin, Hun-Seok Kim
abstract: In this paper, we propose an end-to-end (E2E) optimized concatenated feedback coding scheme that combines a neural inner encoder/decoder with a classical low-density parity-check (LDPC) outer code for reliable feedback-based communication over multipath fading channels. A key contribution of this work is the inclusion of the LDPC decoder within the training loop by exploiting the differentiability of the sum-product algorithm (SPA), allowing gradient-based optimization to flow across the entire feedback-based communication chain. To enhance robustness over multipath fading channels, we integrate orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) guidance as well as the bit-interleaved coded modulation (BICM) paradigm into the proposed concatenated feedback coding approach. The proposed framework supports practical blocklengths and fading scenarios, significantly outperforming both fully neural and non-E2E concatenated baselines.

title: Study of Sliding Electrical Contact Pair Wear Based on Coupled Multi-Physics Simulation
authors: Xiangyu Du, Shaowei Liu, Xiaoquan Lu, Tianyou Zheng
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: Sliding electrical contact is complex frictional contact behavior widely found in rail transportation and other fields. The wear phenomenon in this process has drastic effects on the performance of the contact pair. The wear behavior of sliding electrical contact pair under electromagnetic force and thermal load is very different from the traditional mechanical wear. In this paper, the wear behavior of aluminum alloy-copper alloy contact pair is simulated based on the Archard's model, the wear volume and depth of the contact pair under interference fit are calculated, and the effects of electromagnetic force and temperature on the wear characteristics are discussed. The results of this paper can provide references for extending the service life.

title: Research on Application Technology of Smart Gateways in Distribution Station Rooms Based on Power Internet of Things
authors: Yufeng Zhang, Taikun Li, Baosong Deng, Haoran Bu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: In response to issues such as cumbersome device integration, manual configuration of network parameters and protocols by professionals, and low deployment efficiency in traditional power distribution room monitoring, this paper proposes a smart gateway solution based on the power Internet of Things. The solution is built on a four-tier collaborative architecture of “perception-edge-platform-application,” with its core innovation being a plug-and-play communication mechanism for RS485-based devices. By standardizing communication rules and automating the detection and configuration process, terminal devices can achieve zero-configuration data interaction without pre-burning network parameters such as addresses and baud rates once connected to the bus. This reduces device integration time from hours to minutes. The solution effectively breaks through the efficiency bottlenecks of traditional methods, enhancing the deployment and operational efficiency of monitoring systems.

title: Linear Receive Beamforming for Continuous-Aperture Array (CAPA) Systems
authors: Chongjun Ouyang, Zhaolin Wang, Xingqi Zhang, Yuanwei Liu
abstract: The performance of linear receive beamforming in continuous-aperture array (CAPA)-based uplink communications is investigated. Three continuous beamforming strategies are proposed based on the principles of maximum-ratio combining (MRC), zero-forcing (ZF), and maximum signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) (i.e., optimal beamforming). For MRC beamforming, closed-form expressions for both the beamformer and the achievable sum-rate are derived. For ZF beamforming, a closed-form solution is developed using channel correlation to effectively eliminate inter-user interference. For optimal beamforming, a closed-form beamformer is obtained by solving an operator-based Rayleigh quotient maximization problem, and the associated achievable sum-rate is characterized. Numerical results confirm that CAPAs outperform traditional spatially-discrete arrays (SPDAs), achieving superior sum-rate performance under all three beamforming schemes.

title: Routing and Wavelength Assignment with Minimal Attack Radius for QKD Networks
authors: Mengyao Li, Qiaolun Zhang, Zongshuai Yang, Stefano Bregni, Alberto Gatto, Raouf Boutaba, Massimo Tornatore
abstract: Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) can distribute keys with guaranteed security but remains susceptible to key exchange interruption due to physical-layer threats, such as high-power jamming attacks. To address this challenge, we first introduce a novel metric, namely Maximum Number of Affected Requests (maxNAR), to quantify the worst-case impact of a single physical-layer attack, and then we investigate a new problem of Routing and Wavelength Assignment with Minimal Attack Radius (RWA-MAR). We formulate the problem using an Integer Linear Programming (ILP) model and propose a scalable heuristic to efficiently minimize maxNAR. Our approach incorporates key caching through Quantum Key Pools (QKPs) to enhance resilience and optimize resource utilization. Moreover, we model the impact of different QKD network architectures, employing Optical Bypass (OB) for optical switching of quantum channels and Trusted Relay (TR) for secure key forwarding. Moreover, a tunable parameter is designed in the heuristic to guide the preference for OB or TR, offering enhanced adaptability and dynamic control in diverse network scenarios. Simulation results show our method significantly outperforms the baseline in terms of security and scalability.

title: Tri-hybrid Beamforming Design with Reconfigurable Antennas
authors: Mengzhen Liu, Ming Li, Rang Liu, Qian Liu
abstract: Reconfigurable antennas can dynamically adjust their operating characteristics, thereby enhancing adaptability and performance. To fully exploit this flexibility in modern wireless communication systems, this paper proposes a novel tri-hybrid beamforming architecture that integrates pattern-reconfigurable antennas with analog and digital beamforming. The proposed tri-hybrid architecture operates across three layers: a radiation beamformer in the electromagnetic (EM) domain for dynamic pattern alignment, an analog beamformer in the radio-frequency (RF) domain for array gain enhancement, and a digital beamformer in the baseband (BB) domain for multi-user interference mitigation. First, a comprehensive mathematical model of the tri-hybrid beamforming system is developed to provide a theoretical foundation. Then, an optimization problem is formulated to maximize the sum-rate under practical constraints, and then is solved using fractional programming (FP), a penalty-based, majorization–minimization (MM), and manifold optimization methods. Simulation results validate the performance gains achieved by the proposed tri-hybrid architecture and highlight its potential for future wireless communication systems.

title: Toward Autonomous Solar Farms: A Deep Learning Framework for Real-Time Fault Detection and Predictive Maintenance
authors: Lotfi Ezzeddini, Tarek Frikha, Jalel Ktari
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advances in Data-Driven Analytics And Intelligent Systems (ADACIS)
abstract: The efficient and reliable operation of photovoltaic (PV) systems is essential for maximizing energy production and ensuring long-term sustainability. However, environmental and mechanical factors can lead to various panel defects, such as dust accumulation, physical dents, or surface degradation, significantly reducing system efficiency. Traditional inspection methods are often time-consuming, costly, and limited in scalability. To address these challenges, this study proposes an advanced automated detection system based on YOLOv11, the latest version of the YOLO object detection architecture, for the identification and classification of PV panel faults. Using a synthetic dataset containing three classes, clean_solar, dent_solar, and dust_solar, we trained and evaluated a YOLOv11m model, achieving exceptional performance with a mAP@50-95 of 0.978 and a recall of 0.975. The results demonstrate high precision across all defect types, particularly for dented panels (100% precision and recall). Beyond detection, we propose a post-detection decision module to trigger targeted maintenance interventions, such as automated cleaning alerts or critical repair notifications, based on the detected fault type and confidence level. This integrated approach enables predictive and optimized maintenance, reducing operational costs and downtime. The findings confirm that YOLOv11 is a robust and efficient solution for real-time, large-scale PV system monitoring, paving the way for intelligent, autonomous solar farm management.

title: Cellular-Connected UAV Path Planning on Radio Maps: LLMs Enhanced Approach
authors: Xuetong Pei, Fuyuan Ma, Shutong Wang, Zesheng Wang, Wenrui Ding, Yufeng Wang
abstract: In this paper, we propose a large language models (LLMs) enhanced path planning framework for a cellular-connected unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV), which can significantly improve the efficiency of path planning without sacrificing path quality. The ray tracing model is used to generate the radio map of UAV cellular connectivity from real-world geospatial data. By introducing quadtree-assisted representation, the radio map is formatted into prompts that can be understood by LLMs, bridging the gap between simplified obstacle environments and realistic electromagnetic environments. Meanwhile, a combined approach using LLMs and the graph-based search method is employed to find a path which can guarantee reliable connections between the UAV and its associated ground base station throughout the flight. Simulation results demonstrate that our proposed LLM-enhanced framework not only finds the path that guarantees communication quality, but also addresses computational and memory limitations of the conventional method, with a significant reduction in operations and storage.

title: Strengthening Public Health Governance Through Machine Learning-Based Prediction of Anemia in Children
authors: Narender Pal, Kamlesh Sharma
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: This paper explores the critical role of systematic data collection in shaping effective government policies. It provides detailed insights into diverse data sources, collection methodologies, preprocessing, and analysis techniques, while also addressing challenges related to data accuracy and reliability. Figures and tables are used to illustrate the contribution of data to policymaking. Through case studies in health, education, social welfare, and environmental sectors, the paper highlights the importance of robust data ecosystems. It concludes with key measures to strengthen data systems for more inclusive, transparent, and accountable governance.

title: Fractional Fourier Domain PAPR Reduction
authors: Yewen Cao, Yulin Shao, Rose Qingyang Hu
abstract: High peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR) has long posed a challenge for multi-carrier systems, impacting amplifier efficiency and overall system performance. This paper introduces dynamic angle fractional Fourier division multiplexing (DA-FrFDM), an innovative transmission framework that significantly reduces PAPR by dynamically selecting an optimal transform angle in the fractional Fourier domain. By exploiting the dual nature of PAPR across time and frequency domains, DA-FrFDM adaptively maps signals from an intermediate domain to time domain that minimizes peak fluctuations while preserving average power. A tailored optimization algorithm is developed to efficiently determine the angle that minimizes PAPR for each signal block. Simulation results demonstrate that DA-FrFDM outperforms state-of-the-art PAPR mitigation techniques such as clipping, selective mapping, and partial transmitted sequence, achieving superior PAPR reduction for both Gaussian and QAM signals. These findings highlight DA-FrFDM as a promising solution for enhancing power efficiency in future multi-carrier systems.

title: A Shared Infrastructure Verification Framework with Transient Perturbation Probing for SDN Topology Poisoning Defense
authors: Ruiqing Li, Xuanbo Huang, Lutong Chen, Hang Yin, Zixu Huang, Kaiping Xue
abstract: In Software-Defined Networking (SDN) architecture, the controller builds a global network view to manage the data plane topology through Link Layer Discovery Protocol (LLDP). However, this process is vulnerable to Topology Poisoning Attacks (TPAs), which inject false topology information into the view of the controller. Although several detection methods have been proposed, many of them continue to suffer from limited detection precision and high false positive rates. To defend against this threat, this paper proposes a detection system based on Shared Infrastructure Verification (SIV), which introduces two key techniques: transient perturbation-based link probing and traffic correlation-based link classification. The link probing technique is based on the observation that a fabricated link must use an existing legitimate data-plane link to communicate. Therefore, the traffic pattern of the fabricated link will closely resemble that of the underlying physical link. By injecting carefully controlled, millisecond-scale transient perturbations into the suspected links, we can observe the resulting traffic fluctuations across the network. The traffic correlation-based link classification method then examines these patterns to detect fabricated links. We evaluate the effectiveness of SIV through large-scale, multi-scenario experiments using the Ryu controller and the Mininet platform. Experiments demonstrate that SIV achieves a detection precision of 91.43% with a false positive rate below 1%, showing a clear improvement over traditional methods in identifying adversarial attacks.

title: Simulation Analysis of Primary Frequency Regulation Dynamic Characteristics of Thermal Power Units Under Different Control Strategies
authors: Changya Xie, Fubo Cui, Shuangbai Liu, Yuou Hu
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: Based on the six-equation two-phase flow model, the dynamic simulation model of a thermal power unit is established in this paper. Based on the thermal system model, the grid primary frequency modulation control system model is proposed, and the control model is applied to the control valves at different positions of the thermal system.
