abstract: Many countries face aging populations and declining birth rates, prompting exploration of information and communication technology (ICT)-driven solutions, including robotics. Japan’s Moonshot Project Goal 1 aims to develop cybernetic avatars (CAs) for a sustainable society where individuals maximize abilities and pursue diverse lifestyles. CAs go beyond teleoperated avatars by integrating ICT and robotics to enhance physical and cognitive functions, enabling teleoperators (TOs) to engage in social activities remotely. Stable teleoperation requires high communication quality, especially when network paths lack guaranteed bandwidth or quality of service (QoS). This paper proposes a CA reliability-ensuring platform with hybrid traffic management, comprising communication quality monitoring and visualization, jitter buffering, and dynamic path switching. The platform deploys called support node with these functions at several points in the end-to-end (E2E) network and in TO and CA devices. To evaluate the platform, a dual-arm robot (as CA) and control device were set up between two sites 70 km apart, with tests introducing quality degradation such as jitter and packet loss. In the E2E network, normal root mean square (RMS) of the difference of packet delay was 7.28 ms, calculated as a magnitude of jitter, while added jitter increased it to 20.87 ms. Using jitter buffering, the RMS was reduced to 0.84 ms. Dynamic path switching also stabilized teleoperation under packet loss, confirming the platform’s effectiveness.

title: Research on Big Data-driven Digital Economy Analysis Model and System Architecture for Regional Economic Optimization
authors: Min Wang, Lin Chen
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 International Conference on Information Economy, Data Modeling and Cloud Computing

title: Enterprise Cash Flow Optimization and Decision Support System Based on Random Forest Optimization
authors: Mengqi Gu, Yantao Li, Shuyan Meng

title: Multi-Objective Optimization for Siting and Sizing of Energy Storage Systems in Microgrids Using Nsga II
authors: Yuze Li, Wei Pei
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: This paper proposes a multi-objective optimization method for optimal siting and sizing of energy storage systems (ESS) in microgrids using NSGA-II. The objectives include minimizing operational costs and carbon emissions, considering power balance, storage dynamics, and installation constraints. Results from a case study on an IEEE 33-bus system highlight clear trade-offs between economic efficiency and environmental impact, offering valuable insights for sustainable microgrid design.

title: A Hybrid Quantum-Classical Framework for Power Optimization in CF-mMIMO O-RAN
authors: Srikanta Dash, Keshav Singh, Fan-Shuo Tseng, Shahid Mumtaz, Sudip Biswas
abstract: The increasing densification of user equipment (UE) and the adoption of open radio access network (O-RAN) architectures in 6G demand energy-efficient and scalable resource allocation frameworks. Cell-free massive MIMO (CF-mMIMO) systems, while promising improved spectral efficiency (SE), introduce significant computational complexity in jointly optimizing radio units (O-RUs), fronthaul, and general-purpose processors (GPPs). In this work, we propose a hybrid quantum-classical optimization framework based on the quantum approximate optimization algorithm (QAOA) integrated with a constrained optimization by linear approximation (COBYLA)-based classical feedback loop. The optimization problem, comprising O-RU activation, UE association, GPP scheduling, and power control is formulated as a constrained mixed-integer program and mapped into a Hamiltonian suitable for quantum execution. Our framework supports multiple orchestration strategies, including end-to-end, local coordination, and radio-only. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves up to 20% energy savings and up to 50% faster convergence compared to classical MILP baselines, even in large-scale CF-mMIMO networks. The algorithm is designed to be NISQ-compatible, making it a viable candidate for real-world near-term deployment.

title: How Machine Learning and Modulation Supports Subject Brain-Computer Interface Training
authors: José Del R. Millán
abstract: Brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) enable assistive control and motor rehabilitation by leveraging residual neural activity. When BCI control is treated as a learned skill, system design must support the activity-dependent plasticity underlying learning and consolidation. A major challenge is providing coherent and stable feedback in the presence of strong non-stationarity in neural signals, particularly EEG. We address this challenge using two complementary strategies to stabilize neural features used for BCI control. First, we employ an unsupervised machine learning algorithm that continuously aligns calibration and online feature distributions, preserving decoder validity and enabling transfer learning from expert to naïve users. Second, we integrate non-invasive electrical neuromodulation with BCI operation to modulate cortical excitability, reduce neural variability, and promote more distinct and controllable neural patterns. Together, these approaches aim to improve BCI performance, accelerate learning, and support long-term consolidation of BCI control.

title: Research on Variable Universe Fuzzy Iterative Learning Control Method for Inverter Spot Welding Power Source
authors: Huiyan Xu, Jianning Lu, Lixin Ren
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: In order to achieve a faster and more stable welding current tracking in the highly nonlinear inverter resistance spot welding process, a variable universe fuzzy iterative learning control strategy is proposed. The method adopts a PI-type closed-loop iterative learning law, introduces a fuzzy controller to rectify the learning gain in real time, and designs a function-type dynamic scaling factor to realize the adaptive adjustment of the fuzzy universe. Build a simulation model of resistance spot welding system in MATLAB/Simulink and simulate to verify, the results show that the proposed method can improve the tracking accuracy of the welding current, accelerate the response speed, improve the stability of the welding current and the dynamic performance of the system effectively. The method has better robustness and adaptability.

title: Microgrid Flexibility Operation Domain Calculation Method Based on Convex Relaxation and Adaptive Search Algorithm
authors: Shizheng Gao, Shuailong He, Wenhui Zheng, Bohan Sun
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: With the accelerating energy transition, high-penetration renewable microgrids have become vital flexible resources for secure distribution network operation. This paper proposes an AC power flow - based flexibility operation domain model using second-order cone relaxation to incorporate network losses and voltage constraints. A curvature-sensitive adaptive directional search algorithm is designed to enhance boundary identification, and convex hull fitting is applied for explicit domain representation. Case studies on the 7-bus system show that, compared with traditional uniform search, the proposed method improves efficiency by 31% and reduces boundary error by 24%, while higher sampling density in high-curvature regions effectively eliminates local distortions, demonstrating strong engineering applicability.

title: Cross-Dataset Shrimp Larvae Counting via Tiled Inference and Domain Filtering
authors: Sarra Ben Abbes, Divya Velayudhan, Naoufel Werghi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advances in Data-Driven Analytics And Intelligent Systems (ADACIS)
abstract: Accurate counting of post-larval (PL) shrimp is essential for effective hatchery management, yet manual methods remain time-consuming, error-prone, and invasive. In this study, we propose a lightweight and modular vision-based detection framework that combines slicing-based inference with domain-informed filtering to enhance object detection in high-density shrimp imagery. Unlike existing approaches that are typically trained and tested on the same dataset, our method is evaluated under a cross-dataset generalization protocol—trained on a mix of curated private dataset and a public dataset and tested on two available, unseen datasets. The detection framework is built on top of YOLO-based architectures to ensure real-time performance and scalability. We demonstrate that our approach significantly improves detection recall in crowded scenes while maintaining high precision, outperforming both baseline inference and a commercial reference model. The results highlight the importance of inference strategy design in small-object detection tasks. Our system offers a scalable, non-intrusive solution for aquaculture monitoring and may generalize to other dense-object counting scenarios in biological or environmental imaging.

title: Optimal Channel Selection for a 5-Class Imagined Vowel-based Brain–Computer Interface on Edge
authors: Nobuaki Kobayashi, Takuya Matsunaga
abstract: Active brain–computer interfaces (BCIs) that rely solely on user recall offer advantages over passive BCIs requiring stimulus presentation devices in terms of portability, system simplification, and low power consumption. However, the data from these recollections exhibit significant individual variation and scattered features, making it difficult to achieve high classification accuracy. This study focuses on vowel recollection, acquiring electroencephalography (EEG) data when users mentally recall the five vowels /a/, /e/, /i/, /o/, and /u/ without actually vocalizing them, and classifying these data using deep learning (DL). To construct a practical BCI system enabling real-time processing, previous experiments using a small number of electrodes for imagined vowel acquisition employed EEG signals from electrodes placed near the language areas of the left temporal lobe. Classification accuracy for the five vowels (5-class or 6-class classification including mute) remained at approximately 20–30%. Therefore, this study acquired imagined vowel data from three subjects using 16 electrodes distributed around the parietal Cz region. Based on the classification accuracy of multiple DL models using the acquired EEG data, useful electrodes contributing to improved classification accuracy were selected. Consequently, even using eight electrodes, a high classification accuracy of approximately 53.8% was achieved at maximum. Furthermore, the memory footprint and the multiply-accumulates required to run the DL model efficiently to classify the imagined vowel data acquired in this experiment was a level sufficiently feasible for real-time processing on edge devices.

title: Stress and Vibration Fatigue on Magnetic Shunt of Power Transformer Under Electromagnetic Force
authors: Jun Li, Huina Yang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: For the magnetic shunt of the power transformer clamp, the situation is that it endures alternative electromagnetic force from the leakage magnetic field, which further causes vibration fatigue and potential accidents, and thus this paper focuses on studying the vibration fatigue mechanism of L-shaped and U-shaped magnetic shunts. I have taken a 334MVA singlephase oil-immersed transformer as the research object, adopted methods like exploring electromagnetic force characteristics via the virtual displacement principle, analyzing stress through transient dynamics, and predicting lifespan using electromagnetic force characteristics and random vibration principles, and conducted comparative analysis of electromagnetic force, stress, and lifespan under various working conditions, with key positions for online monitoring identified. Reached the effect that the results offer valuable insights for transformer anti-fatigue design and online monitoring.

title: Optimisation of Dither Compensation Parameters of Electro-Hydraulic Servo System Based on MF-SMA
authors: Qiwei Li, Caijin Zheng
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechanical Engineering, Intelligent Manufacturing, and Automation Technology (MEMAT)
abstract: Aiming at the hysteresis loop effect in electrohydraulic servo systems, this paper proposes an intelligent control method for dither compensation based on the moth-flame slime mold optimisation algorithm (MF-SMA). By introducing a nonlinear decreasing parameter strategy and a dynamic individual selection range mechanism and integrating the moth-flame algorithm, the global search capability and convergence speed of the MF-SMA are optimised for the parameter optimisation of the dither signal. The inclusion of the optimised dither signal in the controller has significant advantages in improving the system response speed and control accuracy, and provides a new idea for the intelligent compensation and control of complex electrohydraulic servo systems.

title: Quantum Self-Heterodyne Sensing for Rydberg Atomic Receiver
authors: Mingyao Cui, Qunsong Zeng, Zhanwei Wang, Kaibin Huang
abstract: Rydberg Atomic REceivers (RAREs) have shown compelling advantages in precise measurement of radio-frequency signals, empowering quantum wireless sensing. Existing RARE-based sensing systems primarily rely on the heterodyne-sensing technique, which introduces an extra reference source to serve as the atomic mixer. However, this approach entails a bulky transceiver architecture, requiring additional reference sources and transmitter-receiver signal decoupling. To address this problem, we propose a novel concept called selfheterodyne sensing. It utilizes the self-interference generated by the transmitted sensing signal as the reference signal, thus greatly simplifying the transceiver architecture. We derive the transmission model of self-heterodyne sensing and reveal that a self-heterodyne RARE functions as an atomic autocorrelator, where the received signal represents the autocorrelation of the transmitted signal at different delays. This characteristic translates the range of a sensing target into the frequency of the received signal. Inspired by this finding, a two-stage algorithm is devised to estimate the target range via frequency estimation and Newton refinement. Numerical results validate the superiority of the proposed quantum self-heterodyne sensing method.

title: Joint Beamforming, Power Allocation, and User Grouping for NOMA-ODDM Enabled ISAC Systems
authors: Salma Sultana, Shuhao Zeng, Ahmed Abdelhadi, Husheng Li, Zhu Han, H. Vincent Poor
abstract: This work explores the integration of Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) and Orthogonal Delay-Doppler Division Multiplexing (ODDM) within an Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) framework. The proposed system leverages ODDM for high-mobility scenarios and NOMA for efficient resource utilization, thereby enabling enhanced trade-offs between communication and sensing. To achieve a balance between communication and sensing performance, an optimization problem is formulated to maximize the weighted sum of a sensing performance metric and communication throughput by jointly optimizing user grouping, power allocation, and beamforming, which are inherently coupled. To solve this problem efficiently, it is decomposed into three subproblems—user grouping, power allocation, and beamforming—which are then addressed iteratively to improve overall system performance. Simulation results validate the efficacy of the proposed framework under various mobility conditions, demonstrating improved sum-rate and sensing accuracy compared to Orthogonal Multiple Access (OMA) systems. This study offers valuable insights into advanced ISAC architectures, which are critically important for future 6G networks.

title: AarogyaLLM: LLM Guided DL Framework for Smart Telesurgery Systems in Healthcare with 6G
authors: Dev Mehta, Lakshin Pathak, Mahek Jain, Karm Vyas, Ayush Dharaiya, Rajesh Gupta, Sudeep Tanwar, Joel J. P. C. Rodrigues
abstract: Intrusion detection in self-regulating manufacturing requires light yet precise modes to do real-time threat prevention. The performance of TinyML, 1D-CNN, GRU, and LSTM models is tested based on different metrics in this research. TinyML works better than all models with 97% accuracy and log-loss as low as 1.2, which is an indication of confident predictions. One of the significant aspects of TinyML is improved accuracy, and decreased sensitivity, with increased false negative and false positive rates also making it more useful in resource-constrained areas. For natural language generation tasks, Mixtral 8x7B 32768 shows the highest BLEU score of 0.60 and METEOR score of 0.86, reflecting its high similarity with reference outputs. It also scores the highest in ROUGE-1 (0.77) and ROUGE-2 (0.75), providing high-quality phrase-level recall. These results confirm that TinyML is the most effective for intrusion detection, while Mixtral 8x7B 32768 excels in text generation tasks.

title: Task-Oriented Resource Allocation for Image Semantic Communication in Cloud-Network-End Architecture
authors: Xinyi Cai, Daosen Zhai, Ruonan Zhang, Jianfeng Ma, Ning Xi, Haotong Cao, Wael Bazzi, Shahid Mumtaz
abstract: In this paper, we propose a task-oriented semantic communication system based on the cloud-network-end (C-N-E) architecture to improve the energy efficiency of image transmission. Within the system, a cloud server provides storage and computation resources for image data collected by multiple cameras. The semantic information of an image is modeled as a scene graph, enabling the analysis of end-user interests. To reduce communication overhead, only useful semantic information relevant to user interests is transmitted. Considering the delay constraint, we formulate an optimization problem to minimize the total energy consumption by jointly selecting semantic information and allocating computation and communication resources. To solve this problem efficiently, an iterative algorithm based on optimal matching and sequential convex approximation is developed. Comparative simulations validate the efficacy of our algorithm.

title: A Cymbal Transducer as a Single Element Device for Enhanced Cavitation Generation_supp5-3675507.mp4
authors: Andrew Feeney
abstract: Cavitation driven by low-frequency ultrasound has been widely recognized as an effective method of transdermal drug delivery, but challenges remain regarding the size and efficiency of the sonicator. The cymbal transducer is a promising candidate for such applications; however, the conventional design is limited by its relatively low power output and difficulty to manufacture with consistency, limiting its success in arrays and for extended treatment times. This study introduces a modified cymbal transducer, operating at 66.2 kHz, designed to offer higher output pressure and easier fabrication process, while being compact (OD=36 mm, Thk=6 mm). The transducer was then characterised in terms of its vibration mode shape, acoustic pressure field, acoustic power, and cavitation performance captured through high-speed imaging and acoustic detection of bubble collapse shockwave emissions. Furthermore, the device was employed to permeate fluorescent nanoparticles (10 kDa) into agar-based phantom. The new design is capable of outputting up to 3.05 MPa peak-negative-pressure (PNP) at its focal region (MI=11.8), and spatial peak temporal average intensity (I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SPTA</sub>) of 1.59 W/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, significantly surpassing other devices. High-speed shadowgraphic imaging reveals superior cavitation performance that has not been demonstrated in prior studies. The transducer is also capable of delivering nanoparticles at a rate of 1.19 mm/min into the phantom. As a result, the modified cymbal proved to be not only a robust sonicator with potential for sonophoresis but also viable as a compact power ultrasonics source.

title: SemTexIB: Semantic Text Communication with Information Bottleneck: Integrating Rate and Semantic Similarity into Training Objectives
authors: Abdulrahman Alamoudi, Ahmet Faruk Saz, Yashas Malur Saidutta, Faramarz Fekri
abstract: Recent major developments in semantic communication systems stem from integration of deep learning (DL) techniques. Following the discovery of capacity achieving codes, the primary motivation for adopting the semantic approach, which retrieves meaning without requiring an exact reconstruction, is its potential to further conserve resources such as bandwidth and power. In this paper, we propose a novel semantic communication framework for textual data over additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) channels via DL. Our framework leverages the information bottleneck (IB) principle to balance minimizing bit transmission under wireless channel rate constraints with maximizing semantic information retention. Unlike previous works, we integrate the bilingual evaluation understudy (BLEU) sentence similarity score into the training objective to enhance model performance. In particular, inspired by knowledge distillation, we utilize large language models (LLMs) during training to transfer their knowledge of text semantics into our model. Using IB principle, we train a neural semantic encoder at the transmitter and a neural semantic decoder at the receiver that incorporates into its objective function the rate constraint together with the BLEU score and the knowledge encoded in the soft probabilities produced by the LLM. Through extensive experiments, our proposed framework demonstrates a notable improvement of up to 45% in text semantic similarity compared to state-of-the-art benchmarks operating at the same channel capacity, significantly outperforming traditional communication systems. Moreover, it exhibits robustness to variations in signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and achieves significant gains across both low and medium SNR regimes.

title: Closed-Form Near-Optimal Signal Detection For Underwater Acoustic Communication Using Student’s t-Distribution Approximation
authors: Takumi Ishihara, Hiroyuki Fukumoto, Yosuke Fujino
abstract: In this paper, we propose a closed-form, near-optimal signal detection (SD) scheme for underwater acoustic wireless (UAW) communication affected by non-Gaussian impulsive noise. First, we introduce an optimal SD scheme based on a Neyman-Pearson criterion-driven likelihood-ratio test (LRT), which directly employs a noise probability density function (PDF) calculated from measured UAW noise. We demonstrate that this optimal SD scheme significantly outperforms the conventional linear correlator (LC)-based SD scheme designed for Gaussian-noise channels, in terms of detection error rate (DER) performance. To address the prolonged noise observation time required for direct PDF calculation, we then propose an SD scheme that approximates the noise PDF using the Student ’s t-distribution (StD). The StD-aided PDF approximation yields a closed-form LRT expression and enables low-complexity statistical parameter estimation, in contrast to the conventional α- stable distribution-based schemes. Simulation results based on a variety of noise datasets reveal that the proposed StD-based SD scheme achieves DER performance close to that of the optimal SD scheme, while maintaining substantially lower computational complexity and shorter noise observation times.

title: Code Constructions for DNA-Based Data Storage
authors: Shu Liu, Kenan Wu, Yaqian Zhang
abstract: Due to its longevity and enormous information density, DNA is an attractive medium for archival storage. In DNA-based storage, coding techniques (DNA codes) are widely studied to protect data from errors. Besides, some biochemical means are necessary in DNA data processing and storage which may increase data error probability. So DNA codes with some biochemical constraints are desired.In this paper, we construct DNA codes with biochemical constraints, including GC-content constraint, homopolymer run-length limit and secondary structure avoidance, as well as error correcting property. We present novel constructions of DNA codes with high code rate. At first, we give a method to construct constrained DNA codes free of secondary structures of stem length m = 3 and homopolymer run-length > ℓ for any given ℓ ≥ 3. In particular, when ℓ = 4, the code has rate 1.4057 and beats a previous work by Benerjee et al. asymptotically. Then, we construct DNA codes with all of the three mentioned constraints simultaneously as well as single error correction via a code concatenation technique. To the best of our knowledge, few constructions are given to satisfy all these constraints and edit error correction with high code rate. At last, motivated by some PCR amplification techniques that require local GC-content constraint, a transformation converting codes with global GC-content constraint to codes with local GC-content constraint is presented.

title: Closer is not always better: Hop-based Cooperative Satellite Caching in Ultra-dense LEO Constellation
authors: Linhui Wei, Yu Liu, Yumei Wang, Guangteng Fan
abstract: Satellite edge caching enables satellites to store and deliver content locally, offering a promising approach to support low-latency and on-demand video services. In this paper, we introduce the concept of Closer is not always better in ultra-dense Low Earth Orbit (LEO) constellations. This refers to scenarios where users are simultaneously covered by multiple satellites, while geographically closer satellites may appear more suitable for service delivery, they often face challenges in inter-satellite cooperation. To address this, we propose a hop-based cooperative satellite caching (HCSC) method. Specifically, we develop a hop-based fast visible satellite selection strategy to identify the top-k satellites for cooperative caching. Furthermore, we design a Dueling Deep Q-Network (Dueling DQN)-based algorithm to optimize caching decisions, aiming to minimize service delay through effective satellite collaboration. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed HCSC method outperforms other baseline caching algorithms by reducing transmission delay and improving cache hit ratio.

title: Attention Driven Reinforcement Learning to Optimize Packet Retransmission in Semantic Communication
authors: Mohamed Kamel, Tamer Nadeem
abstract: Reliable data transmission in noisy environments poses a significant challenge, where retransmissions are costly in terms of bandwidth, time, and energy. Traditional error detection methods, such as Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) and Low-Density Parity Check (LDPC) codes, focus on bit-level accuracy and retransmit all corrupted packets regardless of task relevance. In this paper, we focus on semantic communication, where not all bits contribute equally to the end task. To make this concrete, we study image transmission, where certain regions carry higher semantic importance than others. We leverage DI-NOv2 attention maps and Vision Transformers to identify high-importance regions and employ reinforcement learning to optimize retransmission policies accordingly. This enables selective retransmissions guided by semantic value rather than uniform error detection. Experiments on CIFAR-100 with simulated span corruption show that our framework reduces retransmissions from over 90% with CRC to below 25%, while preserving high classification accuracy above 89%. These findings highlight the potential of Bit Error Tolerance communication systems for efficient and task-aware transmission in noisy environments.

title: Data-Driven Assessment of University Entrance Exams based on Multiple-Choice Questions: Approach for limiting “Ex-aequo” tie situations and random guessing
authors: Tarik Saidi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advances in Data-Driven Analytics And Intelligent Systems (ADACIS)
abstract: This study investigates the impact of iterative refinements in multiple/single-choice questions design on score differentiation and tie-breaking efficacy across three consecutive annual entrance examinations. The initial exam (2021/2022) employed a conventional 50-item single-choice question (SCQ) format with uniform 3-distractor/1-key structure, revealing significant challenges in resolving tie situations (ex-æquo) and controlling random guessing behavior. While the implementation of negative marking for wrong choices helped mitigate guessing, this approach alone proved insufficient to deter low-performing candidates who perceived they had "nothing to lose." In subsequent iterations, we introduced a strategic mix of question difficulties: the second exam (2022/2023) incorporated heterogeneous formats (varying distractor numbers and MCQs) while ensuring 15 - 20 % of questions were deliberately accessible to establish baseline scores. This psychological safeguard created score ownership that discouraged random guessing. The final optimized exam (2023/2024) combined distractor refinement, a negative-points approach, and calibrated difficulty, achieving both ex-æquo reduction in almost all Entrance Exam Scores (EES) rankings and with no ties at the critical part of the lists. Our data reveal that even in the worst-case scenario, candidates who responded to all questions do not exceed 9% of examinees, demonstrating remarkably responsible response patterns overall.

title: Joint Lossy Compression for a Vector Gaussian Source under Individual Distortion Criteria
authors: Shuao Chen, Junyuan Gao, Yuxuan Shi, Yongpeng Wu, Giuseppe Caire, H. Vincent Poor, Wenjun Zhang
abstract: This paper investigates the joint compression problem of a vector Gaussian source, where an individual distortion constraint is imposed on each source component. It is known that the rate-distortion function (RDF) is lower-bounded by the rate derived from the Hadamard inequality, which becomes exact when the semidefinite condition (SDC) holds. However, existing works often overlook the case where the SDC is not satisfied. Moreover, even when the SDC holds, a quantitative characterization of how correlations enable more efficient compression is lacking. In this work, we refine the results when the SDC is satisfied and derive new theoretical results when the SDC is not satisfied, thereby establishing theoretical limits for practical source compression with correlations. Specifically, we examine the properties of optimal source reconstruction and provide upper bounds on its dimension, showing that lower-dimensional reconstructions are essential for efficient compression when the SDC does not hold. Within a scalable two-type correlation (2TC) covariance framework, we prove that the probability of satisfying the SDC decays exponentially with source length, emphasizing the importance of exploring theoretical limits when the SDC is not met. Additional, we determine the component-wise correlations that a vector source should possess to achieve the Hadamard compression rate, revealing the trade-off between distortion constraints and correlations. More importantly, by deriving an explicit RDF with correlations incorporated, we quantitatively characterize the gain in compression efficiency achieved by fully leveraging source correlations.

title: Client-Aware Federated Modeling for Arc Voltage-Based Droplet Transfer Recognition
authors: Lin Chen, Fei Yang, Haichen Li, Swee Leong Sing, Zhaowei Diao, Yi Wu, Mingzhe Rong
abstract: Arc voltage signals play a pivotal role in monitoring droplet transfer modes, which are essential for ensuring stability and quality in wire arc additive manufacturing (WAAM). While deep learning has become the mainstream approach for such monitoring, its effectiveness depends on access to large, labeled datasets. However, collecting sufficient data are challenging for individual labs or manufacturers, and direct data sharing is often restricted due to privacy concerns and commercial barriers. Furthermore, data from different sources typically exhibit significant heterogeneity and domain shift, hindering model generalization. To tackle these issues, this article proposes a client-aware representation alignment (CARA) framework based on federated learning. CARA preserves data locality while enabling collaborative model training by aligning global prototypes across clients and adaptively weighting client contributions during aggregation. These mechanisms jointly improve representation consistency and cross-domain robustness. The achieved high recognition accuracy lays a foundation for subsequent closed-loop control strategies and improved WAAM deposition quality, demonstrating tangible industrial relevance. Moreover, by enabling decentralized model training without data sharing, the proposed federated framework significantly enhances data availability and scalability for deep-learning-based monitoring tasks. Experiments conducted on two real-world datasets, namely CuCrZr and Cu, validate the superiority of the proposed framework under both physically grounded and statistically induced heterogeneity, highlighting its effectiveness in privacy-preserving, generalizable monitoring of WAAM processes.

title: scBIT: Integrating Single-cell Transcriptomic Data into fMRI-based Prediction for Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis
authors: Yu-An Huang, Yao Hu, Yue-Chao Li, Xiyue Cao, Xinyuan Li, Kay Chen Tan, Zhu-Hong You, Zhi-An Huang
abstract: Functional MRI (fMRI) and single-cell transcri ptomics are pivotal in Alzheimer’s disease (AD) research, each providing unique insights into neural function and molecular mechanisms. However, integrating these complementary modalities remains largely unexplored. Here, we introduce scBIT, a novel method for enhancing AD prediction by combining fMRI with single-nucleus RNA (snRNA). scBIT leverages snRNA as an auxiliary modality, significantly improving fMRI-based prediction models and providing comprehensive interpretability. It employs a sampling strategy to segment snRNA data into cell-type-specific gene networks and utilizes a self-explainable graph neural network to extract critical subgraphs. Additionally, we use demographic and genetic similarities to pair snRNA and fMRI data across individuals, enabling robust cross-modal learning. Extensive experiments validate scBIT’s effectiveness in revealing intricate brain region-gene associations and enhancing diagnostic prediction accuracy. By advancing brain imaging transcriptomics to the single-cell level, scBIT sheds new light on biomarker discovery in AD research. Experimental results show that incorporating snRNA data into the scBIT model significantly boosts accuracy, improving binary classification by 3.39% and five-class classification by 26.59%. The codes were implemented in Python and have been released on GitHub (https://github.com/77YQ77/scBIT) and Zenodo (https://zenodo.org/records/11599030) with detailed instructions.

title: Fluid Antenna for MEC Offloading with Game Theory-Assisted Multi-Agent DRL
authors: Mingdong Li, Ying Ju, Xin Liu, Fen Hou, Lei Liu, Qingqi Pei, Shahid Mumtaz, Celimuge Wu
abstract: As an emerging communication technology, fluid antenna (FA) offers remarkable diversity and multiplexing gains due to its port mobility, which significantly reduces transmission delays in communication processes. This capability makes FA a promising solution for enhancing mobile edge computing (MEC) by optimizing communication delay. This paper establishes an FA-aided MEC offloading architecture and proposes a game theory-assisted multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (DRL) scheme to minimize the system delay of MEC. We aim to address the joint optimization problem of FA port selection, beamforming, user transmit power design, and MEC server computation resource allocation. However, the dynamic nature of FA ports and the variability of the associated large number of parameters introduce significant challenges, such as non-convexity and high dimension, in the optimization problem. In this paper, we employ game theory to reduce the dimension of the optimization variables by modeling the power control problem among multiple users as a non-cooperative game. Therefore, we propose a multi-agent deep deterministic policy gradient (MADDPG) algorithm, featuring two types of agents that collaboratively solve the problem. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed scheme, achieving 19.1-65.8% lower delays than benchmarks in MEC efficiency across all scenarios.

title: Class Importance Consistency Matters: Efficient Model Pruning for Long-tailed Recognition Models
authors: Junzhu Mao, Zeren Sun, Yazhou Yao, Xiansheng Hua, Hengtao Shen
abstract: Model pruning, aiming to reduce computational costs for deep neural networks, is essential for deploying deep models in resource-limited scenarios. While significant progress has been witnessed in pruning techniques in recent years, existing methods predominantly focus on balanced datasets. Adapting these approaches to datasets with long-tailed data distributions remains challenging since their pronounced class imbalances often lead to under-learning of tail-class samples, resulting in inaccurate estimation for parameter importance. This causes notable performance degradation after pruning. To this end, we propose Class Importance Consistency (CIC) pruning by introducing a consistency-guided criterion that enhances parameter importance evaluation across all classes. By prioritizing model parameters with stable importance across all classes and pruning those with inconsistent contributions, CIC effectively mitigates the effects of skewed importance scores. Additionally, we introduce a Class-wise Batch Sampling (CBS) strategy to streamline the computation of class-wise importance scores, significantly improving pruning efficiency. Extensive experiments on CIFAR100-LT, ImageNet-LT, and iNaturalist2018 demonstrate that CIC consistently achieves Pareto-optimal parameter-accuracy trade-offs, surpassing state-of-the-art methods in average accuracy by a margin over 1%.

title: Hybrid Machine Learning Framework for Grid-Independent Hydrogen Production Using VESS and Surplus PV
authors: Praveenkumar Rajendiran, Vijayakumar Krishnasamy
abstract: Green hydrogen production is expanding globally as a renewable energy source (RES), but its high production cost can be mitigated by integrating RES and efficient energy management. Effective energy management balances supply and demand amid renewable intermittency. RES meet peak energy loads but require reliable storage. Stationary battery storage systems (SBS) support short-term grid needs but lack capacity for long-duration storage. Virtual energy storage systems (VESS) with demand response management (DRM) shift nonessential loads like thermostatically controlled loads (TCLs) to off-peak times, enhancing grid flexibility. Despite their short-term effec tiveness, SBS and VESS fall short for extended storage. Green hydrogen, produced via electrolysis using surplus photovoltaic (PV) energy and coordinated VESS, offers scalable, long-duration storage, addressing conventional storage limits and reducing grid dependency. A gradient boosted neural network (GBNN) based model was implemented to accurately predict the available VESS energy, achieving an estimated capacity of 0.295 kWh with a high prediction accuracy of 96.7%, thereby enhancing load-shifting decisions and hydrogen (H2) production potential. An XGBoost based regression model was trained to forecast H2 output using PV availability and VESS contribution. This approach resulted in the highest surplus PV utilization of 6.56 kWh and maximum H2 production of 133.09 g. It achieved a substantial drop in H2 production cost to just $0.013/g and maximum grid energy savings of 19.33 kWh, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed energy management framework.

title: Guest Editorial: Special Issue on Advanced Design and Control of High-Speed Motors
authors: Jing Ou, Yingzhen Liu, Nicola Bianchi, Mauro Di Nardo, Woongkul Lee, Dawei Liang, Lu Wang, Dianguo Xu, Tianjie Zou, Kevin Lee, Giulio De Donato
abstract: This Guest Editorial introduces the Special Issue on Advanced Design and Control of High-Speed Motors. High-speed electric machines and their drives are enabling key applications including electrified transportation, aerospace, turbocompressors, and high-power-density industrial systems, while imposing stringent constraints on electromagnetic performance, rotor mechanical integrity, thermal management, and converter–control co-design. Following the review process, 33 papers were selected for publication. Highlights of each paper and their main contributions are discussed.

title: Multimodal Fusion for Industrial Packing Activity Recognition Using Adaptive Weighting Mechanisms
authors: Mincheng Wu, Junhao Zhang, Rushi Li, Xiufang Shi, Shibo He
publish: 2025 IEEE 23rd International Conference on Embedded and Ubiquitous Computing (EUC)
abstract: To enhance the accuracy of industrial packing activity recognition, we propose a novel multimodal fusion method that incorporates an adaptive weight adjustment mechanism based on the information entropy of each modality's probability distribution. This mechanism dynamically adjusts the contribution of each modality, thereby improving the accuracy and stability of the final predictions. Experimental results on the OpenPack dataset demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms traditional fusion approaches in terms of both recognition accuracy and prediction stability, achieving superior performance across multiple evaluation metrics. This study provides new insights and methodologies for multimodal activity recognition in industrial settings, highlighting its potential application value.

title: Landmark-Based Localization in OTIS Networks
authors: Khalil Hadi Hakami, Muhammad Faisal Nadeem, Ali Ahmad, Ali N. A. Koam, Sami Barhoumi
abstract: Interconnection networks are commonly modeled by graphs in which vertices represent processors or devices and edges represent communication links. In such networks, localization (or identification) of nodes using a small number of reference points is a fundamental task with direct relevance to routing, fault diagnosis, monitoring, and navigation. A standard graph-theoretic measure of this capability is the metric dimension (also called the locating number), defined as the minimum cardinality of a resolving set whose distance-vectors uniquely distinguish all vertices. Because computing the metric dimension is NP-hard in general, determining exact values for structured network families remains an active and practically motivated line of research. In this paper we study the metric dimension of Optical Transpose Interconnection System (OTIS) networks generated from four classical base graphs, the cycle graph, the second power of the path graph, the complete graph, and the star graph. Using distance-structure analysis, symmetry arguments, and carefully constructed resolving sets, we obtain exact metric dimension formulas for these OTIS families.

title: Which Data Harms My Regression Model: Enhancing Model Performance on Low-Quality Data through Fast Data Attribution
authors: Qingkai Sui, Yalin Wang, Chenliang Liu, Diju Liu, Xiaofang Chen, Yongfang Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: With the rapid advancement of model architectures, the accuracy of industrial predictive modeling now largely hinges on data quality. However, real-world industrial datasets frequently contain low-quality samples that compromise model performance. While existing data preprocessing methods can effectively remove salient outliers, they persistently struggle to detect latent anomalies. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a fast data attribution-based dataset selection method for regression models, termed FASTDAR, which enables the model to identify training samples that are detrimental to its performance and subsequently perform dataset selection. FASTDAR integrates deep network data attribution into the Leave-One-Out (LOO) influence calculation paradigm of linear regression models through model linearization and parameter dimensionality reduction. Considering the synergy among samples, the truncated Monte Carlo method is adopted to estimate marginal influences of each sample, and sample utility is defined for dataset selection. Validation on real-world industrial datasets demonstrates the effectiveness and practicality of our method. Experimental results show that models trained on FASTDAR selected data achieve significant performance improvements on both validation and test sets, outperforming multiple baseline methods.

title: MMHAR-28: Human Action Recognition Across RGB, Thermal, Depth, and Event Modalities
authors: Tomiris Rakhimzhanova, Askat Kuzdeuov, Artur Muratov, Huseyin Atakan Varol
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: Currently, the majority of human action recognition (HAR) models are trained on RGB video datasets. However, RGB cameras have limitations such as motion blur, low illumination, privacy concerns, and the lack of 3D spatial details. Existing HAR datasets are often unimodal or lack consistent protocols across different modalities, hindering robust multimodal learning. To bridge this gap, we present MMHAR-28, an extensive multimodal HAR dataset featuring 10,298 videos representing 28 actions captured across four camera types: RGB, depth, thermal, and event-based sensors. It includes various daily activities, sports exercises, and multi-person interactions, providing a unified benchmark for multimodal learning. To show the efficacy of MMHAR-28, we trained various HAR models such as VideoMamba, UniFormerV2, and TSM, examining the effects of increased temporal context, modality-specific performance, and the benefits of multimodal feature generalization. Notably, the multimodal Uniformerv2-MM-32 model achieved a top-1 accuracy of 97.86% on RGB, 90.71% on depth, 86.58% on event, and 98.57% on thermal modalities. We have made our dataset, source code, and models publicly available at https://github.com/IS2AI/MMHA-28 to support research in this area.

title: Dead Zone Bilateral Control for High Performance Robotic Teleoperation
authors: Rohan Kota, Patrick Dills, Gregory Reardon, Matthew L. Elwin, J. Edward Colgate
abstract: Autonomous robot policies are commonly trained using demonstration data acquired via robotic teleoperation, a process which can be time-intensive and physically demanding for human operators. Bilateral control can speed up robotic teleoperation by allowing the operator to feel the forces experienced by the remote manipulator, but it can also transmit undesirable forces, such as friction and damping, back to the operator. Here, we present dead zone bilateral control, an improvement to the conventional position-position bilateral control scheme that prevents the transmission of unwanted forces during free-space motion. We implement our controller on a custom 2-degree-of-freedom teleoperation device and show that it reduces the energy required for free-space motion by approximately 50%. During a user study in which 16 participants performed a peg rolling task, our controller reduced completion times by an average of 25% compared to the standard position-position bilateral controller. The results suggest that dead zone bilateral control can expedite the collection of teleoperated task demonstrations, allowing researchers to gather larger datasets for training autonomous robot policies.

title: Iterative Motion Compensation for Canonical 3D Reconstruction From UAV Plant Images Captured in Windy Conditions
authors: Andre Rochow, Jonas Marcic, Svetlana Seliunina, Sven Behnke
abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) phenotyping of plants plays a crucial role for understanding plant growth, yield prediction, and disease control. We present a pipeline capable of generating high-quality 3D reconstructions of individual agricultural plants. To acquire data, a small commercially available Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) captures images of a selected plant. Apart from placing ArUco markers, the entire image acquisition process is fully autonomous, controlled by a self-developed Android application running on the drone's controller. The reconstruction task is particularly challenging due to environmental wind and downwash of the UAV. Our proposed pipeline supports the integration of arbitrary state-of-the-art 3D reconstruction methods. To mitigate errors caused by leaf motion during image capture, we use an iterative method that gradually adjusts the input images through deformation. Motion is estimated using optical flow between the original input images and intermediate 3D reconstructions rendered from the corresponding viewpoints. This alignment gradually reduces scene motion, resulting in a canonical representation. After a few iterations, our pipeline improves the reconstruction of state-of-the-art methods and enables the extraction of high-resolution 3D meshes.

title: Real-Time RSSI-Based Indoor Localization Using a Two-Stage TinyML Architecture on Edge Devices
authors: Ahmet Gürkan Yüksek
abstract: Indoor positioning is a critical component of navigation and control in automated guided vehicles (AGVs) and autonomous mobile robots (AMRs). Wi-Fi RSSI–based localization provides a cost-effective solution for indoor environments; however, its deployment on edge devices is constrained by signal instability, latency requirements, and limited computational resources. This study proposes a two-stage RSSI-based localization framework specifically designed for TinyML enabled embedded systems. In the first stage, a lightweight quantized neural network generates a rapid coarse position estimate with deterministic execution on microcontroller hardware. In the second stage, localization accuracy is refined within a constrained spatial window using dual axis-based TinyML models that enforce mutual geometric consistency. The influence of access point placement is systematically evaluated using square, triangular, hexagonal, and random topologies with trajectory-based data collection. Experimental results demonstrate that random and hexagonal layouts provide superior localization stability and accuracy. Both dataset-based evaluations and real-world measured RSSI samples confirm that the fine localization stage significantly reduces localization error while preserving real-time operability. In addition to accuracy analysis, the proposed localization pipeline is implemented using fixed-point TinyML inference and executed on microcontroller-class edge hardware. The results demonstrate that the system maintains low latency, bounded computational complexity, and minimal memory usage while operating in real time. The proposed framework therefore provides an effective balance between accuracy, robustness, and computational efficiency for embedded autonomous applications.

title: Precise Ranging to an Aerial Refueling Coupler Using a DNN-Based Detection Method Based on Monocular Camera Data
authors: Ryan Lowe, Akshat Maheshwari, Michael D. M. Kutzer, Levi DeVries, Violet Mwaffo, Donald Costello
abstract: Aerial refueling is considered a key force multiplier for military aviation, as it can be used to extend the range and endurance of aircraft. With the rise of large uncrewed aerial systems (UASs), the need to develop a method by which they can act as the receiver for aerial refueling has become a priority. The Office of Naval Research has funded multiple studies to determine the feasibility of using deep neural networks (DNNs) to enable autonomous aerial refueling for probe and drogue aerial refueling with a UAS as the receiver. In this study, we expand on previous research to present a monocular camera system integrated with a DNN to accurately estimate coordinates and range to a refueling drogue within the final 25 feet of approach. Our method employs a similar-triangle algorithm that computes range and positional estimates from DNN-generated bounding boxes, with ground truth positional data provided by a calibrated motion capture system. Experiments using UR10, YASKAWA, and linear track manipulators, coupled with a motion capture system to provide truth data, demonstrate that the DNN achieves excellent precision and recall. Our experiment resulted in a mAP50 of 0.995 for all classes, and mAP50-95 values of 0.945, 0.851, and 0.898 for the drogue, coupler, and overall, respectively. Our algorithm returned an estimated range to the center of the refueling coupler that was within the 4-inch tolerance provided by our subject matter experts when the camera was within 10 feet of the target. Additionally, the majority of data points were also within the 4-inch threshold when evaluated at ranges within 22 feet. We determined that if the heading offset between the camera and the drogue was known, the algorithm could account for the misalignment and produce satisfactory results. This paper demonstrates that a DNN-based monocular vision camera system can provide range estimations within the margins needed for the aerial refueling mission in a laboratory setting.

title: VLLM-LAD: Visual Large Language Model for Zero-shot Logical Anomaly Detection
authors: Yun Peng, Xiao Lin, Nachuan Ma, Chengju Liu, Qijun Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Logical anomaly detection aims to identify inconsistencies in object relationships and scene semantics, which is a challenging task requiring high-level reasoning capabilities that go beyond low-level structural cues. Existing methods rely heavily on large amounts of labeled data, struggle in zero-shot scenarios and lack deep semantic understanding in complex scenes with multiple objects. To address this, we propose VLLM-LAD, a visual large language model framework specifically designed for zero-shot logical anomaly detection. It incorporates a Local Detail Visual Perceiver (LDVP) module for fine-grained anomaly localization and a Global Semantic Feature Fusion (GSFF) module for enhanced multimodal reasoning. We also introduce a global-local-regional contrastive learning strategy to align visual and textual representations, thereby achieving robust zero-shot performance. Extensive experiments on MVTec LOCO demonstrate that VLLM-LAD outperforms existing methods in accuracy and interpretability. To further advance research in this area, we introduce the RLAD dataset. This large-scale, diverse dataset is designed for logical anomaly detection, encompassing complex scenarios that demand reasoning about object relationships and global semantics. Our method not only excels on the RLAD dataset but also provides detailed textual explanations and segmentation masks, enhancing its transparency and applicability to real-world tasks. This work establishes a new benchmark for zero-shot logical anomaly detection, offering a robust framework and a valuable dataset to propel future research.

title: IEEE Network Magazine Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: Silent Guardians: Independent and Secure Decision Tree Evaluation Without Chatter
authors: Jinyuan Li, Liang Feng Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: As machine learning as a service (MLaaS) gains increasing popularity, it raises two critical challenges: privacy and verifiability. For privacy, clients are reluctant to disclose sensitive private information to access MLaaS, while model providers must safeguard their proprietary models. For verifiability, clients lack reliable mechanisms to ensure that cloud servers execute model inference correctly. Decision trees are widely adopted in MLaaS due to their popularity, interpretability, and broad applicability in domains like medicine and finance. In this context, outsourcing decision tree evaluation (ODTE) enables both clients and model providers to offload their sensitive data and decision tree models to the cloud securely. However, existing ODTE schemes often fail to address both privacy and verifiability simultaneously. To bridge this gap, we propose PVODTE, a novel two-server private and verifiable ODTE protocol that leverages homomorphic secret sharing and a MAC-based verification mechanism. PVODTE eliminates the need for server-to-server communication, enabling independent computation by each cloud server. This “non-interactive” setting addresses the latency and synchronization bottlenecks of prior arts, making it uniquely suitable for wide-area network (WAN) deployments. To our knowledge, PVODTE is the first two-server ODTE protocol that eliminates server-to-server communication. Furthermore, PVODTE achieves security against malicious servers, where servers cannot learn anything about the client's input or the providers' decision tree models, and servers cannot alter the inference result without being detected.

title: E-MHSAC: Physical Layer Key Generation in MIMO-RIS Systems Using Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Tingyu Xie, Guoshun Nan, Qimei Cui, Gang Deng, Huici Wu, Xiaofeng Tao
abstract: Physical layer key generation (PLKG) is an effective technique for establishing symmetric keys between legitimate users, but in harsh environments with limited channel randomness, traditional methods are often constrained by low key generation rates. To address this issue, this paper introduces Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface (RIS) technology, which dynamically reshapes wireless channels by controlling numerous discrete-state reflective elements, providing additional channel diversity. For RIS-assisted MIMO systems, we propose an end-to-end PLKG framework based on deep reinforcement learning, eliminating the need for perfect channel state information. To tackle the dimensionality challenge caused by increasing numbers of RIS elements, we design an Efficient Multi-Head Soft Actor-Critic (E-MHSAC) algorithm that significantly reduces computational complexity through shared feature extraction layers and independent output heads. We innovatively introduce a residual interaction enhancement mechanism and adaptive expectation estimation method, effectively solving policy optimization problems in high-dimensional discrete action spaces. Simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm can adaptively learn environmental characteristics and optimize RIS phase, showing significant performance advantages in real wireless environments. This approach provides efficient security solutions for resource-constrained networks and opens new research directions for RIS-assisted physical layer secure communications.

title: Drowsiness Detection & Alert System with Posture Analysis Using OpenCV & MediaPipe
authors: Anshi Agarwal, Yashvardhan Joshi, Riya, Eshan Singh, Nitasha Soni
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Drowsiness among students negatively impacts focus, productivity, and well-being. Prolonged fatigue and poor posture reduce cognitive function, leading to lower academic performance and health risks. This paper presents a real-time Drowsiness Detection and Alert System with Posture Analysis to enhance focus during study sessions. The system integrates OpenCV for video processing, MediaPipe for landmark detection, and Pygame for alerts. Drowsiness is monitored by analyzing the Eye Aspect Ratio (EAR) and Mouth Aspect Ratio (MAR), while posture indicators-such as head tilting or resting the chin on the hand-are detected using the Holistic MediaPipe model. To help users stay focused, the system provides real-time alerts that encourage quick corrections, supporting both attentiveness and ergonomic safety. Tests conducted with webcam footage show that the system is reliable in detecting facial landmarks and classifying posture. Overall, the proposed approach offers an affordable and non-intrusive way to monitor students effectively.

title: Virtual Multiview Fusion for mmWave Imaging Assisted by Multiple Metasurfaces
authors: Xiaotong Lu, Guanghua Liu, You Xu, Haoran Yuan
abstract: MmWave imaging assisted by metasurfaces is a burgeoning technique attributed to its fine-grained imaging ability by time-varying phase coding, which produces a large virtual aperture. To obtain stereoscopic 3D perception, multiple metasurfaces can be utilized for virtual multiview fusion, allowing for the capture of features that are not visible from a single viewpoint. However, the multipath imaging fusion faces huge data burden and the environment clutter especially reflection from the direct path will cause disturbance. To address these issues, this paper introduces Bayesian compressive sensing to focus on the region of interest (ROI) and design a double sparse prior for high-resolution multiview image reconstruction. First, multiple metasurfaces are utilized to generate virtual multiview imaging results. Then, the Bayesian inference method is leveraged to resist environmental noise and achieve autofocusing imaging with undersampled data. The expectation propagation (EP) is introduced to estimate the statistical parameter iteratively. Further, a double sparse prior is designed based on spike-and-slab to promote inter-sparsity and intra-sparsity simultaneously. Simulation results show that our proposed system demonstrates superior performance by fusing sparse images from multiple metasurfaces arrangements.

title: UAV-enabled Computing Power Networks: Task Completion Probability Analysis
authors: Yiqin Deng, Zhengru Fang, Senkang Hu, Yanan Ma, Haixia Zhang, Yuguang Fang
abstract: This paper presents an innovative framework that synergistically enhances computing performance through ubiquitous computing power distribution and dynamic computing node accessibility control via adaptive unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) positioning, establishing UAV-enabled Computing Power Networks (UAV-CPNs). In UAV-CPNs, UAVs function as dynamic aerial relays, outsourcing tasks generated in the request zone to an expanded service zone, consisting of a diverse range of computing devices, from vehicles with onboard computational capabilities and edge servers to dedicated computing nodes. This approach has the potential to alleviate communication bottlenecks in traditional computing power networks and overcome the "island effect" observed in multi-access edge computing. However, how to quantify the network performance under the complex spatio-temporal dynamics of both communication and computing power is a significant challenge, which introduces intricacies beyond those found in conventional networks. To address this, in this paper, we introduce task completion probability as the primary performance metric for evaluating the ability of UAV-CPNs to complete ground users’ tasks within specified end-to-end latency requirements. Utilizing theories from stochastic processes and stochastic geometry, we derive analytical expressions that facilitate the assessment of this metric. Our numerical results emphasize that striking a delicate balance between communication and computational capabilities is essential for enhancing the performance of UAV-CPNs. Moreover, our findings show significant performance gains from the widespread distribution of computing nodes.

title: Self-Supervised Learning Informed Radio Environment Map Estimation with Few Samples
authors: Jianping Ma, Zezhong Zhang, Junting Chen, Kai Chen, Shuguang Cui
abstract: Accurate radio environment map (REM) estimation is essential for optimizing wireless networks, yet existing neural network-based methods face a critical bottleneck, i.e., their reliance on large, high-quality training datasets, which are usually unavailable. To overcome this limitation, we introduce a novel pretraining strategy, which is inspired by self-supervised learning (SSL), tailored for REM estimation that enhances data efficiency and generalizability by learning radio wave propagation characteristics from partial urban layouts. Our SSL-informed radio environment map (SSL-Radio) employs an encoder-decoder architecture, where the encoder processes unmasked radio maps and sparse measurements, including city structures (e.g., buildings, streets, and the transmitter information), while the decoder reconstructs missing signal strengths by reasoning about spatial dependencies and environmental constraints, enforcing high-level reasoning over low-level interpolation. By translating the problem into a generative denoising problem, our method reduces dependency on extensive data while capturing complex interactions between signal propagation and the environment. Experiments demonstrate consistent improvements in estimation accuracy (2% to 8%) under small-sample regimes, validating its effectiveness in real-world deployment.

title: Optimization of Aerial Relay Placement and Antenna Selection in Untrusted UAV-Assisted Networks via PSO and Deep Learning
authors: Lefteris Tsipi, Emmanouel T. Michailidis, Nektarios Moraitis, Demosthenes Vouyioukas
abstract: This paper investigates physical-layer security (PLS) in UAV-assisted communication networks featuring an untrusted amplify-and-forward aerial relay. To counter potential eavesdropping threats, a destination-aided jamming strategy is employed. The proposed solution adopts a hybrid framework that integrates particle swarm optimization (PSO) and a dual-layer deep neural network (DNN). In this framework, PSO is responsible for optimizing the three-dimensional (3-D) placement of the UAV relay by minimizing the total aggregate path loss, while the DNN tackles the transmit antenna selection (TAS) problem at the source to maximize the average secrecy rate. The effectiveness of the suggested approach is assessed by comparing diverse relay placement techniques and antenna selection methods. The simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently outperforms conventional machine learning (ML)-based approaches and random baselines. Interestingly, single-antenna selection achieves slightly better secrecy rates than dual-antenna configurations. Overall, the proposed method provides a scalable and computationally efficient solution for enhancing the security of UAV-enabled communications in scenarios involving untrusted relays.

title: Study on Flexible Load Optimization Scheduling of Virtual Power Plant for the Distribution Network Voltage Overrun Problem
authors: Jiadong Cui, Tengxiao Li
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: With the increasing proportion of distributed photovoltaic systems in the distribution network, the problem of voltage overrun in the distribution network becomes increasingly significant. Flexible loads exhibit characteristics of fast response and controllable energy regulation, which can meet the user's demand and assist in voltage regulation of the distribution network. In this paper, the compensation power required to mitigate voltage deviation is determined through power flow calculations. The optimal scheduling model for a virtual power plant (VPP) is constructed to allocate the reference regulation power reasonably. The Gurobi solver is used to realize the cooperative scheduling of multiple types of equipment. Meanwhile, this paper adopts the state of charge (SOC) to characterize the regulation capability of flexible loads, which can effectively protect user privacy. Simulation results demonstrate that this method can effectively address the voltage overrun issue in a typical IEEE 33-node distribution network. It has good application value in realizing the cooperative scheduling of multiple types of flexible loads, considering both user comfort and the safety of grid operation.

title: Calculation of Attachment and Ionization Collision Cross-Section of a Novel Environmentally Friendly Gas
authors: Nian Tang, Peiqiong Liu, Dongwei Sun
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: This study conducted theoretical calculations on the electron-molecule collision cross-sections of environmentally friendly insulating gases. Using a semiclassical theoretical calculation method and modified parameters, the ionization cross-sections of the environmentally friendly insulating gas <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CF}_{3} \text{SCF}_{3}$</tex> were accurately calculated. Additionally, the method of calculating attachemnt cross-sections via inversion from PT experimental data was adopted: experiments were carried out on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CF}_{3} \text{SCF}_{3}$</tex> gas mixtures at extremely low mixing ratios, their effective ionization rate coefficients were measured, and the attachemnt cross-sections of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CF}_{3} \text{SCF}_{3}$</tex> were further derived.

title: Research on On-Site Intelligent Detection Technology of Voltage Transformers Based on HarmonyOS Architecture
authors: Tao Liao, Kan Zhang, Yufu Zhao, Liming Gao, Shucang Zhang, Xiaoping Peng
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: To address the problems of low intelligence, low efficiency, and delayed data processing in on-site detection of voltage transformers, this paper proposes a 220 kV on-site intelligent detection technology for voltage transformers based on the HarmonyOS architecture. The system, built on the domestically developed and independently controllable HarmonyOS, integrates an adaptive stepwise compensation power supply, a centralized control module, and a remote operation and maintenance terminal to construct a novel solution enabling full-process automation, paperless operation, and digitalization of detection. By employing an intelligent error analysis algorithm and a low-voltage capacitance measurementvariable frequency resonance boosting and inductance tuning method, the system can automatically calculate the distribution capacitance and compensation inductance of the primary circuit, thereby realizing adaptive control of the entire process of boosting compensation, error measurement, and data processing. System testing results show that the detection error of this scheme is stably controlled within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 0.2 {\%}$</tex>, and the detection time for a single transformer is reduced by more than 40% compared with traditional methods, significantly reducing manual adjustments and safety risks. Additionally, it supports remote data management, automatic generation of test reports, and cloud-based analysis and sharing. The research results provide a feasible path for the intelligentization and localization of voltage transformer on-site detection and hold significant importance for promoting the digital transformation of the power grid and the construction of standardized metering.

title: Optimization of Electrical Operation and Arc Flash Risk Mitigation in Medium- and Low-Voltage Systems for Underground Mining
authors: Cesar Quispe Lopez, Jhosselin Madahi Flores Camayo, Luis Angel Perez Ignacio
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: This study addresses the problems of voltage drops, poor protection selectivity, and high incident energy levels in the medium- and low-voltage electrical system of the Mining Unit from Perú. To solve these issues, a model is proposed based on load flow, short-circuit, protection coordination, and arc-flash hazard studies using ETAP 20.0 software. The method incorporates normative derivations from IEEE 1584-2018 and IEC 60909, enabling accurate incident energy calculations and optimization of fault clearing times. The results demonstrate that the proposed settings reduce incident energy from more than 20 cal/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> to values close to 12 cal/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and improve overall system reliability. This solution enhances personnel safety, ensures operational continuity, and provides a replicable model for other underground mining operations.

title: A Dual Function Image System for Multimodal Interface
authors: Chandana. Y, Krupa Chaitanya. Y, Sk. A. Abdul Kareem, G.L. Varaprasad, Anuradha. K, Ramalinga Reddy. K, Syed Rizwana
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: A dual function image system that combines image-to-image a semantic retrieval and text-to-image generation into a single framework is presented in this work. Using the WANG dataset and ImageCLEF, the system is made to handle both content-based search tasks and creative generation tasks. For text-to-image generation, we synthesize high-quality <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 1 2} \boldsymbol{\times} \mathbf{5 1 2}$</tex> images from user-provided prompts using the Stable Diffusion v1.5 model. The system uses Open AI's CLIP (ViT-B/32) to extract high-dimensional semantic embeddings and YOLOv8 for object detection in image retrieval. FAISS is used to index these embeddings for a quick and effective similarity search. With high precision and recall across several categories, the system is evaluated using standard classification metrics and achieves a Top-1 accuracy of 90.38 percentage and a macro-average ROC AUC of 0.9267. Strong multi-modal interaction is made possible by this dual functionality, which supports a variety of applications in design, surveillance, and content discovery by enabling users to produce original visual content and retrieve semantically related images based on visual features.

title: Statistical Security-QoS Guaranteed mURLLC Over Cell-Free Massive-MIMO Mobile Networks
authors: Xi Zhang, Qixuan Zhu, H. Vincent Poor
abstract: The massive ultra-reliable and low-latency communications (mURLLC) services are emerging as a new traffic type for the next-generation mobile wireless networks that support a massive number of mobile users (MUs) demanding the diverse and stringent quality-of-services (QoS) on both short delay and low error-rate. Clearly, supporting mURLLC while guaranteeing the security QoS is crucial for implementing the cell-free massive multiple-input-multiple-output (cell-free massive MIMO) mobile network using finite blocklength coding (FBC). Towards these ends, in this paper we propose to develop a new statistical security-QoS provisioning scheme using FBC while simultaneously guaranteeing statistical delay-bounded QoS and error-rate bounded QoS for mURLLC. First, we establish a cellfree massive MIMO channel model for supporting mURLLC between distributed WiFi access points (APs) and multiple mobile users with the existence of an eavesdropper. Second, we maximize the achievable secrecy rate for cell-free massive MIMO while upper-bounding the secrecy-information leak-probability. Third, we define and develop the new metric of secrecy effective capacity for three-dimensional (3D) statistical QoS provisioning over cell-free massive MIMO networks. Finally, we use numerical analyses to validate and evaluate our developed statistical security-QoS guaranteed schemes.

title: Analysis of Semantic Communication for Logic-based Hypothesis Deduction
authors: Ahmet Faruk Saz, Siheng Xiong, Faramarz Fekri
abstract: This work presents an analysis of semantic communication in the context of First-Order Logic (FOL)-based deduction. Specifically, the receiver holds a set of hypotheses about the State of the World (SotW), while the transmitter has incomplete evidence about the true SotW but lacks access to the ground truth. The transmitter aims to communicate limited information to help the receiver identify the hypothesis most consistent with true SotW. We formulate the objective as approximating the posterior distribution at the transmitter to the receiver. Using Stirling’s approximation, this reduces to a constrained, finite-horizon resource allocation problem. Applying the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions yields a truncated water-filling solution. Despite the problem’s non-convexity, symmetry and permutation invariance ensure global optimality. Based on this, we design message selection strategies, both for single- and multi- round communication, and model the receiver’s inference as an m-ary Bayesian hypothesis testing problem. Under the Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) rule, our communication strategy achieves optimal performance within budget constraints. We further analyze convergence rates and validate the theoretical findings through experiments, demonstrating reduced error over random selection and prior methods.

title: eBPF-Driven ATS Scheduler: An Advanced Stream Processing Approach for Industry 5.0
authors: Abderrahmane Boulahdour, Miloud Bagaa, Adlen Ksentini, Messaoud Ahmed Ouameur, Daniel Massicotte
abstract: In this paper, we present a programmable data plane design that implements IEEE 802.1Qcr’s Asynchronous Traffic Shaper (ATS) purely in software using Extended Berkeley Packet Filter (eBPF), eliminating specialized hardware requirements for industrial Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) deployments. Unlike hardware-bound TSN solutions, our approach dynamically decouples and reprograms functions like filtering, metering, and queuing through in-kernel eBPF hooks, enabling adaptive priority management for concurrent streams within shared priority queues. The design explicitly models the ATS scheduler to parameterize per-stream eligibility times in TSN bridges while maintaining deterministic operation. This software-defined method provides a vendor-agnostic path for integrating ATS capabilities into existing industrial networks, particularly for Industry 5.0’s distributed control scenarios requiring flexible traffic multiplexing. The results confirm correct enforcement of ATS scheduling semantics under heterogeneous workloads.

title: Paddy Leaf Disease Detection and Pesticide Recommender System Using Machine Learning
authors: Nagothu Bala Indra Kumar, Pramod Mathew Jacob
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Paddy is a very important staple crop around the world. However, it is very susceptible to a number of diseases which can greatly reduce its yield and quality. Current methods of disease detection rely on human cultivation, which can be slow, inaccurate, and simply not applicable to large scale agribusiness. A Paddy Leaf Disease Detection and Pesticide Recommender System based on machine learning is intended to address these issues. The system uses image-processing algorithms to extract statistical and texture features from pictures of leaves and those features are mean intensity, entropy, contrast, homogeneity, and smoothness, which were then used to train three supervised learning algorithms: SVM, KNN, and RFC.
