The most important issue being tackled is the the research of using small, low-power, suitable for use in settings like farms and markets devices to enable accurate and efficient quality assessment of food items like fruits and vegetables. On the other hand, Canny and Sobel edge detection algorithms identify shape anomalies, discoloration, or surface distortations. The proposed method extracts local features by means of SIFT, so identifying patterns that imply spoilage. A small weight classifier processes these characteristics to reach a quality grade conclusion for the food. Results and discussion: The system is tested using a custom dataset of tomatoes at different stages of freshness. A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B runs the system. The findings show that the proposed method has a classification accuracy of 92.5%, which is better than three conventional techniques depending on color histogram analysis, basic thresholding, and PCA-based feature reduction.

title: Research on Multiphysics Modeling Methods for Lithium-Ion Batteries
authors: Tengteng Li, Jinqiao Du, Bo Rao, Jie Tian, Zhaojie Liang, Jiuchun Jiang
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: This study investigates multiphysics coupling modeling of lithium-ion batteries using COMSOL Multiphysics to construct an electrochemical-thermal-mechanical coupling model. The interactions among electrochemical, thermal, and mechanical behaviors during battery operation are systematically analyzed. Electrochemical simulations reveal voltage-current responses, electrolyte concentration/potential distributions, and electrode current density variations during charge-discharge cycles. Integrating thermal modules elucidates the dynamic relationship between reaction heat generation and temperature fields, showing positive correlations between temperature gradients, current density, and polarization effects. Mechanical modeling quantifies diffusion-induced and thermal stress distributions, highlighting significant stress concentration in the positive electrode and negligible thermal expansion compared to electrochemical volume changes. Coupled simulations uncover a chain feedback mechanism: “electrochemical reactions → heat generation → temperature rise → thermal expansion → stress concentration”. Optimization strategies for electrode structures, thermal management, and material parameters are proposed. The findings provide theoretical and methodological support for enhancing battery performance, lifespan, and safety.

title: A Digital Twin System for DRL-Based Operation and Maintenance of Nuclear Power Plants
authors: Xiaolong Fu, Yuxin Zheng, Zheng Shi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechanical Engineering, Intelligent Manufacturing, and Automation Technology (MEMAT)
abstract: In the context of the global energy structure's accelerating transition towards low-carbonization, nuclear energy is an energy source with various advantages, such as high efficiency and cleanliness. In the era of artificial intelligence, promoting the intelligence of relevant industries in the development of nuclear power, especially the intelligent operation and maintenance of nuclear power plants, holds significant promise. Digital twin technology offers technical support for the integration of virtual and real aspects in the intelligent operation and maintenance of nuclear power. Nevertheless, in the nuclear power domain, the integration of multi-source heterogeneous data poses challenges. The complexity and diversity of time-series data result in insufficient accuracy of safety early warnings. Consequently, this paper presents a digital twin approach tailored for the intelligent operation and maintenance of nuclear power. This approach encompasses key technologies, including digital twin modeling and dynamic early warning state monitoring. Taking the timeseries data in the circulating water pump scenario as the research subject, a time-series data early-warning algorithm based on deep reinforcement learning is proposed. The F1-score of the algorithm experiment results all exceed 98%, demonstrating significant application potential.

title: Research on Decentralized Complementary Comprehensive Mitigation Strategy for Voltage Sag
authors: Peng Wang, Yuanhui Sun, Fuchun Li, Jianxin Bi, Chunfeng Liu, Wenhuan Cheng
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: Voltage sag can cause abnormal interruption in the production process of industrial users, resulting in huge economic losses and even safety accidents. In view of the different existing voltage sag mitigation schemes but the lack of a unified analysis method, this paper integrates the missing energy on the grid side, the tolerance of process equipment to voltage sag and the compensation effect of mitigation devices from the energy point of view, conducts the supplementary energy analysis of the voltage sag mitigation devices, and proposes a decentralized and complementary voltage sag mitigation scheme. On this basis, with economic optimization as the objective function and the minimum supplementary energy of mitigating voltage sag as the constraint, different voltage sag scenarios were considered to calculate the prevention and control measures configuration of industrial enterprises. Finally, the method proposed in this paper is applied to a petroleum refining enterprise, and the results show that the method can effectively mitigate voltage sags and is more economical than the traditional voltage compensation scheme.

title: Optimizing Supply Chain Management Using Ensemble Learning-Based Predictive Analytics
authors: Sudha S, C. Natarajan, Meenakshi K, A. Saravanan, Sudeshna Seal, Sangeetha S
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Supply Chain Management (SCM) is particularly crucial for making customers happier, lowering costs, and making operations function more smoothly. Three areas where traditional supply chain management systems generally don't meet expectations are demand forecasting, supplier reliability, and inventory control. We need plans that are based on facts and are up to date in order to fix these issues. This paper talks about a methodology for making smart decisions in supply chain management (SCM) that is based on ensemble learning. The goal is to make predictions more accurate and the system better at dealing with difficulties. There are a variety of different machine learning methods used in this model. The suggested solution combines XGBoost, Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM), and Random Forest (RF) using a weighted voting system. The idea is to get better at keeping track of stock and figuring out how much of a product will be needed. When you employ real-world SCM datasets from Kaggle in experimental simulations, the ensemble model is more accurate and can be used in more situations than single models. When you compare it to models built by hand, this is true. Compared to only using models, the suggested strategy made all projections 12 % more accurate. It also reduced down on stock-outs and overstock incidents by 18 % and made all forecasts more accurate.

title: The Social and Psychological Impact of IoT on Daily Life: Implications for Mental Health
authors: Priyanka Gupta, Girish Lakhera, Manu Sharma, Akhilesh Sharma, Sudhanshu Joshi, Janmejai Shah
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: A repeatable bibliometric procedure was used on Scopus (20202025). The corpus completed (117 peer-reviewed documents) was chosen following title/abstract and full-text filtering. Bibliometric approaches (co-authorship, cooccurrence and citation networks) were adopted with bibliometrix/Biblioshiny and VOSviewer (see Methods with specific queries and tool setup). Thematic clustering of keywords generated four thematic clusters; Four thematic clusters were formed using the keyword clustering: Network metrics and counts (e.g., average citations per document = 14.32; average co-authors per document = 4.11) measure the structure of the field. It revealed four main topics of research, including IoT and health monitoring (cluster size = 18 keywords); (2) AI and machine learning applied to mental-health analytics (size = 12); (3) workplace technostress and digital fatigue (size = 11); and (4) privacy, ethics, and policy (size = 11). China is the largest in the number of publications (37/117; 31.6%). The review shows that there is a high level of interest in multidisciplinary but a low degree of cross-disciplinary integration, accompanied by the lack of longitudinal/cross-cultural validation research. It is the first reproducible bibliometric mapping that deals directly with social and psychological effects of IoT over 2020-2025, combining quantitative network measures with a proposal-based thematic framework (P1-P4) and specific recommendations to research design, external validation, and feasibility of implementation of mental-health-sensitive IoT systems.

title: Per-Flow Microburst Mitigation for Low-Latency Networks
authors: Yo-Ru Chen, Shie-Yuan Wang
abstract: Modern high-speed networks face critical performance challenges from microbursts—transient traffic spikes that degrade latency-sensitive applications despite low average utilization. While existing approaches detect and mitigate microbursts, they lack fine-grained control at individual flow levels. We present MicroFlow, a system that advances microburst management through per-flow monitoring and targeted priority-based migration in programmable data planes. MicroFlow dynamically adjusts scheduling priorities during congestion events without requiring packet detouring or bandwidth-consuming recirculation. Our implementation on programmable switches demonstrates significant improvement in congestion management, reducing queuing delays for short flows by 82% compared to traditional QoS mechanisms under mixed workload conditions, while maintaining full link utilization. MicroFlow enables consistent low-latency performance for critical applications during extreme traffic variations without introducing additional packet processing overhead or consuming extra bandwidth resources.

title: Near-Field Sparse MIMO Bistatic OFDM-ISAC for Low-Altitude UAV Swarm
authors: Hongqi Min, Xinrui Li, Yong Zeng
abstract: Integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) is a pivotal technology for low-altitude unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) swarm. As the sensing targets for UAV swarm systems are usually densely located, the conventional compact multi-input multi-output (MIMO) with half-wavelength antenna spacing usually leads to prohibitive hardware, energy and signal processing costs when large array aperture is needed to achieve fine spatial resolution. By relaxing the traditional half-wavelength spacing constraint, sparse MIMO may achieve a larger array aperture without having to increase the number of antenna elements or radio frequency (RF) chains, which improves spatial resolution for both communication and sensing. Besides, sparse MIMO may also result in a larger near-field region. Therefore, in this paper, we study the near-field sparse MIMO bistatic orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM)-ISAC for low-altitude UAV swarm systems and propose the framework that utilizes physical array for communication while virtual array for sensing. The proposed method forms virtual arrays at the ISAC transmitter and sensing receiver simultaneously, while eliminating the angle and range coupling effect. As a result, high-resolution angle estimation based on virtual array is achieved. Simulation results demonstrate that sparse MIMO simultaneously improves communication sum rates and sensing resolution compared to the conventional compact MIMO.

title: Low-Complexity Near-Field Beam Training with DFT Codebook based on Beam Pattern Analysis
authors: Zijun Wang, Rama Kiran, Shawn Tsai, Rui Zhang
abstract: Extremely large antenna arrays (ELAAs) operating in high-frequency bands have spurred the development of near-field communication, driving advancements in beam training design. This paper introduces an efficient near-field beam training method that utilizes the discrete Fourier transform (DFT) codebook traditionally employed for far-field users (FUs). We begin by analyzing the received beam pattern and deriving closed-form expressions for its width and central beam gain as a function of user location. Building on these derived expressions, we propose a beam training method that estimates user distance from the received signal powers using DFT beams, with a computational complexity of O(1). Simulation results confirm the efficacy of our approach, demonstrating its ability to perform low-complexity near-field beam training with high estimation accuracy. Notably, our proposed scheme achieves up to a 1.96 dB SNR gain over the exhaustive search methods in multi-user beamforming scenarios.

title: Table of Content
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)

title: Dynamic Beamforming and Power Allocation in ISAC via Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Duc Nguyen Dao, André B. J. Kokkeler, Haibin Zhang, Yang Miao
abstract: Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) is a key enabler in 6G networks, where sensing and communication capabilities are designed to complement and enhance each other. One of the main challenges in ISAC lies in resource allocation, which becomes computationally demanding in dynamic environments requiring real-time adaptation. In this paper, we propose a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-based approach for dynamic beamforming and power allocation in ISAC systems. The DRL agent interacts with the environment and learns optimal strategies through trial and error, guided by predefined rewards. Simulation results show that the DRL-based solution converges within 2000 episodes and achieves up to 80% of the spectral efficiency of a semidefinite relaxation (SDR) benchmark. More importantly, it offers a significant improvement in runtime performance, achieving decision times of around 20 ms compared to 4500 ms for the SDR method. Furthermore, compared with a Deep Q-Network (DQN) benchmark employing discrete beamforming, the proposed approach achieves approximately 30% higher sum-rate with comparable runtime. These results highlight the potential of DRL for enabling real-time, high-performance ISAC in dynamic scenarios.

title: Enhancing Data Storage Security in Cloud Computing Using Multifactor Biometric and Keystroke Dynamics
authors: Rahul Shah
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Cloud computing has emerged as a dominant paradigm for data storage and access, but the security of sensitive information stored in the cloud remains a critical challenge. Traditional single-factor authentication methods such as passwords are vulnerable to attacks, making them inadequate for protecting cloud environments. To address this, a multifactor authentication model that integrates physiological biometrics (fingerprint recognition) with behavioral biometrics (keystroke dynamics) is proposed. The framework enhances data storage security by requiring dual authentication, thereby mitigating risks of unauthorized access. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the proposed system achieves improved accuracy and reliability compared to individual biometric methods, with notable reductions in false acceptance and rejection rates. The contribution of this work lies in (i) combining fingerprint and keystroke dynamics for robust cloud authentication, (ii) presenting implementation details with dataset validation, and (iii) conducting comparative analysis against baseline methods. This approach strengthens the security framework for cloud storage and provides a foundation for future deployments of multimodal biometric authentication in cloud-based services.

title: Measurement and Evaluation of Psychological States in Horse Racing Spectators Using the β/α Ratio of EEG Signals
authors: Michihiro Kubo, Kengen Koshino, Ryota Horie
abstract: This study measured the β/α ratio of EEG signals for subjects watching actual horse races simultaneously to verify whether the excitement of horse racing affects this ratio. We conducted the study with eight participants who had little experience watching horse races, showing them a race where no one knew the outcome. We measured resting EEG before the race, followed by EEG recording during the race, and administered a questionnaire after the race concluded. The results showed that the β/α ratio tended to be higher during viewing than at rest. Notably, the ratio during the final 100 meters was significantly higher than that of the same course section during the first lap. On the other hand, a discrepancy was observed between the subjective evaluation of excitement in the final straight and the β/α ratio.

title: A Learning-Driven Approach for Real-Time Video Analysis in Dynamic Edge Networks
authors: Xianyang Xu, Rong Cong, Huanlai Xing, Zhiwei Zhao
publish: 2025 IEEE 23rd International Conference on Embedded and Ubiquitous Computing (EUC)
abstract: Edge-based video analytics faces the dual challenge of dynamic network conditions and strict real-time processing requirements. Existing offloading and scheduling methods often fail to adapt efficiently to varying link capacities, resulting in high latency, unstable detection accuracy, and frame loss. In this paper, we propose LDKite, a reinforcement learning-driven, linkadaptive framework for real-time video analytics in dynamic edge networks. LDKite jointly optimizes computation placement and frame offloading decisions by continuously learning from network state feedback, enabling adaptive scheduling that accounts for both network bandwidth variations and task-specific processing demands. The framework incorporates a lightweight policy network that efficiently guides offloading decisions while maintaining scalability to large edge networks. Extensive experiments on realistic edge scenarios demonstrate that LDKite outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving up to 25% reduction in frame processing delay, only 4% frame loss, and 91% detection accuracy. The results validate LDKite's effectiveness in delivering robust, scalable, and high-performance video analytics under dynamic edge conditions, highlighting its potential for deployment in real-world edge computing environments.

title: Enhancing MAC-Layer Security and Performance with Adaptive Backoff Optimization
authors: Sofiane Hamrioui, Redouane Djelouah, Pascal Lorenz
abstract: This paper introduces AMBA, an adaptive backoff algorithm that strengthens security at the Medium Access Control (MAC) layer for IoT and vehicular networks. AMBA effectively counters jamming and denial-of-service (DoS) attacks, delivering impressive results: 15 Mbps throughput, a 60% reduction in latency compared to JRMP, and a 67% improvement in packet loss resilience. The Security Threat Resilience Metric (STRM) shows a 20% boost in resilience in hostile environments. Leveraging real-time traffic analysis and physical layer feedback, AMBA detects and mitigates malicious activity while maintaining optimal network performance. Its lightweight design is perfect for resource-limited environments, offering a scalable, efficient solution for securing next-generation wireless networks.

title: Construction of Multi-Voltage-Level Datasets and Time-Series Power Flow Simulation for Suburban Distribution Networks
authors: Zhenyu Chen, Jianguang Du, Hongwei Lv, Shixuan Li, Haoran Qiang, Kun Jiang, Hao Wu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Energy Evolution and Power Engineering: Transition, Intelligence, and Autonomy (EEPE-TIA)
abstract: With the rapid penetration of distributed photovoltaics (PV) and electric vehicles (EVs), suburban distribution networks are facing new operational challenges, including bidirectional power flow, voltage fluctuations, and reverse power injection. To address the lack of standardized and representative datasets, two typical suburban distribution datasets are developed: the Suburban Residential Dataset and the Suburban Integrated Dataset. The former features a multi-radial, fewloop topology dominated by residential loads, while the latter incorporates residential, commercial, industrial, and EV loads with multi-voltage-level structures and high PV penetration. Using a force-directed layout and load-clustering assignment approach, the dataset construction integrates topology restoration, load mapping, and time-series modeling. Time-series Power Flow(TPF) simulations demonstrate that the datasets accurately reproduce voltage variations and peak-valley characteristics of suburban networks. The proposed datasets enable reproducible studies of distribution network reconfiguration, control optimization, and resilience evaluation with high computational efficiency and precision.

title: Fewer Surfaces, Stronger Links: EMI-Aware IRS Number, Placement, and Phase Design under Rate Guarantees
authors: Rhana Elsayed, Esraa M. Eid, Adly S. Tag Eldien, Mostafa M. Fouda, Mohamed I. Ismail, Shimaa S. Ali
abstract: Intelligent Reflecting Surfaces (IRSs) have emerged as a promising solution for enhancing wireless communication performance by reconfiguring the propagation environment in a passive and energy-efficient manner. However, determining the optimal number of IRSs, their spatial deployment, and the number of reflecting elements under practical constraints remains a challenging problem, particularly in the presence of electromagnetic interference (EMI). In this paper, we propose an EMI-aware optimization framework for multi-IRS-assisted wireless communication systems that jointly optimizes the number of IRSs, their placement, and their phase shift configurations while guaranteeing a minimum achievable data rate. To address the resulting high-dimensional and non-convex optimization problem, a novel Growth Optimizer (GO) algorithm is developed. The proposed approach explicitly accounts for practical deployment constraints, including spatial overlap, rate requirements, and interference effects. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that theGOalgorithm consistently outperforms benchmark optimization methods such as Differential Evolution (DE), Gradient-Based Optimization (GBO), and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO). Specifically, GO achieves the required rate guarantees using fewer IRS units and a reduced number of reflecting elements, thereby significantly lowering system complexity while maintaining robust performance under EMI. These results highlight the effectiveness of the proposed framework for efficient and scalable IRS deployment in next-generation wireless networks.

title: Performance Comparison of Beamsteering Approaches for Coverage Enhancement in Indoor Optical Wireless Communications
authors: Haitham AL Satai, Bastien Béchadergue, Luc Chassagne
abstract: Optical wireless communication (OWC) represents a promising option for overcoming spectrum limitations in radio frequency systems. OWC systems rely on cells generated by fixed access points, making them vulnerable to service degradation due to interference and frequent handovers. Steerable optical antennas, deployed both at the infrastructure and user levels, can mitigate these issues. Although their effectiveness has been experimentally verified, a thorough comparative study—particularly in terms of coverage—against traditional fixed antennas is still needed. In this article, simulations are first used to compare five scenarios corresponding to different beamsteering strategies. Each scenario is described, and the bit error rate and communication coverage are evaluated via Monte Carlo simulations, accounting for photobiological safety–compliant light source parameters and the effects of line-of-sight and non-line-of-sight optical paths. In a second step, an experimental test bench is proposed and then used to evaluate data rate and coverage with perfect or partial beamsteering and without beamsteering. The experimental results are consistent with the simulations and show that beamsteering significantly improves coverage compared to other pointing approaches, in particular by improving the quality of the received signal, thereby enabling better use of the entire available spectrum.

title: Orthogonal Ternary Element-Assemblage Codes for FFMA over Gaussian Multiple-Access Channels
authors: Qi-Yue Yu, Long-Qu Zhou, Ting-Wei Yang, Hao-Jun Sun
abstract: The finite-field multiple-access (FFMA) technique has been proposed to address the multiuser finite blocklength (FBL) problem by reordering the channel coding and multiplexing operations, thereby enhancing reliability in multiple-access systems. By leveraging virtual resources constructed over finite fields for user separation, FFMA inherently supports p-ary source transmission. This paper investigates ternary source transmission within the FFMA framework, which offers higher spectral efficiency than binary systems due to its expanded symbol alphabet. To enable efficient ternary multiuser transmission, we propose orthogonal ternary element-assemblage (EA) codes. Based on these codes, we develop a ternary polarization-adjusted finite-field time-division multiple-access system (Ternary-FF-TDMA) that achieves enhanced spectral efficiency through optimized resource allocation. For decoding, we introduce a bidirectional symbol-level merging ternary belief propagation (BSM-TBP) algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed Ternary-TD-FFMA system achieves higher spectral efficiency and better error performance compared to conventional non-orthogonal multiple-access (NOMA) systems.

title: Learning-Augmented Performance Model for Tensor Product Factorization in High-Order FEM
authors: Xuanzhengbo Ren, Yuta Kawai, Tetsuya Hoshino, Hirofumi Tomita, Takahiro Katagiri, Daichi Mukunoki, Seiya Nishizawa
abstract: Accurate performance prediction is essential for optimizing scientific applications on modern high-performance computing (HPC) architectures. Widely used performance models primarily focus on cache and memory bandwidth, which is suitable for many memory-bound workloads. However, it is unsuitable for highly arithmetic intensive cases such as the sum-factorization with tensor n-mode product kernels, which are an optimization technique for high-order finite element methods (FEM). On processors with relatively high single instruction multiple data (SIMD) instruction latency, such as the Fujitsu A64FX, the performance of these kernels is strongly influenced by loop-body splitting strategies. Memory-bandwidth-oriented models are therefore not appropriate for evaluating these splitting configurations, and a model that directly reflects instruction-level efficiency is required. To address this need, we develop a dependency-chain–based analytical formulation that links loop-splitting configurations to instruction dependencies in the tensor n-mode product kernel. We further use XGBoost to estimate key parameters in the analytical model that are difficult to model explicitly. Evaluations showthat the learning-augmented model outperforms the widely used standard Roofline and Execution-Cache-Memory (ECM) models. On the Fujitsu A64FX processor, the learning-augmented model achieves mean absolute percentage errors (MAPE) between 1% and 24% for polynomial orders (P) from 1 to 15. In comparison, the standard Roofline and ECM models yield errors of 42%–256% and 5%–117%, respectively. On the Intel Xeon Gold 6230 processor, the learning-augmented model achieves MAPE values from 1% to 13% for P = 1 to P = 14, and 24% at P = 15. In contrast, the standard Roofline and ECM models produce errors of 1%–73% and 8%–112% for P = 1 to P = 15, respectively.

title: FedU-KAN: Cloud-Enhanced Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning for Medical Image Segmentation Based on U-KAN
authors: Yingjie Liu, Haotian Chi, Yonggang Li, Yuwei Wang, Shunrong Jiang, Xiaojiang Du, Nadjib AitSaadi
abstract: Machine learning is gradually transforming medical image segmentation. However, its accuracy often relies on large-scale medical datasets, while centralized data collection raises serious privacy concerns. To address this issue, federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw data, thus effectively protecting patient privacy. Despite this advantage, commonly used segmentation models, such as U-Net and its variants, typically have large parameter sizes, making them inefficient for local training on FL clients. To overcome this challenge, we propose FedU-KAN, a framework built upon the lightweight U-KAN architecture, tailored for federated medical image segmentation tasks. Moreover, we design an adaptive differential privacy mechanism that dynamically adjusts gradient clipping based on feature importance. This approach helps preserve anatomical details while reducing the risk of privacy leakage. We evaluate FedU-KAN on the CVC-ClinicDB and Kvasir-SEG datasets, where it achieves IoU scores of 87.09% and 83.38%, respectively—outperforming standard FL baselines. These results demonstrate that FedU-KAN can effectively balance privacy protection and model performance in real-world medical segmentation scenarios.

title: Deep Unfolding Learning-Based Beamforming Design for Multi-User MIMO-OFDM Integrated Sensing and Communication Systems
authors: Tzu-Chien Chiu, Ming-Chun Lee, Ta-Sung Lee
abstract: To realize integrated sensing and communication (ISAC), multiple-input multiple-output orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (MIMO-OFDM) ISAC systems have been widely discussed. However, current beamforming designs for multi-user MIMO-OFDM ISAC systems are mostly based on iterative optimization-based approaches, which commonly induce high runtime complexity. To resolve this issue, this paper first investigates the relevant beamforming design approach that is based on iterative weighted minimum mean square error (WMMSE) problem and successive convex approximation (SCA). Then, a deep unfolding learning-based beamforming design framework is proposed via unfolding the iterative WMMSESCA procedure into a neural network (NN)-based architecture. To efficiently train the proposed framework, a loss function for unsupervised learning is proposed. Also, approaches that can enhance the training efficiency are discussed. Simulation results demonstrate that our proposed approach can outperform existing methods in ISAC performance, while significantly reducing the computation time.

title: Towards Secure Coherent-State QKD with Practical Quantum Detection
authors: Shuai Han, Mufei Zhao, Renzhi Yuan, Ziyi Xie, Shaojie Yang
abstract: Security analysis of continuous variable quantum key distribution (CV-QKD) remains a topic of extensive academic investigation. In this work, we propose a coherent-state CV-QKD protocol integrating a displacement-based quantum receiver and post-selection strategies. We establish a framework of the protocol under both individual and collective attacks, incorporating practical constraints such as noises and device imperfections. To enhance performance of the protocol, the postselection strategy and the transmitted signal power are optimized for different channel attenuation levels. Numerical results offer insights into the strategy of employing displacement receivers in CV-QKD protocols, revealing that larger transmitted signal power is required under low channel attenuation or severe receiver-side imperfections and noises to achieve higher the secret key rate.

title: In-Memory Noise Estimation using LDPC Codes for Reliable Edge Matrix-Vector Multiplication
authors: Yotam Gershon, Yuval Cassuto
abstract: Reliable matrix-vector multiplication (MVM) in edge devices is a key enabler for modern computational tasks such as AI inference. Novel memory technologies, such as MRAMs, enable highly efficient analog computation of MVM. While significantly improving speed and power consumption, these architectures suffer from reduced reliability. We introduce a coding scheme in which the parity constraints of the code are employed toward in-memory noise estimation, in addition to their traditional role of error correction. Our scheme relies on objects we call analog syndromes, which on one hand can be efficiently computed in memory, and on the other hand are shown analytically to provide more accurate estimation than classical logical syndromes. The scheme utilizes a previously introduced bilayer LDPC design with sub-block locality, and describes how to design the degree distributions.

title: Statistical Feature-Based Misbehavior Detection Against Positional Attacks in Internet of Vehicles
authors: Chia-Hao Fan, Tsung-Wei Chiang, Shi-Sheng Sun, Yi-Han Chiang
abstract: The Internet of Vehicles (IoV) enables vehicles to communicate with each other and infrastructure through basic safety messages (BSMs), thereby reducing accidents by means of early warnings and collision avoidance. In fact, IoV networks are vulnerable to positional attacks induced by malicious vehicles, which can severely compromise traffic safety and system reliability. In spite of the existing works devoted to misbehavior detection for certain types of attacks, extracting statistical features from BSMs to defend against all positional attacks in IoV networks has been largely overlooked. In this paper, we extract six types of positional attacks from the VeReMi-Extension dataset and discover hidden attack patterns. Based on these discovered patterns and our data analysis, we categorize them into the statistical features of Gaussian-like, Lévy-like sparse, and Lévy-like ordinary positional attacks. Then, we propose a three-feature misbehavior detection mechanism to distinguish between normal and malicious vehicles, where the first feature detection employs the Jarque-Bera test to identify Gaussian-like positional attacks, the second feature detection identifies Lévy-like sparse positional attacks by evaluating data sparsity, and the third feature detection uses Isolation Forest to detect Lévy-like ordinary positional attacks. Simulation results show that the proposed solution achieves promising false alarm and miss detection rates, and acts robustly across diverse parameter settings.

title: A Comparative Study of CNN Architectures for Image-Based Malware Classification
authors: Aakersh Chauhan, Bhavana Verma, Dinesh Kuamr Vishwakarma
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: As malware attacks grow more sophisticated, deep learning approaches have gained attention for automated classification. This paper presents a comparative evaluation of three CNN architectures for image-based malware classification: a custom 3-layer CNN, ResNet-50, and ResNeXt-50, converting binary malware files into grayscale images. Key contributions include: (1) demonstrating competitive performance without complex augmentation, and (2) identifying misclassification patterns between similar variants. ResNeXt- 50 achieves 99.29 % accuracy on the Malimg dataset, showing that standard architectures without any advance techniques can match the performance of complex hybrid approaches, highlighting CNN effectiveness for practical malware detection.

title: Two Wheels, One Mind: A Study on Motorcycling as a Mental Health Intervention for IT Professionals
authors: Arun R P, Harihara Shanmugam A R
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Great intellectual demands, sedentary employment, and long hours are helping to cause more mental health problems among information technology professionals. Among these difficulties are especially depression, burnout, and anxiety. Alternative treatment techniques such as mindfulness meditation, physical exercise, and time spent in nature have demonstrated some degree of promise. Riding motorcycles, which incorporates aspects of many other disciplines, could be a creative intervention meant for mental renewal. On the other hand, even although many experts like leisure riding, its potential therapeutic advantages, particularly in relation to stress reduction and mental clarity, are still under-researched in the information technology field. Few studies connect riding a motorcycle to clear gains in cognitive function and psychological well-being. This mixed-methods study gave sixty information technology experts a pre- and post-test. Participants were split into two groups: a control group that did not ride at all and an experimental group that cycled for thirty minutes every day for two weeks. Psychological tests, PSS and GHQ-12, and cognitive tests, Stroop and Reaction Time, were run before the assessment. Collecting biometric data also covered cortisol level and heart rate variability measures. The experimental group showed a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{2 8 \%}$</tex> median drop in felt stress ratings and a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{2 1 \%}$</tex> rise in attention-based cognitive tasks. Both of these changes were noted. By indicating a 15 % increase in heart rate variability (HRV) and a 12 % decrease in cortisol levels, biometric data backed these findings. Rides without comparison revealed significantly significant differences (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{p}<0.05$</tex>).

title: Force Control of a Two-Inertia System Using Torsion Torque Observer-Based Quantization Error Compensation with a Low-Resolution Torque Sensor
authors: Isshin Kimura, Juan Padron, Toshimasa Miyazaki, Kiyoshi Ohishi, Yuki Yokokura, Koichi Inomata, Yoshihito Takeshima
publish: 2026 IEEE 19th International Conference on Advanced Motion Control (AMC)
abstract: In recent years, industrial robots widely used in factories are required to achieve wide-bandwidth and high- precision force control. In force control, feedback is performed using torque data obtained from a torque sensor; however, since the torque data contains quantization errors, discrepancies from the actual torque may occur, resulting in a degradation of control performance. In this study, quantization error compensation was performed using a torsion torque observer (TTOB) to improve force control performance. As a result, it was confirmed that compensating the estimated quantization error enhanced the control performance compared with the uncompensated case.

title: Prototype-Guided Non-Exemplar Continual Learning for Cross-subject EEG Decoding
authors: Dan Li, Hye-Bin Shin, Yeon-Woo Choi
abstract: Due to the significant variability in electroencephalogram (EEG) signals across individuals, knowledge acquired from previous subjects is often overwritten as new subjects are introduced in continual EEG decoding tasks. Existing methods mainly rely on storing historical data from seen subjects as replay buffers to mitigate forgetting, which is impractical under privacy or memory constraints. To address this issue, we propose a Prototype-guided Non-Exemplar Continual Learning (ProNECL) framework that preserves prior knowledge without accessing historical EEG samples. ProNECL summarizes subject-specific discriminative representations into class-level prototypes and incrementally aligns new subject representations with a global prototype memory through prototype-based feature regularization and cross-subject alignment. Experiments on the BCI Competition IV 2a and 2b datasets demonstrate that ProNECL effectively balances knowledge retention and adaptability, achieving superior performance in cross-subject continual EEG decoding tasks.

title: Optimizing Path Tracking Performance in NAO Robots via Multiobjective Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ibtissem Chiha, Taoufik Ladhari
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advances in Data-Driven Analytics And Intelligent Systems (ADACIS)
abstract: Path tracking is a fundamental task for autonomous robots in navigating in dynamic environments. This paper presents a novel approach to optimize the path tracking performance of NAO robots by leveraging multiobjective reinforcement learning (MORL). Unlike traditional single-objective controllers, our method simultaneously optimizes multiple conflicting objectives such as trajectory accuracy, energy efficiency, and smoothness of motion. We design a customized MORL framework that is tailored to the kinematics and sensor capabilities of the NAO robot. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach significantly improves tracking precision while balancing other performance metrics. The results confirm that MORL provides a powerful tool for enhancing autonomous navigation in humanoid robots, paving the way for more adaptive and efficient robotic behaviors in complex tasks.

title: Mechanical Power Modeling and Energy Efficiency Maximization for Movable Antenna Systems
authors: Xin Wei, Weidong Mei, Xuan Huang, Zhi Chen, Boyu Ning
abstract: Movable antennas (MAs) have recently garnered significant attention in wireless communications due to their capability to reshape wireless channels via local antenna movement within a confined region. However, to achieve accurate antenna movement, MA drivers introduce non-negligible mechanical power consumption, rendering energy efficiency (EE) optimization more critical compared to conventional fixed-position antenna (FPA) systems. To address this problem, we develop in this paper a fundamental power consumption model for stepper motor-driven MA systems by resorting to basic electric motor theory. Based on this model, we formulate an EE maximization problem by jointly optimizing an MA’s position, moving speed, and transmit power. However, this problem is difficult to solve optimally due to the intricate relationship between the mechanical power consumption and the design variables. To tackle this issue, we first uncover a hidden monotonicity of the EE performance with respect to the MA’s moving speed. Then, we apply the Dinkelbach algorithm to obtain the optimal transmit power in a semi-closed form for any given MA position, followed by an enumeration to determine the optimal MA position. Numerical results demonstrate that despite the additional mechanical power consumption, the MA system can outperform the conventional FPA system in terms of EE.

title: Intelligent Messaging Channel Selector (IMCS) for Context-Aware AI Agent Communication
authors: Rahul Goel
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) agents must interact with users across a fragmented landscape of messaging platforms, including SMS, WhatsApp, and RCS. Current agent communication strategies often rely on static preferences or simplistic fallback logic, failing to adapt to the dynamic context of interactions and the heterogeneous capabilities, costs, and reliability of available channels. This deficiency leads to suboptimal communication, increased operational costs, and poor user experience. This paper proposes the Intelligent Messaging Channel Selector (IMCS), a novel framework enabling AI agents to perform dynamic, context-aware selection of the optimal communication channel in real-time. IMCS integrates a multi-dimensional context vector-comprising message urgency, user preferences, and real-time platform performance-into a multi-criteria decision engine. Through a simulated evaluation processing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0, 0 0 0}$</tex> user interactions, IMCS demonstrated a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4. 5 \%}$</tex> improvement in message delivery success rate and a 22 % reduction in cost per interaction compared to a standard static fallback strategy. This paper details the IMCS architecture, its decision-making logic, and the quantitative results that validate its superiority over conventional static approaches in complex, multi-channel environments.

title: Source Coding for Control-Oriented Communications With Lebesgue Sampling
authors: Shuqi Wei, Wei Chen
abstract: Communication-control co-design has attracted considerable recent attention as it represents a fundamental paradigm shift towards next-generation industrial Internet of Things (IoT) and other cyber-physical systems. However, its performance limits, especially the information-theoretic bounds of control-oriented communications with event-triggered feedback widely adopted in emerging smart grids, digital twins, industrial robots, etc. mostly remain open due to the incomplete integration of communication and control theory. In this paper, we investigate signal compression for multi-dimensional networked control systems over additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) and wireless channels. In particular, Jordan canonical decomposition is leveraged to partition the original linear dynamic system characterized by a stochastic differential equation (SDE) into unified, standard and yet dimension-reduced subsystems, each with a Jordan block as the state matrix. To obtain the frequency and distribution of Lebesgue sampling, we analyze two partial differential equations (PDEs), the boundary conditions of which are determined by triggering manifolds, i.e., closed surfaces, curves, and thresholds in the decomposed space. Based on the PDEs as well as high-resolution quantization or rate-distortion theory, we design source coding for event-triggered control (ETC) by optimizing the triggering manifolds. Simulation results validate our theoretical analysis and demonstrate the substantial gain of our data compression for ETC.

title: Multi-State Bandit Reinforcement Learning for Decentralized Spectrum Access
authors: Ashvin Srinivasan, Junshan Zhang, Olav Tirkkonen
abstract: We consider spectrum sharing in a system with multiple network operators serving many users in the same coverage area. Each operator selects a frequency band from a pool. Due to interference, the aggregate throughput of an operator would likely deteriorate as the number of operators using the band increases. The objective is to devise a decentralized method for the operators to select the bands, assuming that the networks and user populations of the operators are similar. For this, we consider a decentralized multi-armed bandit algorithm operating in two phases, namely Estimation and Allocation. In the estimation phase, operators learn load-dependent reward distributions; and in the subsequent allocation phase, these estimates are used to determine an allocation of bands across operators. We extend the framework to multi-state systems, where the reward distribution depends on additional observable state variables capturing load variations and environmental factors. Analyzing the system with a variance-based approach using Bernstein’s Inequality, we show that the decentralized algorithm achieves a regret which is sublinear in the time horizon as compared to an optimal centralized decision. In multi-state systems, we demonstrate a trade-off between increasing estimation overhead and decreasing regret during allocation phases.

title: User Authentication on Smart Devices using Touchless Micro Gestures and Radio Fingerprints
authors: Syed Mujtaba Sherazi, Ashfaq Khokhar, Sang Wu Kim
abstract: We introduce a near-field WiFi-based micro-gesture recognition and user authentication system for secure, touchless interaction with handheld devices. Unlike macro-gesture solutions requiring multiple access points (APs), our system targets sub-5cm gestures performed 2–10cm above a smartphone-scale prototype, capturing Channel State Information (CSI) at 80–100Hz via a single off-the-shelf transceiver–antenna pair under heavy RF interference. We develop amplitude–phase features and novel CSI descriptors (e.g., radio cross section, amplitude-phase cross-correlation, spectral energy ratios) that unify micro-gesture recognition and user identification in a single classification task without explicit segmentation cues. On a new dataset of eight participants (five micro-gestures each), the system attains up to 94% gesture accuracy, up to 87% user authentication accuracy, and 80–90% joint user+gesture accuracy, surpassing literature-style feature baselines and raw IQ approaches. The compact hardware, robust feature engineering, and strong performance indicate practical viability of near-field micro-gesture–driven authentication on smart devices.

title: Constant Power Load Control Strategy for Buck Circuits Based on Composite Cascade Observers and Adaptive Terminal Sliding Mode
authors: Zhuo Wang, Yaofang Zhang, Cui Wang, Yupeng Xiang, Chen Sun
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Energy, Power and Electrical Engineering (EPEE)
abstract: With the proliferation of new energy microgrids and electric vehicle powertrains, Buck converters supplying constant power loads (CPL) are increasingly susceptible to instability issues. Conventional PI control methods exhibit limitations in handling the inherent nonlinearities and large-signal disturbances introduced by CPLs, owing to their slow dynamic response and limited robustness. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel control framework that integrates a Composite Cascaded Extended State Observer (CESO) with an Adaptive Terminal Sliding Mode Controller (ATSMC). The CESO is designed to accurately estimate and compensate for the system's nonlinear dynamics, including external disturbances and model uncertainties, by employing a cascaded observer structure that enhances observation precision. Meanwhile, the ATSMC incorporates an adaptive law to adjust the switching gain dynamically, thereby significantly reducing chattering effects and ensuring finite-time convergence of the output voltage. The combined approach not only improves the large-signal stability of the Buck converter under CPL conditions but also enhances transient performance with faster recovery and stronger robustness against load variations. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed strategy in achieving superior voltage regulation and dynamic response.

title: Performance of RIS-Aided Satellite Communications under Correlated Line-of-Sight Conditions
authors: Ahmed Al-Amri, Akram Al-Hourani, Saman Atapattu
abstract: This paper presents an analytical framework for modeling line-of-sight (LoS) availability in Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface (RIS)-assisted satellite communication systems under urban blockages. A correlated LoS probability model is proposed using a bivariate Bernoulli framework that captures spatial dependencies between satellite-to-user and satellite-to-RIS links. We derive closed-form expressions for the system-level success probability, incorporating elevation angle, blockage statistics, and RIS deployment geometry. A key result is a closed-form expression for the optimal RIS-user separation distance that maximizes system reliability. Monte Carlo simulations across diverse urban environments validate the accuracy of the proposed model. Numerical results reveal that denser urban settings require shorter RIS-user distances for optimal performance. The framework enables tractable performance analysis and guides efficient RIS deployment in urban SatCom scenarios.

title: Joint Trajectory and Antenna Port Selection Optimization for Fluid Antenna System-enabled Resilient UAV Networks
authors: Xiaoren Xu, Dongyu Wei, Ye Hu, Zhaohui Yang, Mingzhe Chen
abstract: In this paper, a novel resilient unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) framework that enables UAVs to efficiently adjust their trajectories and antenna ports to serve disconnected users due to unexpected accidents is designed. In the proposed framework, a set of UAVs equipped with fluid antennas provide service for ground users. At the beginning, each UAV optimizes its three dimensional (3D) location and selects an antenna port to maximize the sum data rate of all users. During the service period, several UAVs may not be able to continue to serve ground users due to unexpected accidents. The remaining UAVs must adjust their trajectories and antenna ports to provide communication services for the users originally served by UAVs with accidents. This problem is formulated as an optimization problem that aims to maximize the total data rates of all users during the entire service period including the period that all UAVs can provide service, the period that some UAVs cannot provide service and the remaining UAVs must adjust their trajectories and antenna ports, and the period that the remaining UAVs find fixed locations to serve users. To solve this problem, an attention and gate recurrent unit (GRU) based reinforcement learning (AGRL) method is designed. In this method, the GRUs are utilized to capture previous UAV actions including trajectories and antenna port selections and states. The transformer is used to analyze the importance of previous UAV actions and states, thus further improving the total data rates of all users. To further improve the training speed of the designed AGRL method, we mathematically derive the optimally initial UAV locations. Simulation results show that the proposed AGRL method can improve the expected data rate of all users by up to 9.89% and 10.19% compared to the value function decomposition RL (VDRL) method and the proposed AGRL method without optimizing antenna port selection.

title: Access Point Switch On/Off for Energy Efficient Scalable Cell-Free MIMO Networks
authors: Ala Eddine Nouali, Mattia Merluzzi, Jean-Baptiste Dore, Jean-Paul Jamont
abstract: Cell-free network has emerged as a promising architecture to ensure high-capacity and uniform quality of service (QoS) to users through cooperation among numerous distributed access points (APs). In such networks, user-centric clustering (UCC) has emerged to guarantee network scalability, where each UE is served by a preferred set of APs. Forming the optimal cluster of APs for each mobile UE is a challenging task, particularly when the cluster must be dynamically adjusted to satisfy all the QoS requirements under channels conditions and limited processing capabilities of both the user equipment (UE) and the AP. Moreover, the inherent densification required by cell-free networks can significantly increase the overall energy consumption, posing substantial challenges for sustainable network operation. To address these energy-related challenges, strategic utilization of AP sleep-mode activation (SMA), also known as AP switch on/off (ASO) schemes, has been explored. In this paper, we design a deep reinforcement learning framework for the long-term basis ASO strategy, combined with a UCC approach based on a many-to-many matching game scheme in intermediate steps. Numerical results show that our proposed solution is able to optimize network energy efficiency (EE) without compromising perceived QoS under dynamic and varied network traffic conditions.

title: Power Electronics-Driven Intelligent Adaptive Control System for Electrical Discharge Machining of Molybdenum-Titanium-Zirconium Alloy
authors: Xin Mu, Shuai Liu, Meiqin Liang, Zhengzhong Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Mechanical Engineering, Intelligent Manufacturing, and Automation Technology (MEMAT)
abstract: In order to fully leverage the capabilities of adaptive electrical discharge machining (EDM) and address the conflict between machining stability and machining efficiency, a power electronics-driven intelligent adaptive EDM system is proposed. Based on “perceived” discharge pulse signals in the gap between the electrode and the workpiece, the system integrates power electronic parameter detection and computer-aided signal processing to compute machining environment and state indices through a “cognitive process”. A power electronics-optimized control framework is designed to define desired machining state expectations, balancing efficiency and stability via real-time adjustment of EDM power supply parameters and electrode servo dynamics. Two cascaded adaptive control loops are adopted: one for generating control behavior guidelines and the other for optimizing power electronic drive and servo control behavior. Experimental results indicate that the system significantly enhances machining capabilities, enabling stable and rapid machining of nuclear industry-grade molybdenum-titaniumzirconium alloys. Traditional electrical discharge machining cannot process this type of alloy. The integration of power electronic drive optimization and digital control technology promotes the development of EDM towards high-efficiency, low-energy-consumption electrical manufacturing, demonstrating profound implications for advancing electronic power and intelligent manufacturing technologies.

title: Beamforming Design for CAPA-Based Multicast Communications
authors: Mengyu Qian, Xidong Mu, Li You, Michail Matthaiou
abstract: A continuous aperture array (CAPA)-based multicast communication system is investigated in this paper, where a base station (BS) employs a CAPA to serve a set of multicast users. Under a transmit power constraint, the problem of maximizing the system multicast spectral efficiency (SE) by designing the CAPA beamformer is formulated, where the involved beamformer is a continuous current density function across the CAPA surface. By introducing auxiliary variables, the non-convex multicast SE objective function is first transformed into a tractable form. Then, to address the reformulated problem, an efficient block coordinate descent (BCD)-based algorithm is developed. The CAPA beamforming design subproblem can be optimally solved via the Lagrangian dual method and the calculus of variations (CoV) theory. Numerical results demonstrate that the considered CAPA can significantly improve the multicast SE compared to a conventional spatially discrete array (SPDA).

title: Dynamic impact of new energy subsidy policies on regional green transformation considering spatial spillover effects and spatiotemporal graph neural networks
authors: Jinyi Lian
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 International Conference on Information Economy, Data Modeling and Cloud Computing
abstract: None

authors: Junni Wu

title: Leveraging AI to Detect and Predict Data Issues in Supply Chain Logistics
authors: Junaith Meeran Haja Mohideen
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advances in Data-Driven Analytics And Intelligent Systems (ADACIS)
abstract: In modern logistics and transportation systems, the continuous collection and analysis of vehicle telemetry data particularly latitude and longitude play a vital role in enabling route optimization, operational efficiency, and safety. However, real-world telemetry streams frequently contain missing or erroneous records due to network disruptions, hardware failures, or sensor malfunctions. Such data quality issues can undermine the accuracy of downstream analytics and affect operational decision-making. Traditional correction techniques, which rely on manual checks or simple deterministic rules, are inadequate for handling high-velocity, large-scale data environments.This paper introduces a new system that makes GPS data from vehicles more accurate and reliable. It works by collecting information from vehicles in real time using Amazon Kinesis. The data is then stored in Amazon S3 using Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose. After collecting the data, AWS Glue DataBrew checks it carefully to find any missing or incorrect location details. To fix these problems, the system uses a Bayesian model inside Amazon SageMaker. This model uses math and past patterns to guess the missing values in a smart way. In tests, this method was more than 96% accurate, which is much better than simple methods like taking an average.Moreover, by adopting the Data as a Product (DaaP) paradigm, the system ensures that enriched telemetry data is treated as a governed, discoverable, and high-quality product, ready for consumption by downstream analytics, machine learning applications, and business decision-makers. This DaaP approach promotes data reliability, discoverability, and reusability, ensuring that enriched data streams deliver continuous value across the organization. It offers a scalable, AWS-native framework capable of maintaining data integrity in real-time logistics environments. Furthermore, this approach holds promise for broader applications in supply chain management, smart city infrastructure, and dynamic resource allocation, with future work exploring real-time Bayesian inference, adaptive learning, and edge computing integration.

title: Predictive Trajectory-Based Thermal Comfort Control in Smart Buildings
authors: Mohamed Khayri Rahmani, Hajer Chtioui, Jalel Ben Hadj Slama, Mireille Gettler Summa, Anouar Ben Khalifa
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Advances in Data-Driven Analytics And Intelligent Systems (ADACIS)
abstract: This paper proposes a thermal comfort prediction and control framework based on optimal trajectory generation to regulate HVAC systems in shared office environments. The methodology integrates real-time environmental data—comprising internal temperature, humidity, air velocity, mean radiant temperature, and external weather conditions—with subjective comfort votes collected through a digital platform. A predictive model estimates the thermal comfort level using machine learning techniques, with XGBoost achieving a prediction accuracy of 62.7% and an F1-score of 64.5%. A trajectory generation mechanism computes a sequence of intermediate comfort states that guide the HVAC system progressively toward the desired comfort point. Unlike traditional direct control strategies that apply setpoint conditions immediately, the proposed approach defines a smooth, energy-efficient transition path, resulting in a 27% reduction in energy consumption while improving thermal stability and comfort attainment speed. Simulation results demonstrate that controlling HVAC inputs along the optimal trajectory leads to better thermal stability, faster comfort attainment, and lower control effort compared to direct setpoint tracking. By enhancing the prediction accuracy of thermal comfort and minimizing deviations from optimal environmental conditions, the trajectory-based control significantly outperforms conventional strategies, making it a more reliable and sustainable solution for intelligent indoor climate regulation.

title: An Investigation of Optimized Supply Chain Strategies in the Organized Retail Sector: A Case Study of the Furniture Industry
authors: Fasahat Nazir Buchoo, S Subramanian, R Sathish, S Subramanian, Pavithra G, Poorani D
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: A major driver of operational efficiency in the organized retail industry is supply chain management (SCM). This is especially true in the furniture industry, which has specific difficulties including high transportation costs, enormous inventory, and fluctuating demand. The SCM has a major impact on operational efficiency. Efficient SCM policies determine how much consumers' degree of satisfaction is raised and how much expenses are cut. Common in the furniture sector, which sometimes runs under challenging circumstances, are problems including inventory control inefficiencies, supply delays, and inaccurate demand forecasts. Suitable methods of supply chain management could solve these problems and improve the performance of the Thus. The proposed method comprises all three elements: integration of advanced forecasting models, inventory optimization techniques, and transportation management systems. Performance was evaluated via simulation in the context of the furniture retail industry using real-time data in a case study.

title: Resilient Urban Infrastructure Through AI-Powered 6G Connectivity
authors: Arpan Ray P. Sathyaraj, Hara Krishna Reddy Koppolu, Rahul Gupta, Shabrinath Motamary, C B Selva Lakshmi, Karthick R
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Advances in Computation, Communication and Information Technology (ICAICCIT)
abstract: Quick urbanization and techno cities require smart and future-proof infrastructures. In this paper, we propose an implementation-oriented framework to achieve resilient urban infrastructure using <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{A I}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 G}$</tex> wireless networks. AI-native <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 G}$</tex> connections ensure urban systems respond to sudden changes, allocate resources as efficiently as possible, and make decisions in real-time. It is our vision to demonstrate a system implementation utilizing RIS, edge AI and NTN to deploy ultra- reliable, low-latency and self-healing networks. The end-to-end latency of the proposed architecture is < 1 ms, the recovery time by RIS/UAV rerouting is 0.5 s, and it achieves 92.4% on prediction accuracy for the traffic congestion and 99.97% in the stress-test case. We describe the development, deployment, and performance measurement in both real-time simulation and prototype deployment. Finally, we discuss in Section 7 resilience metrics and standardization for sustainable and smart urban development.

title: Optimal Placement of Hollow-Core Fiber Spans for Latency Reduction in Optical Transport Networks
authors: João Pedro, Bruno Correia, Diogo Morão
abstract: Recent progress in hollow-core fiber (HCF) technology is raising the prospects for the deployment of this type of fiber in future infrastructure upgrades. Although earlier deployments of HCF will take advantage of its low latency property to realize point-to-point links (e.g., in the metro and aggregation network segments), HCF generations with better specifications (improving performance and deployment robustness) may become suitable for more demanding regional and long-haul network applications. In these scenarios, they may co-exist with traditional fiber types in order to ensure, at the network-level, an appropriate balance between latency reduction and the increase of capital expenses associated to both the higher cost of HCF spans and the potential reductions in reach. Therefore, fiber infrastructure owners will face a new design problem, which consists of optimizing the spans where HCF is to be used to minimize service latency while meeting optical performance degradation and cost increase bounds. This paper describes an optimal method to solve this problem, by leveraging the approximation of incoherent accumulation of noise to derive an integer linear programming (ILP) model, and two variants of a heuristic algorithm. A set of detailed simulations over a reference optical transport network highlight the optimality and scalability of the proposed method.

title: Variational Graph Autoencoder-Driven Initialization for Genetic Algorithms in D2D Resource Allocation
authors: Maher Marwani, Georges Kaddoum
abstract: Radio Resource Management (RRM) in wireless networks faces several challenges related to balancing complex optimization, stringent QoS, and tight convergence time. Traditional heuristics are computationally expensive, while learning methods frequently struggle with constraint enforcement. In this paper, we propose a two-stage hybrid framework for D2D power and RB allocation. First, a Variational Graph Autoencoder (VGAE) models channel state information (CSI) as a graph to learn and generate diverse, high-quality feasible allocations. These initialize a Genetic Algorithm (GA) for refinement. The proposed hybrid approach combines the generative power of VGAE with the fine-tuning ability of GA, achieving faster convergence, improved constraint satisfaction, and near-optimal performance with reduced computational cost.
